dapp-developer.title: Web and Blockchain Fundamentals
dapp-developer.description: Understand how the web works and how blockchain differs from traditional client-server architecture. Learn about decentralization, consensus, and why smart contracts exist.

resources.label: But How Does Bitcoin Actually Work?

dapp-developer.challenge: Write a plain-text explanation of how a transaction goes from a user wallet to a confirmed block on Ethereum.

children.title: HTTP and the Request-Response Cycle
children.description: Every web interaction is a request sent to a server that returns a response. Understanding HTTP methods, status codes, and DNS resolution is the baseline before layering blockchain concepts on top — without it you will not know why dApps need an RPC endpoint as a substitute for a traditional server.

children.title: JavaScript Async and Promises
children.description: Blockchain calls are always asynchronous because they involve network requests and potentially long confirmation times. If you misuse async/await or mishandle Promise rejections, your dApp will silently fail or display stale data that confuses users.

children.title: What a Blockchain Actually Is
children.description: A blockchain is an append-only linked list of batched transactions secured by cryptographic hashes and distributed across many nodes. Misunderstanding this means you will not grasp why "immutability" is not absolute, why finality takes time, or why reading state costs no gas but writing state does.

resources.label: But How Does Bitcoin Actually Work? - 3Blue1Brown

children.title: Wallets, Addresses, and Keys
children.description: A wallet does not store coins — it stores private keys that prove ownership of on-chain balances. If you confuse a wallet address with a contract address, or conflate signing with encrypting, you will build insecure key-handling code that can expose users to fund loss.

resources.label: Public Key Cryptography Explained - Khan Academy

children.title: From Signed Tx to Confirmed Block
children.description: A transaction goes through signing, broadcasting to the mempool, miner/validator selection, block inclusion, and then a number of confirmation blocks before it is considered final. Missing this mental model causes developers to show incorrect "success" states before a transaction is actually irreversible.

children.title: Gas and the EVM Execution Model
children.description: Every opcode the EVM runs costs gas, paid in ETH by the sender. Misunderstanding gas limits versus gas price leads to transactions that revert out-of-gas, incorrect fee estimates, and dApps that leave users with failed transactions they cannot explain.

children.title: Nodes, Validators, and Consensus
children.description: Ethereum reaches agreement through a proof-of-stake consensus mechanism where validators vote on the canonical chain. Without this understanding, developers do not know why transactions can be reorganized, why finality is probabilistic at first, or why connecting to a single node is a centralization risk.

children.title: Smart Contracts as Trustless Backend
children.description: A smart contract is code deployed at an address on the blockchain that executes deterministically for every node, replacing the need for a trusted server. Failing to understand this trustless execution model leads to designs that re-introduce off-chain trust assumptions and defeat the purpose of building on a blockchain.

dapp-developer.title: Solidity Basics
dapp-developer.description: Learn the Solidity language syntax, data types, and how the EVM executes bytecode. Write and deploy your first smart contracts using Remix IDE.
dapp-developer.challenge: Deploy a simple storage contract on Remix that stores and retrieves a number, then read the emitted event log.

children.title: Value Types vs Reference Types
children.description: Solidity distinguishes value types (uint, bool, address) that are copied on assignment from reference types (arrays, structs, mappings) that point to storage or memory locations. Confusing the two causes subtle bugs where you modify a memory copy thinking you changed storage, leading to state that never actually updates on-chain.

children.title: State Variables and Functions
children.description: State variables persist between transactions in contract storage, while local variables live only in memory during a single call. Misusing visibility modifiers (public, private, internal, external) exposes sensitive state or prevents necessary cross-contract calls, both of which can be catastrophic in production.

children.title: ABI Encoding and Contract Interfaces
children.description: The ABI is the specification that tells callers how to encode function arguments and decode return values when communicating with a contract. Without understanding the ABI you cannot build a frontend that calls contracts, debug hex calldata, or write contracts that call each other correctly.

resources.label: ABI Encoding Deep Dive - OpenZeppelin Blog

children.title: Events and Logs
children.description: Events are the cheapest way to emit data from contracts; they are stored in transaction receipts as logs and are the primary data source for off-chain indexers and frontends. If you do not emit structured events, your dApp will have no efficient way to display historical activity or react to on-chain state changes.

children.title: Remix IDE for Rapid Prototyping
children.description: Remix is a browser-based Solidity IDE with a built-in compiler, JavaScript VM, and debugger that requires zero local setup. Skipping it in favor of a local toolchain immediately means slower iteration during the learning phase when understanding the compiler output and deploy workflow is most important.

children.title: Constructor and Deployment Parameters
children.description: The constructor runs once at deployment and is used to initialize state, set the owner, and accept deployment arguments that would otherwise require an extra transaction. Misunderstanding constructor behavior causes developers to deploy contracts with uninitialized state that cannot be corrected without a full redeployment.

children.title: require, revert, and Custom Errors
children.description: require and revert stop execution and refund unused gas when a condition is not met; custom errors added in Solidity 0.8 are more gas-efficient and carry structured data. Using plain revert strings instead of custom errors wastes user gas, while omitting input validation entirely opens contracts to misuse and fund loss.

resources.label: Custom Errors in Solidity - OpenZeppelin Blog

children.title: Overflow Protection and SafeMath
children.description: Prior to Solidity 0.8 integer arithmetic could silently overflow, causing balances to wrap around to zero or maximum values. Even with built-in checks in 0.8+, understanding when to use unchecked blocks for gas optimization and when overflow protection is critical prevents both wasted gas and subtle arithmetic bugs.

dapp-developer.title: Ethereum Developer Toolchain
dapp-developer.description: Set up a professional local Ethereum development environment using Hardhat. Learn to compile, test, and deploy contracts from the command line.

resources.label: Full Stack Ethereum Development - Nader Dabit

dapp-developer.challenge: Create a Hardhat project, write a deploy script for a simple counter contract, and log the deployed address to console.

children.title: Hardhat Configuration and Tasks
children.description: hardhat.config.js or .ts is the single control plane for your compiler version, networks, plugins, and custom tasks. Misconfiguring it causes the compiler to produce incompatible bytecode, deploy to the wrong network, or fail to verify on Etherscan — all issues that surface only at the last step of a workflow.

children.title: Local Hardhat Network and Forking
children.description: Hardhat Network is an in-process EVM that mines blocks instantly and lets you fork mainnet state so you can test against real protocol contracts and token balances. Without understanding forking, you cannot realistically test contract interactions with Uniswap, Aave, or any deployed protocol before going to a testnet.

children.title: Writing Hardhat Deploy Scripts
children.description: Deploy scripts are reusable JavaScript or TypeScript files that compile contracts, deploy them with arguments, and log addresses — forming a reproducible deployment record. Skipping structured scripts and deploying ad hoc from the console makes it impossible to reproduce deploys across testnets or audit what was deployed when.

children.title: Environment Variables with dotenv
children.description: Private keys and API keys must never be committed to source control; dotenv loads them from a local .env file into process.env at runtime. Storing secrets in hardhat.config.js directly causes accidental key leaks when sharing code, which is the number one cause of drained developer wallets.

children.title: Providers and Signers
children.description: A Provider is a read-only connection to the network, while a Signer wraps a private key and can authorize transactions. Conflating them is the most common ethers.js mistake — it produces errors like "missing signer" when trying to write, or accidentally exposing private keys by passing a Signer where only a Provider is needed.

children.title: Contract Instances and Calling Functions
children.description: An ethers.js Contract instance is created from an address and an ABI, and it maps Solidity function signatures to callable JavaScript methods. Without understanding how ethers encodes arguments and decodes return values you will send malformed calldata and misinterpret results, especially with BigInt values.

children.title: Running Scripts with npx hardhat run
children.description: Hardhat injects a pre-configured ethers instance and the correct network context into scripts run with npx hardhat run, eliminating manual provider setup. Running scripts with plain node bypasses this injection and leads to missing network configs, wrong chain IDs, and frustrating connection errors.

children.title: Reading Contract State from Scripts
children.description: Scripts can call view functions on deployed contracts to verify state, automate monitoring, or generate reports — all without spending gas. Not knowing how to do this forces developers to rely on Etherscan or Remix for state inspection, which is impractical in any serious development workflow.

dapp-developer.title: Connecting React to Ethereum with ethers.js
dapp-developer.description: Build a React frontend that connects to MetaMask and reads data from a deployed smart contract using ethers.js. Understand providers, signers, and contract instances.

resources.label: Full Stack dApp Tutorial - Nader Dabit
Build a dApp with React and Solidity

dapp-developer.challenge: Build a React page that connects to MetaMask, reads a value from your counter contract, and shows the current block number.

children.title: window.ethereum and EIP-1193
children.description: MetaMask injects a window.ethereum provider into every page that implements the EIP-1193 standard, giving dApps a doorway to request accounts and send transactions. Not understanding this injection means you will not know why the provider is undefined in server-side rendering or how to detect when MetaMask is not installed.

children.title: Requesting Accounts and Handling Permissions
children.description: eth_requestAccounts triggers a MetaMask popup for the user to approve wallet access; the returned array of addresses is used throughout the session. Skipping permission handling causes silent failures where your app assumes an address is available but the user never approved access, leading to confusing blank states.

children.title: Async State Management for Contract Reads
children.description: Contract reads are async RPC calls that should be tracked with loading, error, and data states in React. Without proper state management, components render before data arrives, throw uncaught promise errors, or display stale values after the user switches accounts.

children.title: Displaying Wallet Balance and Block Number
children.description: Fetching ETH balance uses ethers.js provider.getBalance() which returns a BigInt in wei that must be formatted to ether for display. Displaying raw wei values, or not refreshing on new blocks, produces a confusing UX where numbers look wrong and balances never update.

resources.label: Full Stack dApp Tutorial - Dev.to Nader Dabit

children.title: Writing to Contracts via Signer
children.description: State-changing contract calls require a Signer that holds a private key (or delegates to MetaMask); calling a write function on a read-only Provider results in a "call revert exception" or "missing signer" error. Understanding the Provider-to-Signer upgrade path is essential for every button that modifies blockchain state.

resources.label: Build a dApp with React - freeCodeCamp

children.title: Waiting for Transaction Receipts
children.description: Calling contract.someWriteFunction() returns a TransactionResponse immediately, but the transaction is not confirmed until you await tx.wait() which resolves after the specified number of blocks. Not awaiting the receipt means your UI shows success before the transaction is actually mined, which confuses users if the tx later fails or gets dropped.

children.title: JSON-RPC Methods Every dApp Developer Uses
children.description: eth_call, eth_sendTransaction, eth_getBalance, and eth_getLogs are the four JSON-RPC methods behind almost every dApp interaction. Not understanding them means you cannot debug failing requests in network tools, interpret error responses from RPC providers, or write scripts that interact with any EVM node directly.

children.title: Chain IDs and Network Detection
children.description: Every EVM network has a unique Chain ID that prevents transactions signed for one network from being replayed on another. Failing to validate the connected chain ID before submitting transactions can cause users to interact with the wrong contract version, sending funds to a dead address on a testnet instead of mainnet.

dapp-developer.title: Smart Contract Patterns and ERC Standards
dapp-developer.description: Learn widely used smart contract design patterns and ERC token standards. Use OpenZeppelin contracts as audited base implementations.
dapp-developer.challenge: Deploy an ERC-20 token using OpenZeppelin, then write a React button that transfers tokens between two test accounts.

children.title: ERC-20 Fungible Token Standard
children.description: ERC-20 defines a common interface (transfer, approve, transferFrom, balanceOf) that allows any token to work with any wallet or DEX without custom integration. Deviating from the standard — for example missing the Transfer event — breaks compatibility with every tool in the ecosystem including wallets, explorers, and analytics dashboards.

children.title: ERC-721 Non-Fungible Token Standard
children.description: ERC-721 assigns each token a unique ID and tracks ownership in a mapping, enabling provably scarce digital assets. Missing the safeTransferFrom receiver check causes tokens sent to contracts that cannot handle them to become permanently locked and unrecoverable.

children.title: Using OpenZeppelin as Audited Base Classes
children.description: OpenZeppelin provides battle-tested, audited implementations of ERC standards, access control, and security patterns that save months of development and reduce audit surface. Reimplementing these from scratch introduces well-known vulnerabilities that the OpenZeppelin team has already fixed, increasing risk without any benefit.

children.title: Ownable and Access Control
children.description: OpenZeppelin Ownable adds an owner address with a modifier that restricts sensitive functions; AccessControl provides role-based permissions for more complex systems. Omitting access control entirely allows any address to call admin functions like mint, pause, or withdraw, which is one of the most common causes of DeFi exploits.

children.title: Reentrancy Guard Pattern
children.description: Reentrancy occurs when an external contract calls back into your contract before the first execution completes, potentially draining funds by re-entering a withdrawal function. This vulnerability emptied The DAO of 3.6 million ETH in 2016; applying the checks-effects-interactions pattern or OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard is non-negotiable for any contract that sends ETH.

children.title: Proxy and Upgradeable Contract Patterns
children.description: Proxy patterns allow deployed contracts to be upgraded by pointing a proxy address at a new implementation, preserving the original address and user balances. Misunderstanding storage layout collisions between proxy and implementation contracts causes corrupted state that can lock user funds with no recovery path.

children.title: Reading Token Balances and Metadata
children.description: ERC-20 name, symbol, decimals, and balanceOf are standard read functions available on any compliant token contract. Not accounting for the decimals value when formatting balances causes values to display with 18 extra zeros, which erodes user trust and can cause incorrect amount calculations in transfer forms.

children.title: ERC-20 Approve and TransferFrom Flow
children.description: Many DeFi interactions require a two-step approve + transferFrom pattern where the user first authorizes a contract to spend tokens on their behalf. Skipping the approval step causes the downstream transferFrom to revert with an allowance error; not checking existing allowances wastes gas by issuing redundant approval transactions.

dapp-developer.title: Testing Smart Contracts
dapp-developer.description: Write comprehensive unit and integration tests for smart contracts using Hardhat and Chai. Understand how to simulate time, blocks, and edge cases.
dapp-developer.challenge: Achieve 100% line coverage on your ERC-20 contract, including testing transfer-fail and allowance edge cases.

children.title: Unit Testing Contracts with Chai
children.description: Hardhat's test runner uses Mocha and Chai to write expressive assertions against deployed contract state, events, and reverts. Skipping tests and relying on manual Remix interaction means bugs are discovered on testnet (or worse, mainnet) rather than in milliseconds on a local node where they cost nothing to fix.

children.title: Testing Events and Reverts
children.description: Hardhat Chai Matchers provides .emit() and .revertedWith() / .revertedWithCustomError() matchers that make event and revert assertions readable and safe. Without testing reverts explicitly, a contract that silently succeeds when it should have rejected an invalid input goes undetected until a real user loses funds.

children.title: Simulating Time with evm_increaseTime
children.description: Many contracts use block.timestamp for lockup periods, vesting schedules, or auction deadlines; evm_increaseTime lets tests advance the clock without waiting for real blocks. Without this tool you cannot test time-gated contract functions, leaving entire branches of logic untested.

children.title: Fixtures and Test Setup with loadFixture
children.description: loadFixture from @nomicfoundation/hardhat-network-helpers snapshots the EVM state after initial setup and restores it at the start of each test, making test suites orders of magnitude faster than re-deploying contracts in beforeEach. Without fixtures, test suites slow to a crawl and developers skip writing thorough tests to save time.

children.title: solidity-coverage and Line Coverage Reports
children.description: solidity-coverage instruments Solidity bytecode to track which lines, branches, and statements are executed during tests, generating an HTML report. Untested branches are almost always where production bugs hide; a coverage report makes invisible gaps visible before an audit or deployment.

children.title: Gas Reporting with hardhat-gas-reporter
children.description: hardhat-gas-reporter prints a per-function gas usage table after every test run, making it easy to catch expensive operations before they surprise users with high transaction costs. Discovering that a function costs 500,000 gas on mainnet at $50 per call only after deployment is a product-level failure.

children.title: Impersonating Accounts in Tests
children.description: Hardhat's hardhat_impersonateAccount JSON-RPC method lets tests send transactions from any address, including whale wallets or protocol multisigs, without holding the private key. This is essential for testing admin-only paths, governance votes, and contract interactions that require specific caller addresses.

children.title: Testing Multi-Contract Interactions
children.description: Real dApps involve multiple contracts calling each other — a router calling a pool calling a token, for example. Writing tests that only exercise contracts in isolation misses inter-contract bugs like incorrect approval amounts, unexpected revert propagation, and reentrancy across contract boundaries.

dapp-developer.title: Testnets, IPFS, and Contract Verification
dapp-developer.description: Deploy contracts to a public testnet using an RPC provider and verify source code on Etherscan. Store off-chain data on IPFS using Pinata or web3.storage.
dapp-developer.challenge: Deploy a contract to Sepolia, verify it on Etherscan, then store a JSON metadata file on IPFS and log its CID.

children.title: Alchemy and Infura as RPC Providers
children.description: Running your own Ethereum node requires hundreds of gigabytes of disk and days to sync; Alchemy and Infura provide managed JSON-RPC endpoints so you can deploy and interact with contracts immediately. Choosing a provider with unreliable uptime or rate limits will cause your dApp to fail under real traffic.

children.title: Deploying to Sepolia Testnet
children.description: Sepolia is the canonical Ethereum proof-of-stake testnet where you can test contracts with real network conditions using free faucet ETH. Skipping testnet and going straight to mainnet means paying real gas to discover bugs that could have been caught for free.

children.title: Verifying Source Code on Etherscan
children.description: Verified contracts on Etherscan display human-readable source code, a read/write UI, and ABI download — transforming an opaque bytecode blob into a transparent, auditable contract. Unverified contracts cannot be directly used through Etherscan, break user trust, and complicate audits.

children.title: Reading Contract State on Etherscan
children.description: Etherscan's Read Contract tab lets anyone call view functions without a wallet, making it the fastest way to verify that deployed state matches expectations. Not using it means taking longer to diagnose deployment bugs and having no way to share a direct link to contract state with non-technical stakeholders.

children.title: IPFS Content Addressing and CIDs
children.description: IPFS addresses content by its cryptographic hash (CID) rather than by location, meaning the same file always has the same address regardless of who hosts it. Using a centralized URL in an NFT tokenURI creates a single point of failure where the off-chain data can be changed or deleted, breaking the immutability guarantee that NFT ownership implies.

children.title: Pinning Files with Pinata
children.description: IPFS garbage-collects unpinned content; pinning services like Pinata pay nodes to keep your files available indefinitely. Uploading to IPFS without pinning means your files may disappear within hours as nodes evict unpinned content, making your dApp's metadata inaccessible.

children.title: Storing IPFS CIDs in Smart Contracts
children.description: Storing a CID string (or its bytes32 hash) in a contract creates a permanent, tamper-proof link between on-chain ownership and off-chain data. Storing only a HTTP URL makes the metadata mutable and censorable, which undermines the trust model that users expect from a blockchain-backed application.

resources.label: Storing Data on IPFS - Alchemy Docs

children.title: IPFS HTTP Gateways for Frontend Display
children.description: Browsers cannot natively resolve ipfs:// URIs; public HTTP gateways like gateway.ipfs.io and Cloudflare translate them into standard HTTP requests. Hardcoding a single slow public gateway causes images to load slowly or fail entirely — using multiple gateway fallbacks or a dedicated pinning service gateway is required for production.

dapp-developer.title: dApp UX - Transactions, Errors, and State
dapp-developer.description: Build production-quality dApp UX that handles pending transactions, user rejections, network errors, and chain switching. Display real-time feedback to users.
dapp-developer.challenge: Add full transaction lifecycle UI to your dApp: pending spinner, success message, and friendly error handling for rejected or failed transactions.

children.title: Pending, Mining, and Confirmed States
children.description: A transaction moves through pending (in mempool), mining (included in a block), and confirmed (enough subsequent blocks) states, each requiring a different UI response. Treating a transaction as done the moment it is submitted, before receipt, causes users to act on unconfirmed state and exposes them to race conditions if the transaction is dropped.

children.title: Handling MetaMask User Rejection
children.description: When a user clicks Reject in MetaMask, ethers.js throws an error with code 4001 (ACTION_REJECTED in v6); this must be caught and shown as a benign cancellation, not a scary error. Treating a user rejection as an unexpected failure breaks the UX and can show error messages that make users think something went wrong with their wallet.

children.title: react-hot-toast for Transaction Feedback
children.description: react-hot-toast provides a promise-based API (toast.promise) that automatically shows pending, success, and error notifications tied to a transaction's lifecycle. Without feedback toasts, users click submit buttons multiple times (submitting duplicate transactions) because they see no indication that their action was received.

children.title: Disabling Buttons During Pending Transactions
children.description: A transaction button must be disabled and show a loading state from the moment the user submits until the transaction is confirmed or fails; otherwise double-submits create duplicate transactions that waste user gas. Tracking the isPending and isConfirmed states returned by ethers.js or wagmi hooks is the correct way to manage this.

children.title: Detecting and Switching Networks
children.description: A user might open a dApp on Ethereum mainnet while their wallet is on Polygon; the dApp must detect the mismatch and prompt a network switch via wallet_switchEthereumChain before any transaction. Silently allowing transactions on the wrong chain results in failed transactions or, in the worst case, tokens sent to an address that exists on a different chain with no recovery.

children.title: Optimistic UI Updates and Rollbacks
children.description: Optimistic UI shows the expected result of a transaction immediately, then rolls back if the transaction fails — a pattern that makes dApps feel as responsive as Web2 apps. Without optimistic updates, every state-change action makes the UI feel unresponsive for the 12-second block time, degrading the user experience significantly compared to centralized alternatives.

children.title: Decoding Contract Revert Reasons
children.description: When a transaction reverts on-chain, the error data contains an encoded reason string or custom error that must be decoded to display a meaningful message to the user. Showing raw hex revert data or a generic "transaction failed" message leaves users with no understanding of what went wrong or how to fix it.

children.title: Gas Estimation Failures as Early Warnings
children.description: Calling eth_estimateGas before broadcasting a transaction will revert if the transaction would fail on-chain, letting you catch and explain the error before any gas is spent. Skipping gas pre-estimation means users pay gas for transactions that were doomed to fail, creating a terrible experience and eroding trust in the dApp.

dapp-developer.title: Smart Contract Security Fundamentals
dapp-developer.description: Learn the most common smart contract vulnerabilities and how to prevent them. Run automated security scanners and read through audit reports to build a security mindset.
dapp-developer.challenge: Run Slither on your ERC-20 contract, fix every high and medium finding, then write a short threat model for a simple DEX.

children.title: Reentrancy Attacks
children.description: Reentrancy happens when an external call is made before state is updated, allowing the callee to re-enter the function and drain funds in a loop. The DAO hack in 2016 lost 3.6 million ETH through this pattern; applying checks-effects-interactions ordering or OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard is the minimum required for any function that transfers ETH or calls untrusted contracts.

children.title: Access Control Flaws
children.description: Missing onlyOwner or role checks on privileged functions like mint, pause, or upgradeProxy allow any address to call them, enabling an attacker to mint unlimited tokens or drain the treasury. The most expensive hacks in DeFi history have been simple access control omissions, not cryptographic breaks.

children.title: Slither Static Analyzer
children.description: Slither is a Python-based static analysis framework that detects over 80 vulnerability patterns in Solidity source code in seconds. Skipping static analysis before deployment is the equivalent of shipping production code without a linter — many bugs that Slither catches automatically would otherwise only surface in a paid security audit or a live exploit.

children.title: Reading Public Audit Reports
children.description: Public audit reports from firms like Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, and ConsenSys Diligence catalog exactly how past vulnerabilities were found and fixed in real production contracts. Developers who study audit reports build a pattern-matching intuition that lets them spot the same classes of bugs in their own code long before it reaches an auditor.

children.title: Front-Running and Sandwich Attacks
children.description: Miners and MEV searchers can observe pending transactions in the mempool and insert their own transactions before or after them to profit at the user's expense — a sandwich attack on a DEX trade is the most common form. Designs that do not use slippage protection or commit-reveal schemes are trivially exploitable on any public EVM network.

children.title: Commit-Reveal Scheme for Fairness
children.description: A commit-reveal scheme hides a user's intention (e.g., a bid or a move) in the first transaction and reveals it in a second, preventing front-runners from acting on information before it is finalized. Without this pattern in auctions, games, or randomness-dependent contracts, any action visible in the mempool can be exploited by MEV bots.

children.title: SWC Registry as a Vulnerability Reference
children.description: The Smart Contract Weakness Classification Registry catalogs every known Solidity vulnerability with a numeric ID, description, and example, similar to CVEs in traditional security. Consulting it before finalizing a contract design ensures you have not missed any known attack class relevant to your use case.

children.title: Smart Contract Audit Checklist
children.description: Auditors use systematic checklists covering access control, math, external calls, and event emission to ensure no surface area is missed during a review. Self-auditing against a checklist before professional review reduces the cost of auditing by eliminating low-hanging fruit and lets auditors focus on complex logic bugs.

dapp-developer.title: Mainnet Deployment and Production Readiness
dapp-developer.description: Deploy a fully tested and audited dApp to Ethereum mainnet using production-grade tooling. Set up monitoring, alerts, and a multisig for admin functions.
dapp-developer.challenge: Deploy your dApp to Ethereum mainnet (or a mainnet fork), set up a Tenderly alert for any failed transaction, and document your rollback plan.

children.title: Gas Optimization Before Mainnet
children.description: Gas costs on mainnet directly translate to real money paid by users; optimizing storage layout, using custom errors, and packing variables before deployment can cut per-transaction costs by 30-50%. Deploying an unoptimized contract to mainnet is not just wasteful — on high-traffic contracts it makes the protocol economically uncompetitive.

children.title: Mainnet Fork Final Testing
children.description: Running your entire test suite against a mainnet fork (not just a local node) validates that your contracts interact correctly with real deployed protocols and actual token balances before spending real gas. Skipping this step means integration bugs with live protocols are only discovered after deployment, when they are expensive to diagnose and impossible to roll back.

children.title: Gnosis Safe Multisig for Protocol Admin
children.description: A Gnosis Safe (now Safe) multisig requires M-of-N signers to approve any admin action, eliminating single-key risk for protocol ownership. Using an EOA as the owner of a production contract means a stolen or compromised private key can drain the entire protocol — multisig is mandatory for any contract holding real value.

children.title: Timelocks for Parameter Changes
children.description: A timelock controller delays admin actions (like upgrading a contract or changing fees) by a fixed period, giving users time to exit if they disagree with the change. Without a timelock, a compromised multisig or a rogue admin can instantly drain a protocol or rug users with no warning.

children.title: Tenderly for Transaction Monitoring
children.description: Tenderly provides real-time transaction simulations, alerts on failed transactions, and dashboards for monitoring protocol health across mainnet and testnets. Running a production dApp without monitoring means the first notification of an exploit or bug comes from a user losing funds rather than from an alert you set up proactively.

children.title: Incident Response and Pause Mechanisms
children.description: OpenZeppelin's Pausable pattern lets a trusted admin freeze contract interactions in response to an active exploit, buying time for a fix or upgrade. Without a pause mechanism, the only response to an ongoing exploit is to watch it drain while a fix is being developed, since deployed contracts cannot be changed without a proxy.

resources.label: Incident Response for Smart Contracts - ConsenSys

children.title: Upgrade Strategy and Proxy Management
children.description: Transparent and UUPS proxy patterns allow logic upgrades while preserving state and contract address, but storage collisions during upgrades can corrupt existing data. Upgrading without using OpenZeppelin's upgrade-safe contracts and storage gap pattern has permanently bricked several production protocols.

children.title: Bug Bounty Setup on Immunefi
children.description: A bug bounty program incentivizes white-hat hackers to responsibly disclose vulnerabilities rather than exploit them; Immunefi is the leading Web3 bug bounty platform with standardized severity tiers. Protocols without a bug bounty are less likely to receive responsible disclosure, meaning attackers have less incentive not to exploit vulnerabilities they find.

web3-frontend.title: Web and Ethereum Fundamentals for Frontend Devs
web3-frontend.description: Understand the Ethereum JSON-RPC API and how browsers connect to wallets. Learn the difference between a provider, a signer, and a contract interface.
topics: JSON-RPC methods like eth_call and eth_sendTransaction
web3-frontend.challenge: Use window.ethereum directly in a plain HTML page to request accounts and display the connected address.

children.title: Injected Provider (window.ethereum)
children.description: The browser injects window.ethereum when a wallet extension is installed, exposing the EIP-1193 interface. If you don't understand this layer, you'll write code that silently fails in non-MetaMask environments and breaks on mobile browsers where no injection exists.

children.title: Provider vs Signer
children.description: A provider reads chain state anonymously while a signer holds a private key and can authorize transactions. Confusing the two causes runtime errors when you try to call write methods on a read-only object, a mistake that is hard to debug without understanding the distinction.

children.title: Vite + React Setup for Web3
children.description: Vite's native ESM bundling handles the BigInt and crypto globals that Web3 libraries require without the polyfill pain of older CRA setups. Skipping a proper Vite config leads to mysterious 'Buffer is not defined' errors that consume hours of debugging time.

children.title: Ethereum JSON-RPC Methods
children.description: Every wallet and node interaction ultimately maps to a JSON-RPC call such as eth_call or eth_sendTransaction. Understanding these raw methods prevents you from treating higher-level libraries as magic boxes and helps you diagnose failures when the abstraction leaks.

children.title: Accounts and Addresses
children.description: Ethereum has two account types — externally owned (EOA) and contract accounts — and confusing them leads to incorrect gas estimates, failed transactions, and broken token balance logic. Every address is a 20-byte hex string derived from a public key, and displaying them without checksum encoding causes subtle user-facing errors.

children.title: Transaction Lifecycle
children.description: A transaction passes through pending, included, and confirmed states before it is safe to act on. Updating UI immediately after submission without waiting for block confirmations causes ghost transactions and inconsistent state that confuses users and breaks optimistic updates.

children.title: TypeScript for Web3 Frontend
children.description: TypeScript catches ABI mismatches and incorrect address types at compile time rather than silently passing wrong data to the blockchain. Without strict types, a single transposed hex string can drain funds or cause unrecoverable state changes with no console warning.

children.title: Wallet Browser Extensions
children.description: Installing MetaMask, Coinbase Wallet, and at least one mobile wallet simulator lets you test multi-wallet flows that production users rely on. Testing with only one wallet hides provider conflicts that break apps when a second extension is installed.

web3-frontend.title: viem - Low-Level Ethereum Client
web3-frontend.description: Learn viem as a type-safe, modern alternative to ethers.js for Ethereum interactions. Build public clients for reading and wallet clients for sending transactions.
web3-frontend.challenge: Use viem to read the total supply and name of a deployed ERC-20 token on a testnet, then display the values in a React component.

children.title: Public Client and Chain Config
children.description: A viem publicClient is stateless and transport-bound, meaning you must pass the correct chain object or every RPC call will target the wrong network silently. Getting chain configuration wrong is one of the most common sources of 'contract not found' errors in multi-chain apps.

children.title: readContract and ABI Encoding
children.description: viem's readContract encodes calldata from the ABI definition automatically, but passing a mismatched ABI or wrong function name silently returns undefined instead of throwing. Understanding how ABI encoding works ensures you can debug unexpected zero values and revert errors.

children.title: BigInt in JavaScript
children.description: All Ethereum values are returned as BigInt in viem because standard JavaScript numbers lose precision for amounts above 2^53. Mixing number and BigInt types in arithmetic causes TypeErrors at runtime, and displaying raw BigInt wei values to users produces meaningless 18-digit numbers.

children.title: Type-Safe ABI with viem
children.description: Using as const on ABI arrays lets viem infer exact function return types at compile time, so you catch type mismatches before hitting the network. Without this pattern, every contract call returns unknown and you lose TypeScript's primary value for preventing runtime errors.

children.title: Wallet Client and Account
children.description: A viem walletClient wraps a signing account and is required for any state-changing operation. Attempting to write through a publicClient throws a non-obvious error, and creating a walletClient from window.ethereum requires explicit account injection that is easy to omit.

children.title: Error Handling in viem
children.description: viem exposes structured error types like ContractFunctionRevertedError with decoded revert reasons, but you must narrow errors with instanceof checks to access them. Treating all errors as generic strings discards the revert reason and leaves users with 'transaction failed' instead of actionable feedback.

children.title: Testnets and RPC Endpoints
children.description: Alchemy and Infura provide reliable Sepolia testnet RPCs that behave like mainnet without real funds. Using public rate-limited RPCs in development causes intermittent failures that are impossible to distinguish from bugs in your own code.

children.title: viem vs ethers.js
children.description: viem is tree-shakable and TypeScript-first while ethers.js has a larger ecosystem and more existing tutorials. Choosing the wrong library for your team's familiarity level causes slow onboarding, so understanding the tradeoffs helps you make an informed decision.

web3-frontend.title: wagmi - React Hooks for Ethereum
web3-frontend.description: Use wagmi hooks to manage wallet connections, account state, and contract reads in React applications. Understand how wagmi wraps viem under the hood.
web3-frontend.challenge: Build a React component that uses wagmi to display wallet balance, let the user connect and disconnect, and read a contract value.

children.title: WagmiProvider and Config
children.description: wagmi requires wrapping your app in WagmiProvider with a config object that defines chains, transports, and connectors. Getting the provider tree wrong — such as placing it inside a component that re-mounts — causes all hooks to reset on every render, wiping connection state.

children.title: useAccount and useConnect
children.description: useAccount exposes the connected address, chain, and status while useConnect provides connector instances for triggering wallet popups. Accessing address before checking isConnected returns undefined and causes rendering errors or incorrect zero-address contract calls.

children.title: useReadContract
children.description: useReadContract caches contract reads using TanStack Query and re-fetches on block updates when watch is enabled. Without caching awareness, duplicate hook usage in the same component tree sends redundant RPC calls that quickly exhaust free-tier rate limits.

children.title: useWriteContract and useWaitForTransactionReceipt
children.description: useWriteContract returns a hash immediately after wallet signature, but the transaction is not confirmed until useWaitForTransactionReceipt resolves. Acting on the hash alone — updating UI, unlocking gated content — before confirmation creates inconsistent state that breaks when transactions fail or are replaced.

children.title: Chain Switching with useSwitchChain
children.description: useSwitchChain prompts the wallet to switch to a target network and add it if missing. Calling contract functions before confirming the user is on the right chain causes transactions to fail silently on the wrong network with no meaningful error for the user.

children.title: wagmi CLI Type Generation
children.description: The wagmi CLI reads ABIs and generates fully typed React hooks for each contract function, eliminating manual ABI casting. Skipping this step means every ABI import is typed as any, which voids TypeScript's protection and hides breaking changes when contracts are redeployed.

children.title: wagmi and viem Relationship
children.description: wagmi is a React layer over viem: every hook ultimately calls a viem action, and wagmi passes the config's viem clients through React context. Understanding this means you can drop down to raw viem when hooks don't cover an edge case instead of working around the abstraction with hacks.

children.title: Connector Architecture
children.description: wagmi connectors abstract over different wallet protocols — injected, WalletConnect, Coinbase SDK — and normalize them to a common interface. Writing connector-specific code instead of using this abstraction breaks when users have different wallets installed.

web3-frontend.title: RainbowKit - Wallet Connection UI
web3-frontend.description: Integrate RainbowKit to provide a polished multi-wallet connect modal with minimal configuration. Customize themes, chains, and wallet lists to match your brand.
topics: Custom theme with lightTheme and darkTheme
web3-frontend.challenge: Replace your custom connect button with RainbowKit, apply a dark theme, and restrict the app to Ethereum mainnet and Sepolia only.

children.title: ConnectButton Component
children.description: RainbowKit's ConnectButton renders the full connect modal, account avatar, balance, and disconnect flow in a single import. Building a custom connect UI without it requires re-implementing dozens of edge cases — wallet detection, pending states, network switching prompts — that RainbowKit already handles correctly.

children.title: Provider Setup Order
children.description: RainbowKit requires its RainbowKitProvider inside WagmiProvider and QueryClientProvider in a specific nesting order. Reversing any layer causes hook context errors that are cryptic without understanding why the provider tree matters.

children.title: Custom Themes in RainbowKit
children.description: RainbowKit exposes lightTheme, darkTheme, and midnightTheme helpers with a fine-grained override API for colors, fonts, and border radius. Overriding styles via CSS class injection on the modal breaks with RainbowKit version updates because the internal class names are not part of the public API.

children.title: Chain Restriction in RainbowKit
children.description: Passing a restricted chains array to getDefaultConfig limits the wallet modal to only show supported networks, preventing users from connecting on chains where your contracts don't exist. Without chain restriction, users transact on wrong networks and see cryptic contract-not-found errors.

children.title: ENS Name Display
children.description: RainbowKit automatically resolves ENS names and avatars for connected addresses when configured with a mainnet provider. Displaying raw hex addresses to users who have ENS names damages UX and signals that your app is not production-quality.

children.title: Custom Wallet List
children.description: RainbowKit lets you define which wallets appear in the modal and in what order, which is critical for apps targeting a specific ecosystem or region. Showing all 20 default wallets to DeFi power users buries their preferred wallet and increases time-to-connect friction.

children.title: ConnectKit by Family
children.description: ConnectKit is a RainbowKit alternative with a different visual style and simpler configuration that some teams prefer. Knowing both options lets you pick the one that matches your design system without committing to one before evaluating the tradeoffs.

children.title: WalletConnect AppKit (Web3Modal)
children.description: WalletConnect's AppKit (formerly Web3Modal) supports non-EVM chains and offers a unified modal for Solana and Bitcoin wallets alongside Ethereum. Apps targeting a multi-chain user base benefit from AppKit's broader wallet coverage that RainbowKit does not provide.

web3-frontend.title: Reading and Displaying On-Chain Data
web3-frontend.description: Build data-rich dashboards that read multiple contract values, handle loading states, and refresh on block updates. Understand caching and revalidation patterns.
topics: React Query cache and stale time
web3-frontend.challenge: Build a token dashboard that displays name, symbol, total supply, and the connected wallet balance using a single useReadContracts call.

children.title: useReadContracts for Multiple Calls
children.description: useReadContracts batches multiple contract reads into a single multicall, drastically reducing RPC round trips compared to calling useReadContract separately for each value. Using individual hooks per field on a dashboard with 20 tokens quickly exhausts free-tier rate limits and slows initial page load.

children.title: React Query Cache and Stale Time
children.description: wagmi hooks are backed by TanStack Query and respect staleTime and gcTime configuration, meaning stale data is served instantly while a background refetch runs silently. Not configuring stale time causes every navigation to trigger a spinner even for data that changes once per block.

children.title: watchContractEvent for Real-Time Updates
children.description: watchContractEvent subscribes to contract events via WebSocket and calls a callback on each new log, enabling real-time dashboards without polling. Polling via refetchInterval wastes RPC quota and adds latency; event watching gives sub-second updates at far lower cost.

children.title: Handling Reverts Gracefully
children.description: Contract reads can revert if a condition is unmet — for example, calling ownerOf on a non-existent tokenId — and wagmi surfaces this as an error state rather than throwing. Ignoring the error state and always reading result causes undefined property access errors that crash the component.

children.title: Formatting Token Amounts with formatUnits
children.description: ERC-20 balances are stored as integer wei with 18 decimals, and displaying the raw BigInt to users shows an 18-digit number instead of a human-readable amount. Using formatUnits from viem with the correct decimal argument is essential, and getting the decimals wrong by even one position displays values that are off by a factor of 10.

children.title: ENS Resolution with useEnsName
children.description: useEnsName resolves a wallet address to its ENS name when available, turning 0xabc...def into readable identities like vitalik.eth. Skipping ENS display makes your app feel impersonal and harder to use for power users who expect their on-chain identity to be recognized.

children.title: Skeleton Loaders for On-Chain Data
children.description: Contract reads have unpredictable latency depending on RPC provider and network congestion, so a skeleton loader prevents layout shift and signals that data is loading. Rendering null or zero while fetching causes flash-of-wrong-content that erodes user trust in the displayed numbers.

children.title: ABI Decoding and Contract Instances
children.description: Passing an outdated or mismatched ABI to a contract hook silently returns garbage data because viem decodes the bytes it receives using whatever ABI you provide. Always regenerate ABI files after contract redeployment and store them in version control to prevent subtle data corruption bugs.

web3-frontend.title: Writing Transactions and User Flows
web3-frontend.description: Implement complete write flows including form validation, transaction submission, confirmation waiting, and error handling. Build flows that feel reliable to users.
web3-frontend.challenge: Build an ERC-20 transfer form with approve, transfer, and full transaction state feedback including pending, success, and error messages.

children.title: Simulate Before Write
children.description: Calling simulateContract before writeContract lets you catch revert reasons locally without spending gas or prompting the user for a transaction that will fail. Skipping simulation means users see MetaMask popups for transactions that immediately revert, which destroys trust and creates support burden.

children.title: Gas Estimation and Limits
children.description: Letting the wallet estimate gas works for simple transfers, but complex DeFi interactions can run out of gas mid-execution, causing partial state changes and lost funds. Adding a 20% buffer over eth_estimateGas prevents out-of-gas failures while keeping transactions economical.

children.title: Approve Before Interact Pattern
children.description: DeFi protocols cannot pull ERC-20 tokens from a user's wallet without a prior approve transaction granting an allowance. Forgetting the approval step causes every downstream transaction to revert with TRANSFER_FROM_FAILED, a confusing error for users unfamiliar with the ERC-20 model.

children.title: Multi-Step Transaction UI
children.description: Flows like approve-then-swap require orchestrating two sequential wallet prompts with state machine logic to track which step is active, pending, or failed. Implementing this as ad-hoc boolean flags leads to impossible states — showing 'swap' button before approval is confirmed — that confuse users and cause duplicate approvals.

children.title: User Rejection Error Handling
children.description: When a user clicks 'Reject' in their wallet, MetaMask throws error code 4001 and wagmi surfaces this as a UserRejectedRequestError. Not distinguishing this from a network error causes your app to show 'Transaction failed' instead of the friendlier 'You cancelled the transaction'.

children.title: Toast Notifications for Tx Feedback
children.description: react-hot-toast or sonner lets you show a persistent pending notification that updates to success or error when the transaction resolves. Without a toast system, users must manually check Etherscan and cannot tell whether their transaction is pending or lost.

children.title: Address Validation Before Submit
children.description: Sending tokens to an invalid or zero address is irreversible, so validating with isAddress from viem before enabling the submit button is non-negotiable. A missing checksum on a copy-pasted address is a common source of funds being sent to the wrong recipient.

children.title: Optimistic UI vs Confirmed State
children.description: Optimistic updates show the result immediately and roll back on failure, while confirmed updates wait for a receipt before changing UI. Choosing optimistic UI for financial state like balances is dangerous because a failed transaction leaves the UI showing incorrect amounts.

web3-frontend.title: Multi-Chain and Network Handling
web3-frontend.description: Build apps that work seamlessly across multiple EVM-compatible chains. Handle network detection, chain switching prompts, and chain-specific contract addresses.
topics: Detecting wrong network and showing warnings
Testing against L2 networks like Arbitrum and Optimism
web3-frontend.challenge: Make your dApp work on Ethereum mainnet, Arbitrum, and Optimism with the correct contract address loaded per chain.

children.title: Custom Chain Definitions in viem
children.description: viem ships chain definitions for all major EVM networks, but new L2s and testnets require a custom chain object with correct chainId, RPC URL, and native currency. Using a wrong chainId causes signed transactions to be rejected by nodes with 'invalid chain id' errors that look like wallet bugs.

children.title: Contract Address Mapping per Chain
children.description: Every network deployment of a contract produces a different address, and hardcoding a single address breaks your app silently on every other chain. A chainId-keyed address map ensures the correct contract is used per network and makes adding new chains a one-line change.

children.title: Wrong Network UX Warning
children.description: Detecting chain mismatch and showing a banner with a one-click switch prompt prevents users from signing transactions that will fail on the wrong network. Apps that silently submit transactions on the wrong chain cause real financial losses when users believe they interacted with a mainnet contract but actually hit a testnet.

children.title: Adding Custom RPC URLs
children.description: wagmi transports accept custom HTTP or WebSocket RPC URLs per chain, enabling you to use Alchemy or Infura keys instead of public nodes that rate-limit aggressively. Public RPCs are unsuitable for production because they fail silently under modest traffic and expose users to unreliable data.

children.title: Arbitrum and Optimism Differences
children.description: Arbitrum and Optimism have different block times, precompile availability, and gas pricing models from mainnet that affect contract behavior and UI polling frequency. Assuming L2 behaves identically to mainnet causes apps to display stale data or estimate gas incorrectly.

children.title: Bridging and Cross-Chain Awareness
children.description: Users frequently have assets on one chain and need to bridge before interacting with your protocol, and surfacing a bridge link at the right moment dramatically reduces drop-off. Ignoring cross-chain UX forces users to leave your app, find a bridge themselves, and return — many never do.

children.title: Hardhat Fork of L2 Networks
children.description: Hardhat can fork Arbitrum and Optimism mainnet locally so you test against real deployed contracts without spending gas. Testing only on Ethereum mainnet forks misses L2-specific precompile behavior that can cause contracts to behave differently than expected.

children.title: Tenderly Multi-Chain Simulation
children.description: Tenderly's simulation API lets you test transactions on any chain in a sandbox environment without broadcasting them. This is invaluable for verifying complex multi-chain flows before a production deployment where mistakes cannot be reversed.

web3-frontend.title: Signatures, Auth, and Sign-In with Ethereum
web3-frontend.description: Implement Sign-In with Ethereum (EIP-4361) for gasless authentication. Understand message signing, nonce-based anti-replay protection, and server-side verification.
web3-frontend.challenge: Build a SIWE login page that issues a JWT after signature verification and shows a gated dashboard to authenticated users only.

children.title: EIP-4361 SIWE Message Format
children.description: SIWE defines a standard human-readable message format that includes domain, nonce, and expiry to prevent replay attacks and phishing. Signing raw arbitrary strings instead of a SIWE-formatted message is exploitable: a malicious site can reuse the signature to impersonate the user on another service.

children.title: Nonce Generation and Anti-Replay
children.description: A server-generated cryptographically random nonce is included in every SIWE message and consumed on verification to prevent replay attacks. Reusing nonces or skipping nonce validation allows an attacker to replay a captured signature and impersonate a user across sessions.

children.title: Typed Data vs Personal Sign
children.description: EIP-712 presents structured data to users in a human-readable format inside their wallet, while personal_sign shows only raw bytes. Using personal_sign for complex authorization flows is a security vulnerability: users cannot see what they are authorizing and are prime targets for blind-signing attacks.

children.title: Permit (EIP-2612) Gasless Approvals
children.description: EIP-2612 permit allows token approvals via a signed message instead of an on-chain transaction, eliminating the two-step approve+transfer flow and saving users one gas payment. Not supporting permit in DeFi UIs forces users to pay extra gas and creates friction that reduces conversion rates.

children.title: JWT vs Cookie Sessions for SIWE
children.description: Iron Session stores encrypted session data in a cookie without a database, keeping the auth stack simple and avoiding JWT secret management. Using JWTs for SIWE without understanding expiry and rotation creates long-lived tokens that cannot be revoked if a user's wallet is compromised.

children.title: Server-Side SIWE Verification
children.description: The server must verify the SIWE message signature, check the nonce, validate the domain, and confirm the expiry before issuing a session. Skipping domain validation allows a malicious site to forward a user's SIWE signature to your API and log in as them.

children.title: Off-Chain Authorization with Signatures
children.description: Signed messages can gate access to API endpoints, prove NFT ownership, or submit off-chain votes without spending gas. Storing raw signatures in a database without tying them to a specific action and expiry allows signature reuse across unintended contexts.

children.title: Signature Recovery in Frontend
children.description: viem's recoverMessageAddress recovers the signer from a signature client-side, enabling instant ownership checks without a server round trip. Using this for security decisions without server-side confirmation is dangerous, as client-side recovery can be spoofed through prototype pollution attacks.

web3-frontend.title: Performance, Accessibility, and Testing
web3-frontend.description: Write unit and integration tests for Web3 React components using mocked providers. Audit performance and accessibility of your dApp and fix common issues.
topics: Mock Service Worker for RPC calls
web3-frontend.challenge: Write tests for your connect button, a contract read component, and a transfer form using mocked wagmi hooks, achieving 80% coverage.

children.title: Mocking wagmi Hooks in Vitest
children.description: wagmi provides a createConfig-compatible mock setup that lets you inject fake wallet state into tests without running a real blockchain. Without mocked providers, every component test tries to connect to window.ethereum, throws in jsdom, and produces 500 lines of cryptic errors instead of useful test failures.

children.title: Mock Service Worker for RPC Calls
children.description: MSW intercepts HTTP fetch calls at the network level, allowing you to stub RPC responses without modifying application code. Mocking at the module level instead masks the actual network boundary and lets integration bugs through that only appear in production.

children.title: Lighthouse Performance Audit
children.description: Lighthouse scores Web3 apps harshly because heavy wallet libraries like wagmi and viem add significant bundle weight. A score below 70 on mobile increases bounce rate measurably, and the audit pinpoints which libraries to code-split or lazy-load for the biggest gains.

children.title: Bundle Analysis with vite-bundle-visualizer
children.description: vite-bundle-visualizer generates a treemap of your bundle so you can see exactly which Web3 libraries contribute the most bytes. Without bundle analysis, optimizing performance is guesswork, and you may spend time on micro-optimizations while a 200 KB unused import sits ignored.

children.title: WCAG Compliance for Connect Flows
children.description: Wallet connect modals and transaction confirmation dialogs must meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios and keyboard navigation requirements to be usable by all users. Inaccessible connect flows legally exclude users in jurisdictions with digital accessibility laws and alienate a significant percentage of the user base.

children.title: Address Display Accessibility
children.description: Long hex addresses should be truncated with ellipsis and include a copy-to-clipboard button with proper aria-labels because screen readers cannot meaningfully read a 42-character hex string. Displaying full raw addresses without accessible controls creates an unusable experience for keyboard and screen reader users.

children.title: Synpress for E2E Wallet Testing
children.description: Synpress extends Playwright or Cypress with MetaMask automation, enabling true end-to-end tests that click through wallet popups and confirm transactions. Using unit tests alone cannot catch wallet-flow regressions where the modal opens but the confirmation button is unreachable.

children.title: Coverage Reporting with Istanbul
children.description: Vitest integrates Istanbul for coverage reports that show which Web3 hook call paths are untested. Coverage below 80% on transaction components is a meaningful risk because untested error branches are where users encounter crashes in production.

web3-frontend.title: Production Build, Security, and Deployment
web3-frontend.description: Harden and deploy your Web3 frontend to production with secure RPC key handling, Content Security Policy headers, and monitoring. Perform a security review of client-side code.
topics: Hiding RPC keys via proxy API routes
Front-end phishing and address spoofing mitigations
web3-frontend.challenge: Deploy your dApp to Vercel with RPC calls proxied through an API route, add strict CSP headers, and verify no API keys are exposed in the client bundle.

children.title: Vercel Deployment for Web3 Apps
children.description: Vercel's edge network and serverless functions are well-suited to Web3 frontends, enabling RPC key proxying through API routes without managing infrastructure. Deploying without an RPC proxy exposes your Alchemy or Infura API keys in client-side code where they can be extracted and abused.

children.title: Proxying RPC Calls via API Routes
children.description: Routing all RPC calls through a Next.js or Vercel API route hides your provider API key from the client bundle and allows you to add rate limiting per wallet address. Exposing the key directly means any user can extract it from network requests and drain your quota.

children.title: Content Security Policy for dApps
children.description: A strict CSP that whitelists known RPC and IPFS gateway origins blocks injected scripts from exfiltrating wallet signatures or replacing contract addresses on the page. Web3 apps without CSP are prime targets for address-swapping attacks that redirect user transactions to attacker-controlled contracts.

children.title: Subresource Integrity for CDN Assets
children.description: Adding integrity hashes to any third-party scripts prevents a compromised CDN from serving a modified script that intercepts wallet signatures. A single tampered analytics script with access to the page DOM is sufficient to steal every signed transaction from every user.

children.title: Code Splitting Web3 Libraries
children.description: wagmi and viem add roughly 150-200 KB to your bundle, and lazy-loading wallet connection code until the user clicks 'Connect' reduces initial page load significantly. Loading the entire Web3 stack upfront harms Core Web Vitals and penalizes users who may never connect a wallet.

children.title: Rate Limiting RPC Usage
children.description: Free-tier RPC providers enforce hard rate limits that cause 429 errors under normal user traffic, and without rate limiting your proxy those limits are hit by a single high-frequency user. Implementing per-address rate limiting on your proxy protects your quota and prevents one user from degrading service for everyone.

children.title: Address Spoofing Prevention
children.description: Displaying checksummed EIP-55 addresses and using monospace fonts for hex strings makes it visually obvious when one character has been changed by a clipboard hijacker. Users who paste addresses into your app without checksum validation can lose funds to malware that replaces the last few characters of a copied address.

children.title: Monitoring with Tenderly Alerts
children.description: Tenderly webhook alerts notify you when contracts receive unexpected calls or emit anomalous events, enabling rapid incident response before losses compound. Deploying to production without monitoring means you learn about exploits from Twitter rather than your own alerting system.

defi-developer.title: Web and Blockchain Fundamentals for DeFi
defi-developer.description: Build the conceptual foundation needed to understand decentralized finance protocols. Learn how Ethereum transactions, gas, and smart contract state enable financial primitives.
topics: Smart contract state and storage slots
defi-developer.challenge: Write a one-page summary explaining how an AMM pricing curve differs from a traditional order book exchange.

children.title: AMM vs Order Book
children.description: Automated Market Makers replace the traditional order book with a mathematical pricing curve, meaning any trade is always executable but at a price determined by the reserve ratio. Without grasping this, you cannot reason about slippage, price impact, or why liquidity depth matters when building on top of a DEX.

children.title: EVM Gas and Storage Model
children.description: Every DeFi interaction runs inside the EVM where compute and storage each cost gas; a misunderstanding of storage slots and SLOAD/SSTORE costs will lead to protocols that bleed users dry on fees. DeFi contracts must be gas-optimized because small per-call costs compound to millions of dollars across thousands of users.

children.title: Protocol Categories and Composability
children.description: DeFi protocols are grouped into DEXes, lending markets, derivatives, yield aggregators, and stablecoins; each layer can call any other, creating money-lego composability. Misunderstanding these boundaries leads to integration bugs where a contract assumes token behavior that only holds on one specific protocol.

children.title: ERC-20 Token Standard
children.description: ERC-20 is the universal language all DeFi protocols speak when moving value; a subtle misread of the approve/transferFrom pattern or a non-standard return value can cause a silent failure that drains a pool. Every DeFi developer must be able to read and reason about an ERC-20 implementation from memory.

children.title: Wallet and Key Lifecycle
children.description: Understanding how private keys generate addresses and sign transactions is a prerequisite for every DeFi task from testnet deployments to multisig operations. Getting this wrong in production means losing admin control of a live protocol with no recovery path.

children.title: Reading a Block Explorer
children.description: Etherscan is the primary debugging tool for every on-chain interaction; being unable to decode a transaction trace or an event log means you are flying blind when a protocol behaves unexpectedly. Mastering internal calls, event logs, and decoded input data is a core DeFi developer skill.

children.title: Fixed-Point Arithmetic in Solidity
children.description: Solidity has no native floating-point, so all DeFi math uses integer arithmetic with scaled units such as WAD (1e18) and RAY (1e27); a single missed scaling operation can cause massive rounding errors or overflow that silently misallocates user funds. Understanding precision conventions is non-negotiable before touching any financial formula.

resources.label: Solidity Docs - Units and Global Variables

children.title: Percentage and Basis Points
children.description: Fees, interest rates, and collateral ratios in DeFi are almost always expressed in basis points (1 bps = 0.01%) or ray-scaled percentages to avoid floating-point errors. Confusing these units when writing a fee calculation can result in charging 100x the intended fee or making the protocol trivially exploitable.

defi-developer.title: Solidity for DeFi - Core Patterns
defi-developer.description: Master Solidity patterns critical to DeFi: interfaces, low-level calls, and token handling. Understand how protocols interact with each other through shared interfaces.

resources.label: DeFi Solidity Patterns - Smart Contract Programmer

defi-developer.challenge: Write a Solidity contract that holds ERC-20 tokens and distributes them proportionally to depositors without using floating-point math.

children.title: Interfaces and Protocol Composability
children.description: DeFi contracts integrate with other protocols exclusively through Solidity interfaces, which define the ABI without any implementation; getting the interface wrong means calls silently revert or return garbage data, breaking the entire integration. Every major protocol (Uniswap, Aave, Compound) publishes official interfaces that must be used verbatim.

children.title: Checks-Effects-Interactions Pattern
children.description: The checks-effects-interactions (CEI) pattern is the single most important security rule in DeFi: all state changes must happen before any external calls to prevent reentrancy. The DAO hack and dozens of subsequent exploits were all CEI violations; ignoring this pattern when writing token withdrawal or swap logic is a critical mistake.

children.title: SafeERC20 and Non-Standard Tokens
children.description: Many real ERC-20 tokens (USDT, BNB) do not follow the spec exactly, returning no value or reverting on zero transfers; using raw transfer() instead of SafeERC20's safeTransfer() causes silent failures that can brick a DeFi contract. All DeFi contracts should use the OpenZeppelin SafeERC20 wrapper unconditionally.

children.title: ERC-777 Hooks and Reentrancy Risk
children.description: ERC-777 adds send/receive hooks that execute arbitrary code during token transfers, creating a reentrancy vector that has been exploited multiple times in lending protocols that accepted ERC-777 tokens as collateral. If your protocol accepts arbitrary tokens, you must account for this attack surface explicitly.

children.title: call vs delegatecall vs staticcall
children.description: The three Solidity call variants have fundamentally different security properties: call executes in the target's context, delegatecall runs the target's code in the caller's storage (the proxy pattern), and staticcall is read-only. Using delegatecall carelessly is how many proxy upgrade exploits occur when storage layouts collide.

children.title: Custom Errors and Revert Data
children.description: Custom errors introduced in Solidity 0.8.4 are significantly cheaper than string revert messages and provide typed revert data that off-chain tooling can decode for better debugging. DeFi protocols with hundreds of code paths cannot afford the gas overhead of string reverts, and custom errors also make auditing simpler.

children.title: Storage Slot Packing
children.description: Multiple Solidity variables that fit within 32 bytes can be packed into a single storage slot, reducing SLOAD/SSTORE costs by up to 3x; failing to pack tightly in a high-frequency DeFi contract like an AMM can double user gas costs at scale. Understanding how the compiler lays out storage is essential for writing efficient DeFi code.

children.title: Immutables and Constants
children.description: Variables declared as constant or immutable in Solidity are embedded directly in the bytecode and never access storage, making reads essentially free; DeFi protocol parameters like fee tiers or oracle addresses that never change should always use these qualifiers to save meaningful gas per interaction.

defi-developer.title: Foundry - Professional Smart Contract Toolchain
defi-developer.description: Set up the Foundry toolkit and learn to write tests in Solidity rather than JavaScript. Use Anvil for local forks and Cast for command-line contract interaction.
topics: Writing Foundry tests with Test and assertEq
Using Cast to call and send transactions
defi-developer.challenge: Migrate an existing Hardhat test suite to Foundry and add a fuzz test that covers at least 10,000 random input combinations.

children.title: Forge Test and Assertions
children.description: Foundry's Forge lets you write tests entirely in Solidity, eliminating the JavaScript glue layer and making tests far faster and closer to the code being tested. Without a proper Foundry test setup, fuzz testing and invariant testing of complex DeFi math is impractical.

children.title: Mainnet Forking with Anvil
children.description: Anvil can fork Ethereum mainnet at any block, letting you test your DeFi protocol against real Uniswap, Aave, and Chainlink deployments without deploying anything live. Testing without a mainnet fork means your integration tests use mock contracts that may not faithfully replicate the real protocol's edge cases.

children.title: Property-Based Fuzzing
children.description: Foundry's fuzzer generates thousands of random inputs against your DeFi functions to find edge cases that hand-written tests miss, such as division-by-zero in swap math or overflow in interest calculations. DeFi protocols that skip fuzz testing routinely ship with subtle arithmetic bugs that only surface at extreme values.

children.title: Gas Snapshots
children.description: forge snapshot records per-test gas usage and flags regressions in pull requests, ensuring that protocol optimizations do not accidentally get reverted. Without gas snapshots, DeFi teams routinely ship changes that silently increase swap or deposit costs by 20-30% with no visibility.

children.title: Cast for On-Chain Debugging
children.description: Cast lets you call any on-chain contract, decode transaction calldata, convert hex values, and query storage slots directly from the terminal, making it indispensable for debugging live DeFi integrations. Developers who rely solely on block explorers miss the ability to script multi-step investigations and automate checks.

children.title: Forge Scripts for Deployment
children.description: Forge scripts run Solidity deployment logic with mainnet simulation, dry-run mode, and automatic verification, replacing brittle JavaScript deploy scripts with type-safe Solidity. Using unstructured deploy scripts for a DeFi protocol increases the risk of deploying to the wrong address or with wrong constructor arguments.

children.title: foundry.toml Configuration
children.description: The foundry.toml file controls compiler version, optimizer runs, remappings, and RPC endpoints; a misconfigured optimizer setting can change the bytecode enough to invalidate a security audit. DeFi teams should pin the optimizer settings and Solidity version in foundry.toml to ensure audit and deployment bytecode match exactly.

children.title: Cheatcodes for State Manipulation
children.description: Foundry cheatcodes like vm.prank, vm.warp, and vm.deal let you impersonate addresses, fast-forward time, and fund accounts without modifying production code, enabling realistic simulation of liquidations, expired locks, and reward harvests. Without cheatcodes, testing time-dependent DeFi logic like vesting or TWAP is nearly impossible.

defi-developer.title: Building Automated Market Makers
defi-developer.description: Implement a simplified constant-product AMM from scratch to understand how Uniswap V2 pricing and liquidity work. Learn how LP tokens represent pool shares.

resources.label: Build Uniswap V1 from Scratch - Smart Contract Programmer

defi-developer.challenge: Build and test a minimal AMM that correctly calculates output amounts including a 0.3% fee, verified with at least 5 Foundry fuzz tests.

children.title: Constant Product Formula
children.description: The x*y=k invariant is the pricing engine behind Uniswap V2 and most AMM forks; without understanding how the curve determines output amounts and why the invariant must hold after every swap, you cannot safely implement or modify any AMM. Even small errors in the invariant check enable arbitrageurs to drain a pool.

children.title: Concentrated Liquidity (Uniswap V3)
