children.description: Uniswap V3 lets LPs concentrate capital within a specific price range defined by ticks, dramatically improving capital efficiency but introducing complex tick math and position management. Misunderstanding tick spacing or the sqrt price encoding leads to incorrect LP position calculations and significant financial loss.

children.title: LP Token Share Accounting
children.description: LP tokens represent a proportional share of a pool's reserves; the share math on mint and burn must account for existing liquidity to prevent first-depositor inflation attacks and donation exploits. Multiple DeFi protocols have been drained by an attacker who donated tokens to a fresh pool before any other LP joined.

children.title: Price Impact and Slippage Protection
children.description: Price impact is the percentage by which a trade moves the pool price, and slippage protection (a minimum output amount check) is the mechanism that stops users from being sandwiched. A DEX router that omits the amountOutMin check exposes every user to maximal extractable value front-running with no upper bound on losses.

children.title: Multi-Hop Routing
children.description: Uniswap V3's Router supports multi-hop paths encoded as a packed bytes sequence, allowing swaps through intermediate tokens when no direct pool exists; constructing this path incorrectly causes the transaction to revert or to route through an illiquid pool with catastrophic slippage. Every DEX aggregator and DeFi protocol that builds on Uniswap must handle multi-hop paths correctly.

children.title: Fee Tiers in Uniswap V3
children.description: Uniswap V3 offers multiple fee tiers (0.01%, 0.05%, 0.3%, 1%) and LPs choose which tier to provide liquidity in; selecting the wrong fee tier when integrating means routing through a low-liquidity pool with high slippage. Fee tier selection is a critical integration decision that affects every swap's cost and reliability.

children.title: Impermanent Loss Mechanics
children.description: Impermanent loss is the opportunity cost an LP pays when the price ratio of their deposited tokens diverges from the deposit ratio; in a concentrated range it is dramatically amplified because the position bears more delta risk per dollar of liquidity. Failing to model IL when designing a yield farming emission schedule can result in net-negative returns for LPs even at high APR.

children.title: Protocol Fee Mechanics
children.description: Uniswap V3 has a governance-controlled protocol fee switch that can redirect a fraction of LP fees to the treasury; building integrations that hardcode the assumption that 100% of fees go to LPs will produce incorrect accounting once the fee switch is enabled. Protocol fee logic must be considered when calculating LP returns or building fee-capture mechanisms.

defi-developer.title: Price Oracles and External Data
defi-developer.description: Integrate price oracles into DeFi contracts for safe, manipulation-resistant on-chain pricing. Compare Chainlink push oracles vs Uniswap TWAP pull oracles.
topics: Choosing oracles for different use cases
defi-developer.challenge: Write a contract that reads a Chainlink price feed and reverts if the data is stale by more than 1 hour, tested on a mainnet Anvil fork.

children.title: AggregatorV3Interface
children.description: Chainlink price feeds expose latestRoundData() which returns price, round ID, and timestamps; using only the price while ignoring the updatedAt timestamp and answeredInRound means your protocol can silently consume stale data during an oracle outage and allow undercollateralized borrows or incorrect liquidations.

children.title: Staleness and Circuit Breaker Checks
children.description: Chainlink feeds can pause during extreme market conditions; a protocol without a staleness guard will continue using the last known price even if it is hours old, enabling attackers to exploit the discrepancy between stale on-chain price and real market price. Every oracle consumer must check both heartbeat and deviation threshold.

children.title: Uniswap V3 TWAP
children.description: Uniswap V3 stores cumulative tick data in observations that can be used to compute a time-weighted average price over any interval; TWAP is manipulation-resistant over long windows because an attacker would need to hold the price off-market for the full averaging period while losing money to arbitrageurs. Choosing too short a TWAP window eliminates this protection.

children.title: Flash Loan Oracle Attacks
children.description: Spot price from an AMM can be manipulated within a single transaction using a flash loan to move the reserve ratio; any DeFi protocol that uses raw AMM spot price (not TWAP) as its oracle has lost funds to this attack vector. The Harvest Finance, Cheese Bank, and Pancake Bunny exploits all stem from spot price oracle reliance.

children.title: Push vs Pull Oracle Models
children.description: Chainlink uses a push model where node operators update feeds when price deviates past a threshold, while Pyth and Chronicle use pull models where users submit signed price attestations on demand; understanding the freshness guarantees of each model is critical for choosing the right oracle for latency-sensitive DeFi applications.

children.title: Chainlink VRF for Randomness
children.description: Chainlink VRF provides cryptographically provable on-chain randomness that cannot be predicted or manipulated by miners or protocol operators; using blockhash or block.timestamp as randomness in a DeFi lottery or NFT drop allows validators to reorder transactions to guarantee winning outcomes. VRF is the only production-grade randomness solution for high-value DeFi.

children.title: Fallback and Multi-Oracle Patterns
children.description: Production DeFi protocols use a primary oracle with a fallback to a secondary source (e.g., Chainlink primary, Uniswap TWAP fallback) to remain operational if one feed pauses; a single oracle dependency is a single point of failure that can halt all protocol operations or enable exploit windows during outages.

children.title: Oracle Decimals and Scaling
children.description: Different Chainlink feeds return prices with different decimal precisions (ETH/USD uses 8 decimals, ETH/ETH uses 18); failing to normalize decimals when combining multiple oracle feeds causes catastrophic mispricing. Every oracle consumer must read the decimals() return value and scale accordingly.

defi-developer.title: Lending and Borrowing Protocols
defi-developer.description: Build a simplified lending protocol with collateral requirements, health factor tracking, and liquidation logic. Learn how Aave and Compound manage risk.

resources.label: Build a Lending Protocol - Smart Contract Programmer

defi-developer.challenge: Build a minimal lending contract where users deposit ETH as collateral and borrow an ERC-20, with a liquidation function that becomes available below 1.2 health factor.

children.title: Health Factor and Collateral Ratios
children.description: The health factor quantifies how close a borrower is to liquidation by comparing their collateral value (adjusted by LTV) to their outstanding debt; a health factor below 1 means the position is undercollateralized and must be liquidated before bad debt accrues to the protocol. Every lending protocol developer must be able to derive the health factor formula without looking it up.

children.title: Interest Rate Models
children.description: Aave and Compound use utilization-based interest rate models where borrowing rates increase steeply past an optimal utilization point (the kink) to incentivize repayment and protect liquidity; failing to understand the kink means you cannot reason about whether your lending market will remain liquid under stress conditions.

children.title: aToken and cToken Share Models
children.description: Aave's aTokens and Compound's cTokens are yield-bearing wrappers that represent a depositor's claim on the underlying pool; the exchange rate between shares and underlying grows over time as interest accrues, and all internal accounting must use shares rather than raw amounts to prevent rounding exploits. Misunderstanding this share model causes accounting errors that accumulate into insolvency over time.

children.title: Liquidation Mechanism
children.description: Liquidations are the safety valve of lending protocols: when a borrower's health factor falls below 1, anyone can repay part of their debt in exchange for their collateral at a discount (the liquidation bonus). A poorly designed liquidation mechanism that makes liquidations unprofitable or allows partial liquidations to worsen health factor can leave protocols with bad debt.

children.title: Bad Debt and Protocol Insolvency
children.description: If collateral value drops faster than liquidators can act (cascade liquidations or oracle failure), a lending protocol accumulates bad debt that must be covered by its treasury or insurance module; designing without a bad debt absorption mechanism puts all depositors at risk of bank run. The CREAM Finance and Euler Finance exploits demonstrate how bad debt cascades into protocol insolvency.

children.title: Supply and Borrow Caps
children.description: Aave V3 introduced per-asset supply and borrow caps to limit protocol exposure to low-liquidity or manipulable assets; without caps, a large deposit of a thinly traded token can be used as collateral to drain liquid assets from the protocol in a price manipulation attack. Cap parameters are one of the most important governance levers in a lending protocol.

resources.label: Aave V3 Supply and Borrow Caps

children.title: Building a Liquidation Bot
children.description: Liquidation bots monitor health factors off-chain and submit liquidation transactions when positions become eligible; a well-designed bot uses flash loans to fund the repayment so no upfront capital is required. Without liquidation bots, unhealthy positions accumulate and the protocol becomes insolvent, so understanding the MEV competition around liquidations is essential.

children.title: MEV and Liquidation Competition
children.description: Liquidations are a primary MEV opportunity: searchers compete to be first to liquidate undercollateralized positions, often using private mempools and block builders to avoid frontrunning. A liquidation bot that broadcasts to the public mempool will frequently be sandwiched or front-run by MEV bots, making it economically unviable.

defi-developer.title: Flash Loans
defi-developer.description: Understand flash loans as atomic, uncollateralized loans used for arbitrage, liquidations, and collateral swaps. Implement a flash loan receiver contract.
defi-developer.challenge: Write a flash loan receiver that borrows USDC from Aave, performs a simulated arbitrage swap, and repays with fee - tested on a mainnet Anvil fork.

children.title: Atomic Transaction Guarantee
children.description: Flash loans are only possible because Ethereum transactions are atomic: if the borrower's callback does not repay the loan plus fee before the transaction ends, the entire transaction reverts as if it never happened. This atomicity is what makes flash loans trustless and uncollateralized, but it also means every operation in the callback must be carefully sequenced.

children.title: Uniswap V3 Flash Swaps
children.description: Uniswap V3 flash swaps let you receive tokens at the start of a callback and repay either the same or the other pool token by the end, enabling single-transaction arbitrage without upfront capital. Forgetting that Uniswap flash swaps require repayment in the correct token and amount causes the callback to revert and the arbitrage to fail.

children.title: Arbitrage Across AMMs
children.description: Flash loans enable capital-free arbitrage: borrow token A, swap to token B on the cheaper DEX, swap back to token A on the more expensive DEX, repay the loan, and keep the spread. Failing to account for the flash loan fee (typically 0.05-0.09%) and both swap fees can turn a seemingly profitable arbitrage into a net loss.

children.title: Collateral Swaps and Self-Liquidation
children.description: Flash loans enable collateral swaps (replacing one collateral token with another in a single transaction) and self-liquidation (closing a near-liquidation position without posting extra capital); both require orchestrating borrow, swap, repay, and re-deposit operations correctly to avoid triggering actual liquidation mid-transaction.

children.title: Flash Loan-Enabled Governance Attacks
children.description: An attacker can borrow a massive amount of governance tokens via flash loan, vote on a malicious proposal in the same transaction, then repay the loan; protocols that use instantaneous token snapshots rather than time-locked voting power are vulnerable. The Beanstalk governance exploit drained $182M using exactly this attack vector.

children.title: Reentrancy via Flash Loan Callbacks
children.description: Flash loan callbacks are external calls that hand execution back to attacker-controlled code mid-transaction; any state that the lending protocol does not lock before the callback is vulnerable to reentrancy reads that return inconsistent values. The Euler Finance hack involved a complex reentrancy through the flash loan callback that allowed minting unbacked tokens.

children.title: Aave vs Uniswap Flash Loan Fees
children.description: Aave charges a flat 0.05% fee on flash loans while Uniswap V3 flash swaps charge the pool's swap fee (0.01%-1%); choosing the wrong provider for a narrow-margin arbitrage strategy can eliminate all profit. For collateral swaps and other non-arbitrage uses, Aave's flash loan is usually cheaper due to its flat fee structure.

children.title: Balancer Free Flash Loans
children.description: Balancer offers zero-fee flash loans as long as the borrowed amount is returned in the same transaction, making it the preferred provider for collateral swaps and self-liquidation where any fee directly reduces user benefit. However, Balancer's flash loan vault must have sufficient liquidity in the requested token, which is not always guaranteed for exotic tokens.

defi-developer.title: Yield Strategies and Vaults
defi-developer.description: Implement ERC-4626 tokenized vault contracts that automate yield strategies. Learn how yield aggregators like Yearn compose multiple protocols.

resources.label: Build a Yield Vault - Smart Contract Programmer

defi-developer.challenge: Build an ERC-4626 vault that deposits underlying tokens into Compound and compounds accrued COMP rewards on each deposit.

children.title: Deposit and Withdrawal Share Math
children.description: ERC-4626 defines a standard interface for yield-bearing vaults where shares are minted proportional to deposited assets and burned on withdrawal; the convertToShares and convertToAssets functions must use correct rounding direction (round down for deposits, round up for withdrawals) to prevent share inflation exploits. Getting the rounding wrong by even 1 wei has been used to drain protocol funds.

children.title: Vault Strategy Composition
children.description: Yield vaults earn returns by deploying deposited assets into one or more underlying protocols (Aave, Compound, Uniswap) and harvesting rewards; the strategy must be callable by anyone (for harvest incentives) while protecting against malicious harvest calls that drain the strategy. Yearn's vault architecture separates the vault token from the strategy to enable upgrades without affecting depositor shares.

children.title: Reward Distribution with Staking Contracts
children.description: Synthetix-style staking contracts distribute token rewards proportionally to stakers using a rewardPerToken accumulator that increments over time; without understanding this accumulator pattern, any modification to the reward math will break the proportional distribution and either overpay or underpay stakers. This pattern is used in virtually every yield farming contract.

children.title: Liquidity Mining Emissions
children.description: Liquidity mining programs distribute governance tokens to LPs as an incentive, but poorly designed emissions schedules create sell pressure that overwhelms demand and collapses the token price, destroying the very incentive being offered. The emission rate, vesting period, and allocation across pools must be modeled as a system to avoid death spirals.

children.title: Auto-Compounding and Harvest Timing
children.description: Auto-compounding vaults call harvest() to sell reward tokens and reinvest into the base strategy, and the optimal harvest frequency depends on gas costs versus compound interest gained; compounding too rarely leaves yield on the table while compounding too often burns it in gas. Most production vaults use keeper networks like Gelato or Chainlink Automation to trigger harvests at optimal times.

children.title: Fee-on-Entry and Fee-on-Exit
children.description: Many yield vaults charge performance fees on harvested yield and management fees on total assets under management; fee accounting must deduct fees before minting shares or after updating totalAssets to avoid undercharging or overcharging depositors. Incorrect fee accounting is one of the most common bugs in DeFi vault contracts.

children.title: Pause and Emergency Withdrawal
children.description: Production yield vaults must include a pause mechanism that halts deposits and harvests without blocking withdrawals, so users can always exit even during an exploit or strategy failure. A pause that also blocks withdrawals is itself a vulnerability: an admin key compromise lets an attacker freeze all funds while they drain the strategy.

children.title: Strategy Migration
children.description: Vaults need to be able to migrate all funds from one yield strategy to another without disrupting depositors, which requires an atomic withdrawal from the old strategy and deposit into the new one with zero loss of principal. A poorly implemented migration that leaves funds in the old strategy or breaks the exchange rate will cause permanent losses for depositors.

defi-developer.title: DeFi Security and Attack Vectors
defi-developer.description: Study the most common DeFi-specific attack vectors including reentrancy, oracle manipulation, governance attacks, and sandwich attacks. Reproduce past exploits in a test environment.
topics: Reading and learning from public audit reports

resources.label: How to Secure Smart Contracts - Trail of Bits

defi-developer.challenge: Reproduce the reentrancy exploit from The DAO hack in a Foundry test, then patch it and show the patched version passes all tests.

children.title: Reentrancy Variants in DeFi
children.description: Cross-function reentrancy occurs when an attacker reenters a different function than the one being called, exploiting shared state that was not yet updated; in DeFi this often manifests through token callbacks (ERC-777, ERC-721 safeTransfer) that allow an attacker to borrow, then reenter and borrow again before the first borrow updates the debt mapping. The single-function reentrancy guard is not sufficient protection against cross-function variants.

children.title: Price Oracle Manipulation
children.description: Protocols that use on-chain spot price as their sole oracle can be exploited by flash-loan-funded reserve manipulation in the same transaction, allowing an attacker to borrow far more than the real collateral value. This attack has been executed successfully against dozens of protocols and is entirely preventable with TWAP or Chainlink oracles.

children.title: Sandwich Attacks
children.description: A sandwich attack front-runs a victim's swap by buying the target token before the victim's transaction and selling immediately after, profiting from the price impact the victim creates; this is particularly damaging for large swaps on low-liquidity pools and is executed automatically by MEV bots watching the mempool. Integrators must enforce minimum amountOut parameters to bound the damage.

children.title: Governance Attacks
children.description: A governance attack uses accumulated voting power (purchased, borrowed, or delegated) to pass malicious proposals that drain the treasury or change protocol parameters for personal gain; the Beanstalk and Build Finance exploits show that even legitimate governance mechanisms can be weaponized if quorum thresholds or time-lock delays are too low. Robust governance requires time-locks, quorum requirements, and vote-weight snapshots at proposal creation.

children.title: Slither Static Analysis
children.description: Slither is a static analysis framework that detects hundreds of Solidity vulnerability patterns including reentrancy, unprotected functions, and incorrect ERC compliance in seconds without running the code; skipping Slither before audit means paying an auditor to find bugs that a free automated tool would have caught. Every DeFi CI pipeline should run Slither on every pull request.

children.title: Echidna Property Fuzzing
children.description: Echidna is a smart contract fuzzer that tests user-defined invariant properties (e.g., totalSupply never exceeds cap, health factor never goes below 1 without liquidation) by generating thousands of random transaction sequences; invariant violations found by Echidna reveal logic bugs that unit tests and static analysis cannot detect. DeFi protocols with invariant tests are significantly less likely to have protocol-breaking logic bugs.

children.title: Reading Public Audit Reports
children.description: Public audit reports from Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, and Code4rena are among the best learning resources available, showing real vulnerabilities found in real protocols along with the exact code and fix; a DeFi developer who has not read at least 10 audit reports is missing the single most efficient way to build a security intuition. Many issues repeat across protocols, so pattern recognition from audits directly prevents future bugs.

children.title: Reproduce Past Exploits
children.description: Reproducing a real DeFi exploit in a Foundry test using a mainnet fork is the most effective way to understand exactly how an attack worked and why the patch fixes it; passively reading postmortems without coding the attack leaves important gaps in understanding the precise sequence of calls required. Developers who can reproduce the DAO hack, Euler, or Curve reentrancy from memory have a fundamentally different security mindset.

defi-developer.title: Mainnet Deployment and Protocol Launch
defi-developer.description: Deploy a production DeFi protocol to Ethereum mainnet with full auditing, monitoring, and a timelocked governance multisig. Build the operational runbook for incident response.
topics: Gnosis Safe multisig for protocol admin
defi-developer.challenge: Deploy your lending protocol to a mainnet Anvil fork, run the full audit checklist, set up a Tenderly alert for health factor breaches, and write an incident response plan.

children.title: Timelock Controller
children.description: A timelock controller enforces a mandatory delay between when a governance action is proposed and when it can be executed, giving users time to exit the protocol if they disagree with a parameter change or contract upgrade. A DeFi protocol with an instant admin key and no timelock is a single private key compromise away from total fund loss.

children.title: Gnosis Safe Multisig
children.description: A Gnosis Safe multisig requires M-of-N signers to approve any transaction, eliminating single-key risk for protocol admin operations like upgrading contracts, changing fee parameters, or withdrawing from a treasury. Any DeFi protocol where a single team member's compromised key could drain all funds has failed at basic operational security.

children.title: Pre-Deployment Audit Checklist
children.description: A systematic pre-deployment audit checklist covers access control, reentrancy, oracle integration, integer math, upgrade safety, and event emission; skipping any category is how protocols ship with known vulnerability classes that auditors and attackers both recognize. The checklist should be run against every contract change before any mainnet deployment.

children.title: Immunefi Bug Bounty Program
children.description: Immunefi is the primary bug bounty platform for DeFi protocols, offering structured severity tiers (critical up to $10M+) that incentivize whitehats to responsibly disclose vulnerabilities instead of exploiting them; a protocol without a bug bounty program provides no incentive for researchers to report rather than exploit. Launching without a bug bounty leaves a known attack surface with no structured response path.

children.title: Tenderly Alerts and Monitoring
children.description: Tenderly allows setting up real-time alerts on contract events, failed transactions, and balance thresholds, giving protocol teams immediate notification when something goes wrong in production. A protocol without monitoring can take hours to detect an ongoing exploit, during which the attacker drains more funds and users cannot be warned.

children.title: Incident Response Plan
children.description: A written incident response plan defines who has authority to pause the protocol, how to communicate with users during an exploit, and what the recovery path is; protocols that improvise their incident response during an active exploit make poor decisions under pressure that often worsen losses. The plan should be rehearsed with simulated exploit scenarios before mainnet launch.

children.title: On-Chain Governance with Governor
children.description: OpenZeppelin Governor provides a battle-tested on-chain governance system with proposal lifecycle, voting periods, quorum thresholds, and timelock integration; rolling custom governance from scratch has produced several critical vulnerabilities including the Beanstalk $182M exploit. Using Governor with conservative parameters (long voting periods, high quorum) is far safer than custom implementations.

children.title: Transparent vs UUPS Proxy Upgrades
children.description: Upgradeable proxies allow fixing bugs post-deployment but introduce storage collision risk and centralization; the UUPS pattern puts upgrade logic in the implementation and is cheaper to deploy, while the Transparent pattern puts it in the proxy and prevents function selector clashes. Choosing the wrong proxy pattern or leaving the upgrade function unprotected has resulted in permanent protocol compromises.

nft-developer.title: Web and Blockchain Basics for NFT Developers
nft-developer.description: Understand the basics of how blockchains work and what makes NFTs unique compared to fungible tokens. Learn about digital ownership, provenance, and on-chain vs off-chain data.
nft-developer.challenge: Write a plain description of how owning an NFT differs from owning a JPEG file on your hard drive, referencing on-chain ownership records.

children.title: Blockchain Provenance
children.description: On-chain provenance is the defining value proposition of NFTs — every transfer is immutably recorded, so ownership history is publicly verifiable without trusting a central authority. If you misunderstand this, you will build systems where token metadata lives off-chain in mutable storage, silently breaking the ownership guarantee users expect.

children.title: Fungible vs Non-Fungible Tokens
children.description: Fungible tokens are interchangeable units of value; non-fungible tokens carry a unique identity encoded in a tokenId that cannot be replicated. Conflating the two leads to incorrect contract architecture — for example, using an ERC-20 to represent artwork, which loses per-token metadata and transfer hooks entirely.

children.title: EVM Gas Model for NFTs
children.description: NFT mints are expensive because they write new ownership mappings and emit events to the blockchain. Understanding gas lets you design efficient storage layouts and batch operations — ignoring it results in contracts that cost users hundreds of dollars to mint a single token.

children.title: Wallets and Address Ownership
children.description: Every NFT is owned by an address, not a person — knowing how wallets derive addresses from private keys clarifies why losing a seed phrase means losing all NFTs permanently. Without this understanding, developers build recovery flows that are impossible to fulfill or make incorrect assumptions about multi-sig custody.

children.title: Marketplaces and Listing Mechanics
children.description: OpenSea and competing marketplaces read tokenURI, royaltyInfo, and collection-level metadata to render listings — if your contract omits these or returns malformed data, your NFTs will appear broken or invisible to buyers. Understanding marketplace integration requirements before writing a single line of Solidity saves costly redeployments.

children.title: Royalties Overview
children.description: Creator royalties are a percentage of secondary sale price routed back to the original minter — they are the primary long-term revenue model for NFT projects. Developers who treat royalties as an afterthought ship contracts that cannot enforce them on-chain, leaving creators reliant on voluntary marketplace compliance.

children.title: Remix IDE for Rapid Prototyping
children.description: Remix lets you write, compile, and deploy Solidity contracts in a browser with zero local setup — it is the fastest way to test ERC-721 fundamentals before committing to a Hardhat or Foundry project structure. Skipping this environment means spending hours on toolchain setup before you have written a single mint function.

children.title: OpenZeppelin Contracts Library
children.description: OpenZeppelin provides audited, battle-tested base contracts for ERC-721 and ERC-1155 that implement all mandatory interface functions correctly. Writing these from scratch without understanding the standard almost always introduces subtle approval or reentrancy bugs that only surface under adversarial conditions.

nft-developer.title: Solidity and ERC-721 Standard
nft-developer.description: Study the ERC-721 standard interface and implement a basic NFT contract using OpenZeppelin. Understand the tokenId model, ownership mapping, and approval mechanics.
nft-developer.challenge: Deploy an ERC-721 contract on Remix that mints tokens with sequential IDs and stores a custom name for each token.

children.title: ownerOf and balanceOf Semantics
children.description: ownerOf reverts on non-existent tokens while balanceOf returns a count — confusing these two causes off-by-one errors when enumerating a wallet's holdings and silent failures when checking ownership in access-controlled functions. Every marketplace and wallet relies on these two functions to display NFT state correctly.

children.title: Approval and Transfer Mechanics
children.description: ERC-721 has two approval levels — per-token approve and collection-wide setApprovalForAll — and any marketplace listing relies on the latter to move tokens on behalf of sellers. Misunderstanding the approval model leads to contracts that silently block marketplace sales or expose owners to unexpected third-party transfers.

children.title: safeMint vs mint
children.description: safeMint calls onERC721Received on the recipient if it is a contract, preventing tokens from being permanently locked in contracts that cannot handle them. Using plain mint to contracts is a silent token-burning bug that has cost real projects irretrievable NFTs.

children.title: tokenURI and Base URI Pattern
children.description: tokenURI is the only link between a token and its metadata — if it returns an empty string or a broken IPFS path, every marketplace will show a blank image. Implementing the base URI pattern lets you update the metadata prefix before a reveal without redeploying the entire contract.

children.title: Hardhat Project Setup
children.description: Hardhat provides a local Ethereum network, test runner, and deployment scripts in one tool — without it, testing NFT mint logic requires deploying to a public testnet, which is slow and consumes faucet ETH. Getting the project structure right before writing NFT logic prevents painful refactors later.

children.title: ABI and Contract Interaction
children.description: The ABI is the interface contract between your Solidity code and any JavaScript or TypeScript frontend — if the ABI is stale or mismatched after a recompile, every frontend call will silently fail or revert with a cryptic error. Understanding how Hardhat generates and exports ABIs eliminates an entire class of integration bugs.

children.title: Unit Testing with Chai and ethers.js
children.description: NFT contracts handle money and ownership — untested edge cases like minting to the zero address or exceeding supply caps have caused millions of dollars in losses on mainnet. Writing tests before deployment is the only reliable way to verify that every state transition behaves as designed.

children.title: Etherscan Contract Verification
children.description: Verifying source code on Etherscan makes your contract auditable by buyers and security researchers, which is a baseline trust signal for any NFT project. Unverified contracts are treated as suspicious by major marketplaces and increasingly blocked by wallet security warnings.

nft-developer.title: NFT Metadata and IPFS
nft-developer.description: Create and pin NFT metadata JSON files to IPFS and reference them in your contract via tokenURI. Understand the metadata standard used by OpenSea and other marketplaces.
topics: Uploading images to IPFS via Pinata
nft-developer.challenge: Upload 5 images and 5 metadata JSON files to IPFS, then deploy an NFT contract where each token correctly resolves to its metadata via tokenURI.

children.title: Content Addressing and CIDs
children.description: IPFS addresses content by its cryptographic hash rather than its location, meaning a CID permanently and uniquely identifies specific bytes — if the content changes, the CID changes. NFT projects that store metadata at centralized URLs can silently alter artwork after minting; content-addressed IPFS prevents this rug-pull vector.

children.title: Pinning with Pinata
children.description: Unpinned IPFS content is garbage-collected by nodes over time — if no one pins your NFT metadata, it disappears and every tokenURI resolves to nothing. Pinata and similar services provide persistent pinning so metadata and images remain accessible as long as the project is funded.

children.title: OpenSea Metadata Standard
children.description: OpenSea defines the de-facto metadata schema including name, description, image, and attributes fields — deviating from this schema causes traits to not appear in marketplace filters, which dramatically reduces an NFT's discoverability and perceived value. Every metadata file must validate against this schema before pinning.

children.title: Base URI vs Per-Token URI
children.description: The base URI pattern stores a common prefix in the contract and appends the tokenId, reducing deployment gas and enabling a pre-reveal placeholder by pointing the base at a single hidden image. Per-token URI storage is more flexible but costs significantly more gas per mint and cannot be batch-updated before reveal.

children.title: IPFS vs Arweave vs Centralized Servers
children.description: Each storage layer offers a different durability and cost profile — centralized servers are cheap but mutable and can go offline, IPFS is immutable but requires paid pinning, and Arweave offers permanent storage for a one-time fee. Choosing the wrong layer for your use case either undermines the NFT's trust model or creates unsustainable operational costs.

children.title: IPFS Gateway Access
children.description: IPFS content is accessed by browsers through HTTP gateways — using a single public gateway like ipfs.io creates a single point of failure for your entire collection's images. Production NFT projects use dedicated gateways or multiple fallback URLs so metadata remains visible even when public gateways are overloaded.

children.title: Pre-Reveal Placeholder Pattern
children.description: Revealing all metadata before mint ends lets buyers cherry-pick rare tokens, which destroys fairness for the rest of the community. The placeholder pattern points tokenURI at a hidden image until minting closes, then switches the base URI in a single transaction to reveal all tokens simultaneously.

children.title: Freezing Metadata on OpenSea
children.description: OpenSea supports a metadata freeze mechanism triggered by emitting a PermanentURI event, signaling to buyers that artwork can never be altered — omitting this signal leaves buyers uncertain about mutability even if you never intend to change anything. It is one of the cheapest trust signals a project can provide.

nft-developer.title: NFT Minting Mechanics
nft-developer.description: Build NFT minting contracts with supply caps, allowlists using Merkle proofs, and public sale phases. Implement per-wallet mint limits and reveal mechanics.
nft-developer.challenge: Build an NFT contract with a 1,000-token supply cap, an allowlist of 100 wallets using a Merkle root, and a 2-per-wallet public mint limit.

children.title: Max Supply and Counter Patterns
children.description: A supply cap enforced on-chain is the only reliable way to guarantee scarcity — off-chain checks can be bypassed by calling the contract directly. Using OpenZeppelin Counters or a simple uint256 with a require guard ensures the cap is enforced atomically within every mint transaction.

children.title: Sale Phases with Booleans and Enums
children.description: Most collections run at minimum two phases — a presale for allowlisted wallets and a public sale — and mixing them up allows public buyers to bypass the allowlist or drains the presale allocation before community members can mint. Encoding phase state as an enum makes transitions explicit and auditable.

children.title: Merkle Proof Allowlists
children.description: Storing thousands of allowlisted addresses on-chain costs enormous gas; a Merkle root stores the entire list as a single 32-byte hash and each user proves membership with a compact proof at mint time. Getting the leaf encoding wrong — for example, hashing a single address instead of double-hashing it — breaks all proofs silently.

children.title: Per-Wallet Mint Limits
children.description: Without per-wallet limits, a single address can deplete the entire supply at once, which is economically rational for bots but fatal for community collections. A mapping from address to mint count enforced with a require check is the simplest and most gas-efficient solution.

children.title: Secure ETH Withdrawal Pattern
children.description: Mint revenue accumulates in the contract and must be withdrawn by the owner — using transfer or send is dangerous because they forward only 2300 gas and fail against smart contract recipients. The pull-payment pattern with call and a reentrancy guard is the safe standard, and its absence has drained dozens of NFT treasuries.

children.title: Payment Splitter for Teams
children.description: Most NFT projects have multiple stakeholders — artists, developers, community funds — who each deserve a share of mint revenue. Hardcoding withdrawal logic for multiple recipients is error-prone and immutable; OpenZeppelin's PaymentSplitter handles proportional distribution and is extensible if shares need to change.

children.title: Checks-Effects-Interactions in NFT Minting
children.description: safeMint calls the recipient contract's onERC721Received hook before updating internal counters in some implementations, creating a reentrancy window where a malicious contract can call mint again before the supply counter increments. Applying the checks-effects-interactions pattern and a ReentrancyGuard closes this window.

children.title: Merkle Proof Front-Running
children.description: Merkle proofs are public once submitted to the mempool, so an attacker watching the mempool can copy a proof and submit a higher-gas transaction to steal the allowlist slot. Binding the proof leaf to the caller's address prevents this attack at essentially zero added cost.

nft-developer.title: ERC-1155 Multi-Token Standard
nft-developer.description: Learn ERC-1155 for efficient multi-token contracts that support both fungible and non-fungible tokens in a single deployment. Build a game item contract as a practical example.
nft-developer.challenge: Build an ERC-1155 contract representing 3 types of game items with different max supplies, and batch mint a starter pack of all 3 to a test address.

children.title: Multi-Token Contract Efficiency
children.description: ERC-1155 stores all token balances in a single nested mapping keyed by both address and tokenId, which means one contract can replace dozens of separate ERC-721 deployments — cutting deployment cost and simplifying frontend integration. Game projects that deploy per-item ERC-721 contracts face exponential operational complexity as their item catalog grows.

children.title: Batch Mint and Transfer
children.description: ERC-1155's mintBatch and safeBatchTransferFrom functions move multiple token types in a single transaction, which is the primary gas advantage over ERC-721. Missing batch support forces users into multiple sequential transactions for operations like distributing starter packs, which multiplies gas costs linearly with pack size.

children.title: Fungible and Non-Fungible in One Contract
children.description: ERC-1155 supports token ids with supply greater than one — making them fungible — alongside token ids with supply of exactly one — making them unique. Designing a game economy with both currency (fungible) and equipment (semi-fungible) in the same contract gives users a single approval to manage all asset types, dramatically improving UX.

children.title: URI Template with ID Substitution
children.description: ERC-1155 uses a single URI function that returns a template string where a client replaces the literal {id} placeholder with the hex-encoded tokenId — if your URI does not include this placeholder or uses the wrong format, every client will request the wrong metadata URL. The spec requires lowercase hex zero-padded to 64 characters.

children.title: Choosing the Right Standard
children.description: ERC-721 is preferable for unique collectibles where each token needs a distinct identity and history; ERC-1155 is better when multiple copies of the same item exist or when gas cost per token matters. Choosing the wrong standard forces either a costly redeployment or architectural workarounds that confuse marketplaces.

children.title: Marketplace Support for ERC-1155
children.description: Major marketplaces support ERC-1155 but with differences — OpenSea treats each unique tokenId as a separate listing and shows edition counts, while some aggregators only partially index batch transfers. Testing your ERC-1155 contract on a testnet marketplace before mainnet launch reveals display issues while they are still free to fix.

children.title: setApprovalForAll Security Model
children.description: ERC-1155's setApprovalForAll grants an operator unlimited control over all of a wallet's tokens across every tokenId in the contract — this is a powerful trust grant that phishing attacks routinely exploit. Developers must warn users clearly in the UI when a marketplace requests this approval and never request it unnecessarily from within the contract itself.

children.title: ERC-1155 Receiver Hook
children.description: Like ERC-721, transferring ERC-1155 tokens to a smart contract requires that contract to implement onERC1155Received and onERC1155BatchReceived — without these, the transfer reverts. This is a frequent source of confusion when integrating ERC-1155 into game vaults or escrow contracts.

nft-developer.title: NFT Royalties and Marketplace Integration
nft-developer.description: Implement on-chain royalty standards using ERC-2981 and understand how NFT marketplaces enforce or bypass royalties. Learn about creator fee policies across platforms.
topics: On-chain royalty vs off-chain policy debate
nft-developer.challenge: Add ERC-2981 to your NFT contract with a 5% royalty, write a test asserting the correct royalty amount for any sale price, and verify it on a testnet marketplace.

children.title: royaltyInfo Function
children.description: ERC-2981 exposes a single royaltyInfo(tokenId, salePrice) function that returns the receiver address and royalty amount — any marketplace that respects the standard calls this before completing a sale and routes the fee automatically. Implementing it incorrectly, for example returning a percentage instead of a calculated amount, means the marketplace silently pays zero royalties.

children.title: Basis Points Math
children.description: Royalties are expressed in basis points — 1 BP is 0.01% — so a 5% royalty is 500 BP. Using percentages directly in Solidity integer math produces truncation errors at small sale prices; basis points preserve two decimal places of precision without any floating-point arithmetic.

children.title: OpenSea Operator Filter Registry
children.description: OpenSea introduced the Operator Filter Registry to block marketplaces that bypass royalties from transferring tokens — integrating it makes your contract refuse transfers initiated by non-compliant platforms. The filter is optional and controversial, but understanding how it works is essential to making an informed choice about royalty enforcement.

children.title: Royalty Recipient Splitting
children.description: A project's royalty recipient is often a team of multiple stakeholders — pointing royaltyInfo at a PaymentSplitter or a protocol like 0xSplits allows automatic proportional distribution without manual accounting. Pointing it at a single EOA creates a trust dependency on one team member and complicates future contributor agreements.

children.title: Testnet Marketplace Verification
children.description: OpenSea's testnet environment on Sepolia renders NFT metadata and validates royalty configurations before you spend mainnet gas — discovering a missing supportsInterface declaration or malformed metadata on testnet costs nothing, while fixing it on mainnet requires a new deployment. Every project should verify its contract renders correctly on the testnet marketplace.

children.title: supportsInterface and ERC-165
children.description: ERC-165 lets marketplaces query whether a contract supports ERC-721, ERC-2981, or other interfaces at runtime — if your contract does not override supportsInterface to declare all implemented interfaces, marketplaces will skip royalty calls and metadata resolution even if the functions are present. This is a silent integration failure that no error message reports.

children.title: Protocol-Level Enforcement
children.description: On-chain enforcement via transfer hooks is the only way to guarantee royalties regardless of which marketplace executes the sale — off-chain agreements rely on marketplace compliance, which has historically been inconsistent across platforms. However, transfer hooks add gas overhead and can break composability with DeFi protocols that move NFTs programmatically.

children.title: NFT License and Off-Chain Rights
children.description: An NFT token represents on-chain ownership but does not automatically grant intellectual property rights to the artwork — the license terms must be explicitly defined either in metadata or a public contract. Projects that omit license terms face legal ambiguity that chills secondary market activity and creator collabs.

resources.label: a16z Can't Be Evil NFT Licenses

nft-developer.title: On-Chain Generative NFTs and SVG Art
nft-developer.description: Generate NFT artwork entirely on-chain using Solidity-produced SVGs encoded as base64 data URIs. Understand the tradeoffs between on-chain and off-chain storage for art.
nft-developer.challenge: Build a fully on-chain NFT that generates a unique SVG based on the tokenId, with at least 3 randomized visual attributes stored in the token URI.

children.title: SVG Strings in Solidity
children.description: Solidity can concatenate SVG markup strings and return them as part of a data URI — making the artwork fully on-chain and immune to IPFS pin expiry or server downtime. If string concatenation is done naively with many short strings, it creates excessive memory allocation and unpredictable gas costs that can push mints past the block gas limit.

children.title: Base64 Encoding for Data URIs
children.description: tokenURI must return a valid URI — for on-chain NFTs that means a data URI where the JSON is base64-encoded, because raw JSON in a URI breaks many clients. OpenZeppelin's Base64 library handles the encoding in pure Solidity, but every additional character of on-chain data adds gas, so minimizing SVG verbosity is critical.

children.title: blockhash Randomness Limitations
children.description: Using blockhash as a randomness source is predictable by miners and validators who can see the outcome before confirming a block — for low-value trait assignment it is acceptable, but for rare-trait selection or prize mechanics it can be gamed to guarantee desired outcomes. Any significant value attached to randomness requires Chainlink VRF.

children.title: Chainlink VRF for Verifiable Randomness
children.description: Chainlink VRF provides cryptographically provable randomness that cannot be predicted or manipulated by any party including Chainlink itself — it is the industry standard for fair trait assignment in generative NFT projects. The two-transaction model (request then fulfill) requires designing reveal flows around asynchronous callbacks.

children.title: Trait Rarity Engineering
children.description: Rarity tables define how likely each trait value is to appear — poorly balanced tables produce collections where 90% of tokens look identical and only a handful are genuinely rare, collapsing secondary market prices for common tokens. Designing rarity curves with weighted random selection and testing the distribution before mint is as important as the artwork itself.

children.title: Off-Chain Generative Pipelines
children.description: Most generative collections generate all artwork and metadata off-chain before mint using tools like HashLips — pre-generating and validating the entire output set lets you catch duplicate combinations, broken trait files, or rarity imbalances before any token is minted. Generating on-the-fly during minting risks running out of valid combinations or creating accidental duplicates.

children.title: Dynamic NFTs that Change State
children.description: Some NFTs are designed to evolve — changing their metadata based on game outcomes, time elapsed, or real-world data feeds. Implementing this requires the contract owner or an oracle to update state, which means the NFT is not immutable; this tradeoff must be communicated clearly to holders who may value permanence.

children.title: Gas Cost of On-Chain Data
children.description: Every byte stored in contract storage costs 20,000 gas for the first write — a fully on-chain 10,000-piece collection with rich traits can cost more in storage gas than the entire mint revenue. Profiling gas usage per token before deployment is mandatory; many on-chain art projects optimize by storing trait indices and rendering SVG from a lookup table.

nft-developer.title: NFT Frontend with React and ethers.js
nft-developer.description: Build a minting website that connects to MetaMask, displays collection info, and handles mint transactions with proper feedback. Fetch and render NFT metadata from IPFS.
nft-developer.challenge: Build a minting page that shows total minted vs supply, lets users mint 1-5 NFTs, and displays a gallery of their owned tokens fetched from the contract.

children.title: Permanence and Trust Trade-offs
children.description: On-chain metadata is immutable and requires no external infrastructure — it is the gold standard for long-term collectible value. Off-chain metadata is cheaper and supports richer media but creates a dependency on IPFS pinning or a server that could fail after the team disbands; every project must communicate this trade-off explicitly to buyers.

children.title: Hybrid Metadata Architectures
children.description: A hybrid approach stores immutable core data like name and tokenId on-chain while delegating high-resolution images to Arweave for permanent off-chain storage — combining guaranteed availability with reduced gas costs. The key requirement is that every component of the metadata has clearly defined durability guarantees documented for holders.

children.title: EIP-5114 and Non-Transferable NFTs
children.description: Soulbound tokens are NFTs that cannot be transferred after minting — they represent credentials, memberships, or achievements tied to an identity rather than tradable assets. Implementing non-transferability requires overriding transferFrom and safeTransferFrom to revert, and understanding this breaks all marketplace listings.

children.title: NFT Utility Beyond Collectibles
children.description: NFTs are increasingly used as access passes, event tickets, voting rights, and on-chain credentials — treating them purely as art collectibles misses a rapidly growing design space. Token-gating libraries like TokenProof and Lit Protocol read NFT ownership to unlock content, services, or governance rights without any custom backend.

children.title: Token-Gating with ownerOf
children.description: Token-gating calls ownerOf on-chain or via an RPC call to verify that a wallet holds a qualifying token before granting access — this is stateless authorization that requires no registration and is impossible to forge. The common mistake is checking ownerOf in a frontend only, which a sophisticated user can bypass by forging the response.

children.title: EIP-1155 Membership Tokens
children.description: ERC-1155 tokens with supply greater than one are ideal for tiered membership systems — a Bronze/Silver/Gold pass can be a single contract with three tokenIds each having different supply caps and privileges. Implementing tiered access requires checking balanceOf for the specific tokenId rather than ownerOf, which only works for ERC-721.

children.title: ENS and NFT Identity
children.description: ENS names can have a primary NFT avatar that surfaces across wallets, dApps, and marketplaces — integrating ENS resolution into your frontend lets you display human-readable identities instead of raw addresses, which dramatically improves social UX in NFT-centric applications.

children.title: Verifiable Credentials on NFTs
children.description: Combining W3C Verifiable Credentials with on-chain NFTs creates a privacy-preserving credential system where users can prove a claim without exposing all their on-chain history. This pattern underpins emerging identity protocols and is the foundation for compliant on-chain KYC without custodial data collection.

nft-developer.title: NFT Contract Security and Testing
nft-developer.description: Audit your NFT contracts for common vulnerabilities including reentrancy on mint, supply manipulation, and metadata override attacks. Write comprehensive test suites.
topics: Supply cap bypass via batch mint
Fuzz testing mint and transfer functions
nft-developer.challenge: Run Slither and a 50,000-run Foundry fuzz test on your minting contract, fix all findings, and document each vulnerability and its fix.

children.title: Bridge Architecture and Risks
children.description: Cross-chain NFT bridges lock the original token on one chain and mint a wrapped representation on another — any vulnerability in the bridge contract can allow attackers to mint unlimited wrapped tokens or drain the locked originals. The Ronin bridge hack ($625M) and Wormhole exploit ($320M) both exploited bridge validation logic, making bridge security the most critical topic in cross-chain development.

children.title: Lock-and-Mint vs Burn-and-Mint
children.description: Lock-and-mint preserves the original NFT on the source chain while a wrapped copy exists on the destination — useful when the original has established provenance but creates a custody risk. Burn-and-mint permanently moves the NFT and is simpler to reason about, but requires full trust in the bridge to mint an equivalent token on the destination chain.

children.title: Deploying NFTs on Polygon and Arbitrum
children.description: L2 networks like Polygon and Arbitrum reduce mint gas costs by 10-100x compared to Ethereum mainnet, making large collections economically viable for users — but L2 NFTs have different finality assumptions and require users to bridge ETH or hold the L2 native token. Choosing the wrong L2 for your audience can strand users who cannot afford the bridge.

children.title: Chain Abstraction and Multi-Chain UX
children.description: Users should not need to know which chain an NFT lives on — chain abstraction layers let wallets and dApps present a unified view across L1 and L2 deployments, handling bridging and gas switching transparently. Building chain-aware UX without abstraction creates confusion that dramatically increases user drop-off rates during mint.

children.title: LayerZero ONFT Standard
children.description: LayerZero's ONFT standard extends ERC-721 with send and receive functions that route messages through its oracle-relayer network, enabling an NFT to exist natively on multiple chains without a custodian. The ONFT gas estimation model requires paying source-chain fees for destination-chain execution, which catches many developers off guard during integration.

children.title: Chainlink CCIP for NFT Transfers
children.description: Chainlink CCIP provides a secure cross-chain messaging protocol backed by the Chainlink DON's security model — using it for NFT transfers provides stronger security guarantees than most third-party bridges at the cost of higher fees. For high-value NFTs where security is worth the premium, CCIP is the recommended approach.

children.title: Unified Collection View Across Chains
children.description: When an NFT collection spans multiple chains, aggregating ownership data requires querying multiple RPC endpoints and reconciling results — tools like Alchemy's NFT API and Moralis abstract this into a single query. Without aggregation, building a gallery or holder dashboard requires maintaining chain-specific RPC clients and merging results manually.

children.title: Provenance Across Chains
children.description: A cross-chain NFT's provenance trail splits at the moment of bridging — the destination chain records a fresh mint event rather than a transfer from the original collection. Communicating this clearly in metadata and marketplace descriptions prevents buyers from being misled about the token's origin chain history.

nft-developer.title: Mainnet Launch and Collection Operations
nft-developer.description: Launch an NFT collection on Ethereum mainnet with a verified contract, marketplace listings, and an admin multisig for revenue withdrawals. Set up post-launch monitoring.
topics: Gnosis Safe for team fund management
nft-developer.challenge: Deploy and verify your NFT contract on mainnet, set up the OpenSea collection page, and configure a Tenderly webhook alert for any mint that fails.

children.title: Minting Site with wagmi and RainbowKit
children.description: A minting site is often the first and only touchpoint between a project and its buyers — slow load times, wallet connection failures, or broken mint buttons during a high-traffic launch can permanently damage a project's reputation. Using wagmi and RainbowKit provides a production-ready wallet layer that handles the edge cases most custom implementations miss.

children.title: NFT Gallery with Metadata Fetching
children.description: Fetching and displaying all tokens in a collection requires resolving each tokenURI, fetching the JSON from IPFS, and loading the image — doing this naively with sequential fetches for a 10,000-piece collection takes minutes. Using indexed APIs like Alchemy's getNFTsForOwner or The Graph makes gallery loading near-instant without hammering IPFS gateways.

children.title: Reveal Transaction Timing
children.description: The reveal transaction sets the final base URI on-chain and must happen atomically at a predetermined time — delaying it allows speculation on the final metadata order, while doing it before mint ends leaks rarity information to late buyers. Pre-staging the IPFS content and pre-signing the reveal transaction lets you execute it in a single block with no downtime.

children.title: Collection Setup on OpenSea
children.description: OpenSea automatically indexes NFTs by reading tokenURI and ERC-2981 data, but a verified collection page with a banner, description, and explicit royalty settings dramatically improves discoverability and secondary market volume. Projects that skip collection setup lose out on OpenSea's organic search traffic and appear unverified to buyers.

children.title: Tenderly Mint Event Monitoring
children.description: Tenderly can watch for every Transfer event from your NFT contract and alert the team via webhook within seconds of an on-chain event — without monitoring, a reentrancy attack or supply manipulation goes undetected until a buyer reports it publicly. Setting up alerts before launch is a non-negotiable part of a responsible NFT release.

children.title: Gnosis Safe for Team Funds
children.description: Mint revenue flowing to a single EOA creates a catastrophic single point of failure — if that key is compromised, the entire treasury is drained instantly. A Gnosis Safe multisig requiring multiple team member signatures to execute withdrawals provides both security and accountability for project funds.

children.title: Slither Static Analysis for NFTs
children.description: Slither detects NFT-specific issues including missing return values on safeTransferFrom, unchecked mint limits, and dangerous approve patterns — running it before any mainnet deployment is a mandatory minimum security step. High severity Slither findings left unaddressed have been the root cause of several NFT exploits that drained thousands of ETH.

children.title: Foundry Fuzz Testing Mint Functions
children.description: Fuzz testing mint and transfer functions with random inputs over tens of thousands of iterations surfaces edge cases that deterministic unit tests never reach — for example, a supply cap bypass when tokenId wraps around a uint256 overflow. Running forge test with high fuzz iterations before launch is the most cost-effective security investment available.

web3-fullstack.title: Web and Blockchain Fundamentals
web3-fullstack.description: Understand the full-stack architecture of a dApp, connecting smart contracts on-chain to a web frontend and an off-chain indexing layer. Learn why each layer exists.
topics: Role of smart contracts as backend
What an indexer does and why it is needed
Next.js vs plain React for dApps
IPFS and Arweave for off-chain storage

resources.label: Web3 Full Stack Overview - Nader Dabit

web3-fullstack.challenge: Draw a labeled architecture diagram for a full-stack dApp showing smart contract, frontend, indexer, and wallet interaction layers.

children.title: Decentralized vs Traditional Architecture
children.description: In traditional apps a central server holds state and enforces rules; in a dApp the smart contract IS the backend, running on every node. Misunderstanding this boundary leads developers to trust the frontend for security, leaving contracts exposed.

resources.label: Web3 Full Stack Overview - Nader Dabit

children.title: JSON-RPC and WebSocket Providers
children.description: Every frontend interaction with the chain goes through a JSON-RPC provider. Choosing HTTP vs WebSocket matters: HTTP is stateless and simple, but WebSocket is required for real-time event subscriptions. Ignoring this causes missed events and stale UI state.

children.title: IPFS and Arweave for Off-Chain Data
children.description: Smart contracts cannot cheaply store large blobs; IPFS and Arweave offload that data while the chain stores only a content hash or URI. Linking to a mutable server instead of a content-addressed system destroys immutability guarantees and enables rug-pull metadata swaps.

children.title: Why Indexers Exist
children.description: The EVM exposes no efficient query API — you cannot ask a node for all Transfer events by address without scanning every block. Indexers like The Graph replay events into a queryable database, making rich UIs possible. Without them, dApps are either slow or they centralise data in their own databases.

children.title: Next.js vs Plain React for dApps
children.description: Next.js adds server-side rendering and API routes that plain React lacks. API routes let you proxy RPC calls to hide API keys, and SSR enables fast initial loads with on-chain data. Choosing plain React forces you to expose provider keys in the client bundle.

children.title: Wallet Interaction Model
children.description: Every user-initiated chain write flows through a wallet that signs the transaction. Understanding the distinction between a read-only provider (public client) and a signing wallet client prevents you from building UIs that silently fail when no wallet is connected.

children.title: Smart Contracts as Immutable APIs
children.description: Unlike a REST API you can redeploy, a non-upgradeable contract is permanent. Every function signature and storage layout you ship becomes a public commitment. Failing to design with this immutability in mind leads to locked funds and forced re-deployments with expensive data migrations.

children.title: Gas as a First-Class Constraint
children.description: Every EVM operation has a gas cost that limits what contracts can do and determines transaction fees. Developers who ignore gas constraints ship contracts that users cannot afford to call on mainnet, or that exceed block gas limits entirely on certain code paths.

web3-fullstack.title: Solidity Smart Contract Development
web3-fullstack.description: Write production-quality Solidity contracts using OpenZeppelin base classes and best practices. Understand events as the primary data emission mechanism for indexers.
web3-fullstack.challenge: Write a marketplace contract that emits a structured event for every listing and sale, with all indexed fields needed for efficient querying.

children.title: Events and Indexed Parameters
children.description: Events are the primary mechanism by which off-chain systems (subgraphs, frontends) learn what happened on-chain. Indexed parameters create Bloom filter topics for efficient log searching. Omitting indexed fields on high-cardinality values like addresses forces subgraph handlers to scan every log, crushing performance.

children.title: Custom Errors for Gas Efficiency
children.description: Custom errors (introduced in Solidity 0.8.4) encode only a 4-byte selector on revert instead of a full string, saving significant gas on every failed transaction. Sticking with string revert messages in high-traffic contracts wastes user funds on every rejection.

children.title: OpenZeppelin AccessControl vs Ownable
children.description: Ownable gives a single admin address all power, which is a centralisation risk. AccessControl provides granular roles (MINTER_ROLE, PAUSER_ROLE) so least-privilege can be enforced. Choosing Ownable for a production protocol creates a single point of compromise that attackers actively target.

children.title: NatSpec Documentation
children.description: NatSpec comments generate ABI-level documentation that wallets display to users during signing prompts. Without NatSpec, MetaMask shows raw function selectors, eroding user trust and making phishing attacks harder to distinguish from legitimate interactions.

children.title: Contract Interfaces for Composability
children.description: Defining a clean interface (IERC20, IMarketplace) decouples your contract logic from your frontend ABI and from other contracts that may integrate with you. Without a stable interface, any internal refactor breaks all callers and forces coordinated multi-contract redeployments.

children.title: Storage Layout Awareness
children.description: EVM storage slots are fixed at compile time; packing multiple values into one slot saves gas, but misaligned packing or unintended overlap causes silent data corruption. In upgradeable contracts, adding storage variables in the wrong order corrupts the proxy's slot mapping irreversibly.

children.title: Structuring Events for Subgraph Indexing
children.description: Subgraph handlers fire once per event, so every piece of data a UI will need must be emitted in that event or derivable from it. Emitting sparse events means the subgraph must make expensive eth_call lookups during indexing, dramatically slowing sync times after contract upgrades.

children.title: Visibility Modifiers and Attack Surface
children.description: Every public and external function is callable by any account on mainnet. Marking functions internal or private when they are only used internally reduces attack surface. Developers who default all functions to public ship contracts with unintended entry points that become exploit vectors.

web3-fullstack.title: Hardhat - Contract Testing and Deployment
web3-fullstack.description: Use Hardhat to write tests, run a local node, and deploy contracts to testnets. Build reusable deploy scripts that track deployed addresses per network.
topics: Deploying with Hardhat Ignition or scripts
web3-fullstack.challenge: Write a full Hardhat test suite for your marketplace contract covering all public functions and edge cases, then deploy to Sepolia.

children.title: Hardhat Configuration and Networks
children.description: The hardhat.config file defines compiler versions, optimisation runs, and named networks with their RPC URLs and private keys. A misconfigured solc version causes subtle ABI incompatibilities that only surface at runtime, while wrong optimizer settings inflate gas costs across all deployed contracts.

children.title: Local Network Forking
children.description: Hardhat Network can fork mainnet at a specific block, giving you a local copy of all deployed contracts and token balances. Without forking, integration tests against real DeFi protocols are impossible unless you re-deploy every dependency locally, making realistic test coverage impractical.

children.title: Writing Mocha Tests with Chai Matchers
children.description: Hardhat ships ethereum-waffle and hardhat-chai-matchers that add assertions like revertedWith, emit, and changeEtherBalances. Without these matchers you write brittle try-catch blocks that miss important edge cases and make test intent unclear to reviewers.

children.title: Fixtures and beforeEach Patterns
children.description: loadFixture snapshots EVM state after first deployment, then resets to that snapshot before each test instead of redeploying. This cuts full test suite time by 3-5x on large contract suites and eliminates cross-test state pollution that causes intermittent failures.

children.title: Tracking Deployed Addresses per Network
children.description: A dApp needs the correct contract address for each chain it supports; hardcoding addresses in source causes wrong-network bugs that are silent until a user loses funds. Generating a deployments JSON per network and importing it into the frontend ensures the right ABI-address pair is always used.

children.title: Environment Variables and Key Security
children.description: Private keys and RPC URLs must never be committed to version control; dotenv loads them from a .gitignored .env file at runtime. A single leaked deployer key lets an attacker drain all funds held by contracts that key deployed, including any proxy admin rights.

children.title: Etherscan Contract Verification
children.description: Verifying source code on Etherscan publishes the human-readable contract so users can audit what they are signing. Unverified contracts look like bytecode blobs that no legitimate user should interact with, and security researchers cannot review them without full decompilation.

children.title: RPC Provider Selection
children.description: Public RPC endpoints rate-limit aggressively and drop requests under load, breaking tests and CI pipelines. Using a dedicated Alchemy or Infura endpoint gives you consistent latency and archive node access needed for forking, which is unavailable on most public RPCs.

web3-fullstack.title: Next.js Frontend with wagmi and RainbowKit
web3-fullstack.description: Build a Next.js dApp frontend with wagmi hooks for contract interaction and RainbowKit for wallet connection. Set up the provider tree correctly for SSR.

resources.label: Build a Full Stack dApp with Next.js

web3-fullstack.challenge: Build a Next.js page that lists marketplace items from the contract using useReadContract and shows the connected wallet's listed items.

children.title: WagmiProvider Configuration in Next.js
children.description: wagmi requires its context provider to wrap the entire app, but Next.js App Router marks components as server components by default. Placing WagmiProvider in a client boundary wrapper and passing it QueryClient correctly is not optional — misplacing it causes hook calls to silently return undefined and breaks all wallet reads.

children.title: Server vs Client Component Split
children.description: Next.js App Router renders server components on the server where no wallet or browser API exists; anything that calls a wagmi hook must be a client component. Getting this boundary wrong causes hydration mismatches that crash the page or strip all interactive Web3 elements in production builds.

children.title: RainbowKit ConnectButton
children.description: RainbowKit wraps the complex multi-wallet connection flow into a single ConnectButton component with built-in chain switching, ENS display, and a mobile-optimised modal. Building this from scratch with raw wagmi hooks takes days and routinely ships with missing edge cases like wrong-network handling.

children.title: useAccount and useConnect Hooks
children.description: useAccount exposes the current address, chain, and connection status that every component displaying wallet-specific data depends on. Ignoring the isConnected guard and rendering wallet-specific data before connection causes undefined-address errors and exposes sensitive UI to disconnected users.

children.title: useReadContract and useWriteContract
children.description: useReadContract caches on-chain reads with TanStack Query and auto-refetches on block updates; useWriteContract submits signed transactions through the connected wallet. Conflating reads and writes — or skipping the simulate step before writes — causes users to pay gas for transactions that will revert.

children.title: useWaitForTransactionReceipt
children.description: Submitting a transaction returns a hash immediately, but the transaction may take 12+ seconds to be mined and could still fail on-chain. Polling with useWaitForTransactionReceipt gives you the confirmed receipt, status, and gas used; skipping it means your UI may show success when the transaction actually reverted.

children.title: wagmi CLI Type Generation
children.description: The wagmi CLI generates fully-typed React hooks from your contract ABI, catching wrong argument types and missing parameters at compile time. Without type generation, passing a BigInt where a string is expected causes silent runtime failures that are extremely difficult to trace back to the ABI mismatch.

children.title: Handling BigInt in React
children.description: Solidity uint256 maps to JavaScript BigInt, which JSON.stringify cannot serialise by default and which React cannot render directly as a child. Forgetting to format BigInt values before display causes blank renders or build-breaking serialisation errors when passing on-chain data through Next.js server components.

web3-fullstack.title: The Graph - Subgraph Development
web3-fullstack.description: Build and deploy a subgraph that indexes events from your smart contract into a queryable GraphQL API. Write entity schemas and mapping handlers in AssemblyScript.
topics: Deploying to the hosted service or Subgraph Studio
web3-fullstack.challenge: Deploy a subgraph for your marketplace contract that indexes all listings and sales, then query the last 10 sales with GraphQL.

children.title: Subgraph Manifest and Schema
children.description: The subgraph.yaml manifest tells the Graph Node which contract addresses to watch, which events to handle, and which block to start from. A wrong startBlock means the indexer replays millions of irrelevant blocks; a missing data source means entire event classes are silently dropped from your API.

children.title: Defining GraphQL Entities
children.description: Entities define the data model stored in the Graph Node's Postgres database and exposed via GraphQL. Poorly normalised entities with large repeated string fields inflate storage costs on The Graph Network and slow query response times; wrong ID strategies cause entity overwrites that silently discard historical data.

children.title: Writing Event Handlers
children.description: Mapping handlers transform raw on-chain event data into Graph entities using AssemblyScript, a strict TypeScript subset. AssemblyScript prohibits closures and many JavaScript patterns, so developers porting JavaScript logic directly produce compile errors; more dangerously, incorrect null handling causes the entire subgraph to crash mid-sync.

children.title: Handling Contract Upgrades in Subgraphs
children.description: When a proxy contract changes its implementation, the ABI and event signatures may change. Adding a new dataSource in the manifest for the new implementation address — while preserving the old one — ensures historical events map correctly and the subgraph does not lose pre-upgrade data.

resources.label: The Graph Data Source Templates Docs

children.title: Subgraph Studio Deployment
children.description: Subgraph Studio is The Graph's hosted deployment platform for testnets and mainnet; the graph deploy command pushes your compiled subgraph and starts indexing. Skipping the Studio and querying the deprecated hosted service means your subgraph will be unreachable when that service is fully sunset.

children.title: GraphQL Querying from the Frontend
children.description: The Graph exposes a standard GraphQL endpoint you can query with any HTTP client, but using Apollo Client or urql adds caching, pagination helpers, and real-time subscriptions. Querying The Graph with raw fetch calls lacks cache invalidation, causing stale UI data after on-chain state changes.

children.title: Pagination with first and skip
children.description: The Graph limits unpaginated queries to 100 results by default; fetching large datasets requires iterating with first and skip or cursor-based pagination using ID comparisons. Ignoring pagination returns incomplete data that makes your UI appear to show the full collection when it is silently truncated.

children.title: Filtering and Ordering in GraphQL
children.description: The Graph auto-generates where filter arguments for every entity field and orderBy clauses for sortable fields.
