Without server-side filtering you fetch thousands of entities and filter in JavaScript, wasting bandwidth and slowing page loads; complex where clauses also expose query planning issues in the Graph Node.

web3-fullstack.title: Querying Subgraphs from the Frontend
web3-fullstack.description: Integrate Apollo Client or urql into your Next.js app to query subgraph data. Build paginated, filterable lists of on-chain events rendered server-side.
topics: Combining subgraph data with live contract reads
web3-fullstack.challenge: Replace all direct contract reads in your listing page with Apollo Client queries to the subgraph, adding pagination for collections over 20 items.

children.title: Apollo Client Setup in Next.js
children.description: Apollo Client requires an InMemoryCache and an HttpLink pointing to the subgraph endpoint, wrapped in an ApolloProvider. Initialising a new ApolloClient instance on every render defeats caching entirely, causing a flood of network requests that can get your subgraph endpoint rate-limited.

children.title: Server-Side Rendering with Apollo
children.description: Apollo's experimental RSC support and getApolloClient patterns allow pre-fetching subgraph data on the server for fast initial page loads and SEO-friendly HTML. Skipping SSR for subgraph data means users see loading skeletons during the first render, degrading Core Web Vitals and perceived performance.

children.title: useQuery and Suspense Integration
children.description: Apollo's useQuery hook returns loading, error, and data states that must all be handled; unhandled loading states cause blank renders while unhandled errors crash the component tree. Using Suspense boundaries with Apollo simplifies loading state management and allows partial rendering without blocking the full page.

children.title: Combining Subgraph Data with Live Contract Reads
children.description: Subgraphs are eventually consistent — data appears seconds after the on-chain event. For values that must be real-time (live price, user balance), combining a subgraph query for historical data with a useReadContract call for the current value gives you both rich history and freshness. Using only the subgraph for real-time values shows stale prices that can mislead users.

children.title: urql as a Lightweight Alternative
children.description: urql is a smaller, more modular GraphQL client than Apollo that works well for apps that only query one or two subgraphs. Apollo's full normalised cache is overkill for simple listing pages; using urql reduces bundle size by ~40 KB, which matters on mobile Web3 users on congested networks.

children.title: Raw fetch for Lightweight Queries
children.description: For simple, infrequently-updated pages, a plain fetch to the subgraph GraphQL endpoint inside a Next.js server component is the simplest approach with no library overhead. The key risk is missing error handling and cache control headers; without them, stale CDN-cached responses serve outdated on-chain data indefinitely.

children.title: Pagination and Infinite Scroll
children.description: Collections on-chain grow unboundedly; returning all entities in one query will eventually hit The Graph's 1000-item hard limit and then crash. Implementing cursor-based pagination (where: { id_gt: lastId }) scales to millions of entities, while offset-based pagination degrades with table size and cannot be reliably parallelised.

children.title: Cache Invalidation Strategies
children.description: Apollo's InMemoryCache normalises entities by typename and id; incorrect id fields in the schema cause Apollo to treat different entities as the same object, merging their data incorrectly. Understanding cache eviction and refetch strategies prevents stale listings from persisting after on-chain sales.

web3-fullstack.title: File Storage - IPFS and Arweave
web3-fullstack.description: Build file upload flows that store images and metadata on IPFS or Arweave from the browser. Learn the permanence tradeoffs between pinning services and Arweave.
topics: Uploading files to IPFS via Pinata API
Arweave and Bundlr for permanent storage
Handling large files and upload progress
web3-fullstack.challenge: Build a Next.js upload page that pins an image and a metadata JSON to IPFS via Pinata and displays the resulting IPFS URL on success.

children.title: Content Addressing and CIDs
children.description: IPFS identifies every file by a CID — a cryptographic hash of its content. The same bytes always produce the same CID, which makes storage verifiable on-chain: the contract stores the CID and anyone can confirm the file has not been altered. Storing a mutable URL instead of a CID means the linked data can be silently replaced after minting.

children.title: Pinning Services — Pinata
children.description: IPFS content is only available as long as at least one node pins it. Without a pinning service like Pinata or web3.storage, your uploaded files vanish when your local IPFS node goes offline, making all on-chain URIs return 404. Pinata's API lets you pin files server-side from a Next.js API route without exposing keys to the browser.

children.title: Arweave One-Time Payment Model
children.description: Unlike IPFS where you pay an ongoing pinning fee, Arweave charges a one-time fee to store data permanently using its endowment model. This makes Arweave preferable for NFT metadata that must survive indefinitely; using IPFS for mission-critical metadata without long-term pinning commitments is a common cause of NFT metadata rot.

children.title: IPFS Gateways for Browser Access
children.description: Browsers cannot natively resolve ipfs:// URIs; they need an HTTP gateway like ipfs.io or cloudflare-ipfs.com to fetch content. Relying on a single public gateway creates a centralisation bottleneck that breaks when that gateway is overloaded; using multiple gateway fallbacks or a dedicated gateway improves reliability.

children.title: Server-Side Upload via API Routes
children.description: Pinata API keys must never be sent to the browser because any user could extract them and abuse your paid pinning quota. A Next.js API route acts as a proxy: the browser sends the file to your server, which forwards it to Pinata with the secret key. Skipping this proxy exposes your Pinata account to key theft on every page load.

children.title: Generating and Pinning Metadata JSON
children.description: NFT and asset metadata follows the OpenSea/ERC-721 schema with name, description, image, and attributes fields. Generating this JSON server-side after the image CID is confirmed ensures the metadata CID is deterministic and the image CID is correct. Uploading metadata before the image is pinned embeds a CID that may never resolve.

children.title: Filecoin as Verifiable Storage Layer
children.description: Filecoin is an incentivised storage network built on IPFS where storage providers are cryptographically proven to hold your data via Proof-of-Storage. For enterprise dApps that need auditable storage guarantees rather than trusting a centralised pinning service, Filecoin provides the verifiability that plain IPFS pinning lacks.

children.title: Handling Upload Progress and Large Files
children.description: Browser file uploads over 50 MB require chunked upload or streaming to avoid memory exhaustion and request timeouts. Pinata's resumable upload endpoint and the IPFS Chunker configuration both support large files; not implementing progress tracking leaves users staring at a frozen UI with no feedback during lengthy uploads.

web3-fullstack.title: Authentication and User Profiles
web3-fullstack.description: Implement wallet-based authentication with Sign-In with Ethereum and store per-user settings off-chain in a database. Combine on-chain identity with off-chain preferences.
topics: Iron Session for JWT-free session cookies
Next.js API routes for auth endpoints
Combining SIWE identity with subgraph data
web3-fullstack.challenge: Add SIWE auth to your app, protect a profile page with a session check, and store a display name per wallet in a Postgres database.

children.title: EIP-4361 SIWE Message Format
children.description: Sign-In with Ethereum defines a standardised plaintext message format that includes domain, chain ID, nonce, and expiry. Wallets display this message to users before signing. Deviating from the spec by omitting the domain or nonce breaks third-party SIWE libraries and removes the anti-replay and anti-phishing protections the spec was designed to provide.

children.title: Nonce Generation and Anti-Replay Protection
children.description: A server-generated nonce embedded in the SIWE message prevents an attacker from replaying a captured signature to authenticate as the victim. If you skip nonces or accept signed messages without validating the nonce against a server-side store, any intercepted SIWE message grants permanent access until the session expires.

children.title: Iron Session for JWT-Free Cookies
children.description: Iron Session encrypts session data directly in a signed cookie, eliminating the need for a Redis session store or JWT infrastructure. Misunderstanding this as equivalent to an unsigned cookie is dangerous: without the encryption secret, anyone could forge any wallet address and gain access to their account.

children.title: Protecting Routes with Middleware
children.description: Next.js middleware runs at the edge before any page renders, making it the right place to redirect unauthenticated requests. Checking session status inside a page component instead of middleware allows the page to partially render and flash private content before the redirect fires, a security vulnerability in high-security contexts.

children.title: Linking Wallet Address to Off-Chain Profile
children.description: On-chain identity is just an address; display names, email notifications, and preferences must be stored in a traditional database keyed by wallet address. Using the raw Ethereum address as the primary key is valid, but failure to checksum-normalise addresses before storage causes duplicate rows when users connect with mixed-case addresses.

children.title: Combining SIWE Identity with Subgraph Data
children.description: After SIWE authentication the server knows a verified wallet address, which can be passed as a filter to subgraph queries to show only that user's on-chain activity. Failing to perform this join server-side and instead trusting a client-supplied address parameter allows any user to view another user's private activity feed.

children.title: Chain ID Validation in SIWE
children.description: The SIWE message includes a chainId field that your server must validate matches the chain your contract is deployed on. Ignoring chainId allows a user to sign a message on a testnet and replay it against your mainnet server, granting them authenticated access without ever touching mainnet.

children.title: EIP-712 Typed Data for Off-Chain Actions
children.description: EIP-712 extends SIWE-style signing to structured domain-specific data like order approvals or delegation permits. Showing users a human-readable typed data prompt rather than a raw hex string dramatically reduces phishing risk. Implementing off-chain authorisation without EIP-712 means users must blindly sign opaque bytes.

web3-fullstack.title: Testing, Performance, and Security
web3-fullstack.description: Test the full stack from smart contracts to frontend with unit, integration, and end-to-end tests. Audit for smart contract vulnerabilities and frontend security issues.
topics: Playwright E2E tests for wallet flows
web3-fullstack.challenge: Write a Playwright test that connects a wallet, lists an item, and verifies it appears on the listings page using a Hardhat testnet.

children.title: Playwright for Wallet Flow Testing
children.description: Playwright can control a headless browser with a pre-seeded MetaMask or mock wallet extension, enabling automated tests that simulate the full connect-sign-confirm flow. Without E2E tests, wallet interaction regressions only surface when real users report broken mint or approve flows in production.

children.title: Mocking wagmi Hooks in Vitest
children.description: Unit testing React components that call wagmi hooks requires mocking the hook return values; wagmi ships a createConfig test utility for this purpose. Without mocked hooks, unit tests spin up a real provider and make actual network calls, making them slow, flaky, and dependent on testnet availability.

children.title: Slither Static Analysis
children.description: Slither parses Solidity AST and runs 80+ detectors for known vulnerability patterns including reentrancy, unchecked returns, and dangerous delegatecall. Running Slither before testnet deployment catches most high and medium severity issues in minutes that a manual review would take hours to find.

resources.label: Trail of Bits Smart Contract Security

children.title: Reentrancy and Checks-Effects-Interactions
children.description: Reentrancy occurs when an external call re-enters your contract before state is updated, allowing attackers to drain funds in a loop. The checks-effects-interactions pattern — validate inputs, update state, then call external contracts — prevents this. Inverting the order caused the $60M DAO hack and remains the most exploited Solidity anti-pattern.

children.title: Content Security Policy in Next.js
children.description: A strict CSP header prevents injected scripts from calling arbitrary RPC endpoints or exfiltrating signing prompts — the core technique in Web3 frontend phishing. Without a CSP, a single XSS vulnerability in your dApp lets attackers inject a script that silently replaces your contract address with their own.

children.title: Rate Limiting API Routes
children.description: Next.js API routes that proxy RPC calls or SIWE nonce generation are public endpoints that can be abused to drain your Alchemy quota or flood your database with nonce records. Adding rate limiting with a library like upstash-ratelimit protects both your infrastructure costs and prevents nonce-exhaustion denial-of-service attacks.

children.title: Lighthouse Audits for dApps
children.description: Lighthouse measures Core Web Vitals including LCP and TBT, which are particularly bad in Web3 apps that synchronously wait for wallet connection on page load. Running Lighthouse before each release and treating it as a quality gate prevents progressive performance degradation that drives users away.

children.title: Bundle Analysis with Vite
children.description: Web3 libraries like wagmi, viem, and RainbowKit add significant bundle weight; unused chains, connectors, and ABI definitions can bloat the client bundle. Using rollup-plugin-visualizer or vite-bundle-visualizer reveals which imports dominate, enabling tree-shaking and code-splitting strategies that cut first-load times.

web3-fullstack.title: Mainnet Launch and Production Operations
web3-fullstack.description: Launch your full-stack dApp to production with verified contracts, a deployed subgraph on The Graph Network, and a Next.js frontend on Vercel. Set up monitoring across all layers.
topics: Publishing subgraph to The Graph Network
Vercel production deployment with env vars

resources.label: Production Web3 Full Stack - Nader Dabit

web3-fullstack.challenge: Deploy all three layers to production - mainnet contract, The Graph Studio subgraph, and Vercel frontend - and document the full deployment process.

children.title: Mainnet Deployment Checklist
children.description: Shipping to mainnet without a checklist routinely causes irreversible mistakes: wrong constructor arguments, unverified source code, missing admin roles, or test keys left as owners. A written checklist reviewed by a second engineer is the single highest-ROI process for preventing post-launch emergencies that cannot be rolled back.

children.title: Gnosis Safe Multisig for Admin Control
children.description: Any contract function that can change fees, pause the protocol, or upgrade a proxy must be controlled by a multisig, not a single EOA. A single stolen deployer key can drain a protocol instantly; a 3-of-5 Safe requires an attacker to compromise three independent signers simultaneously, making targeted attacks economically infeasible.

children.title: Publishing Subgraph to The Graph Network
children.description: Publishing to The Graph Network (vs Subgraph Studio testnet) makes your subgraph available to any indexer against GRT signal. Staying on the hosted service means a single point of failure; once the hosted service is deprecated, your entire data layer goes offline. Migrating to the decentralised network requires GRT signalling and indexer negotiation.

children.title: Vercel Production Deployment with Env Vars
children.description: Vercel's environment variable UI separates preview and production secrets, preventing staging keys from leaking into production builds. Hardcoding contract addresses or RPC URLs in source code — even non-secret ones — makes multi-network support require code changes instead of environment variable swaps.

children.title: Tenderly Production Monitoring
children.description: Tenderly monitors every transaction that touches your contract and can alert on failed calls, unusual gas usage, or specific event emissions within seconds. Without production monitoring, protocol anomalies go undetected until a user files a bug report, by which time the exploit window has been open for hours.

children.title: GRT Signal and Indexer Fees
children.description: On The Graph Network, indexers prioritise subgraphs with higher GRT signal. An under-signalled subgraph may have only one indexer, creating a single point of failure. Understanding curation signal economics helps you budget for reliable query service and avoid situations where your dApp cannot fetch subgraph data during high network demand.

children.title: Post-Launch Incident Response Plan
children.description: A written incident response plan defines who is notified when Tenderly fires an alert, how a pause function is triggered via the Safe, and how users are communicated with during an outage. Without this plan, teams spend critical minutes figuring out who has Safe signing keys while funds are actively being drained.

children.title: Upgrade Strategy and Timelocks
children.description: A TimelockController queues admin actions with a mandatory delay (e.g. 48 hours), giving the community time to detect and react to malicious governance proposals before they execute. Upgradeable contracts without timelocks allow a compromised admin key to drain the protocol in a single block, with no window for community intervention.

wallet-developer.title: Web and Cryptography Fundamentals
wallet-developer.description: Understand the cryptographic primitives that underpin Ethereum wallets including elliptic curve keys, hashing, and digital signatures. Learn how private keys control accounts.
topics: Mnemonics and HD wallets BIP-32 and BIP-39
wallet-developer.challenge: Using only ethers.js, generate a random mnemonic, derive the first 5 addresses from it, and verify by re-deriving from the same mnemonic.

children.title: secp256k1 and Key Derivation
children.description: Ethereum private keys are 256-bit scalars on the secp256k1 curve; the public key is derived by scalar multiplication with the generator point G. Misunderstanding this means you cannot correctly implement address generation or verify that a given private key produces a specific address.

children.title: ECDSA Signatures
children.description: ECDSA produces a (r, s, v) triplet from a private key and a message hash; the v component enables public key recovery from the signature alone. Without understanding recovery, you cannot implement ecrecover in Solidity or verify signatures off-chain securely.

children.title: Keccak-256 Hashing
children.description: Ethereum uses keccak-256 (not SHA-3) for all internal hashing — address derivation, storage slot computation, and signed message prefixes. Confusing it with standard SHA-3 leads to hash mismatches that silently break signature verification.

children.title: Address Derivation Pipeline
children.description: An Ethereum address is the last 20 bytes of keccak-256 applied to the 64-byte uncompressed public key. Getting any step wrong — key serialization format, hash truncation — produces an address that does not match what any standard wallet would generate.

children.title: BIP-32 Hierarchical Deterministic Wallets
children.description: BIP-32 defines a tree of child keys derived from a single 512-bit master seed using HMAC-SHA512; child keys can be hardened to prevent parent key compromise from a leaked child. Misimplementing the derivation path or hardening flag generates entirely different keys than every other wallet, making fund recovery impossible.

children.title: BIP-39 Mnemonics and BIP-44 Paths
children.description: BIP-39 maps entropy to a human-readable wordlist and derives a 512-bit seed via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 with an optional passphrase; BIP-44 imposes a coin-type path structure (m/44'/60'/0'/0/index for Ethereum). Any deviation from the standard path means your wallet cannot import keys from hardware wallets or other software wallets.

children.title: Keystore Encryption (EIP-55 / Web3 Secret Storage)
children.description: The Web3 secret storage format wraps the private key in AES-128-CTR encrypted with a key derived via scrypt or PBKDF2, protecting keys at rest against brute-force. Storing keys in localStorage in plaintext instead exposes every user's funds to any XSS payload.

children.title: Web Crypto API for Browser-Native Security
children.description: The Web Crypto API provides non-extractable CryptoKey objects and hardware-accelerated PBKDF2/AES operations that keep key material inside the browser's secure context and prevent JavaScript extraction. Implementing custom crypto with third-party JS libraries instead introduces supply-chain attack surface directly into the key management code path.

wallet-developer.title: EIP-1193 - The Ethereum Provider API
wallet-developer.description: Understand the EIP-1193 specification that defines how web pages communicate with Ethereum wallets through a standardized provider interface. Build a minimal provider consumer.
topics: Listening to accountsChanged and chainChanged events
wallet-developer.challenge: Build a plain HTML page with no libraries that uses window.ethereum to connect, send ETH, and display account and chain changes in real time.

children.title: Provider Request Lifecycle
children.description: EIP-1193 standardizes the window.ethereum object so any dApp can call provider.request({ method, params }) without knowing which wallet is installed. If you bypass this standard and call wallet-specific APIs directly, your dApp breaks for every wallet except the one you tested against.

children.title: Provider Events and Chain Changes
children.description: The accountsChanged and chainChanged events are the canonical way to react to wallet state transitions in real time; not subscribing to them means your UI shows stale account addresses and wrong chain IDs until the page is refreshed. The disconnect event also signals that the wallet has become unreachable and requires reconnection.

children.title: Read vs Write RPC Methods
children.description: eth_call executes code locally without a transaction and costs no gas, while eth_sendTransaction submits a state-changing transaction that the user must sign; conflating the two leads to unnecessary gas spend or missing state changes. Methods prefixed with wallet_ (e.g., wallet_switchEthereumChain) are wallet-specific extensions not part of core Ethereum JSON-RPC.

children.title: personal_sign vs eth_signTypedData
children.description: personal_sign prepends the Ethereum signed message prefix to prevent accidentally signing raw transactions but produces an opaque byte string with no schema; eth_signTypedData_v4 (EIP-712) shows structured, human-readable fields in the wallet UI. Using personal_sign for structured payloads that should use EIP-712 makes phishing indistinguishable from legitimate requests.

children.title: EIP-1559 Fee Model
children.description: EIP-1559 replaced the legacy gas price with a base fee (burned) plus a priority fee (miner tip); setting only maxFeePerGas too low causes transactions to be stuck for hours during fee spikes. Wallets must fetch the current base fee from the pending block header and suggest sensible maxPriorityFeePerGas defaults to keep UX smooth.

children.title: Nonce Management
children.description: Nonces must be strictly sequential per sender address; using a stale nonce resubmits a previous transaction while using a gap nonce leaves all subsequent transactions pending until the gap is filled. Wallets managing multiple in-flight transactions must track pending nonces locally rather than relying solely on eth_getTransactionCount.

children.title: RLP Serialization
children.description: Ethereum encodes transactions as Recursive Length Prefix (RLP) lists before hashing and signing; any byte order error or missing field makes the transaction hash differ from what the node expects, causing signature verification failure. Understanding RLP is essential for building offline signing flows where you serialize without network access.

children.title: Offline Signing Flow
children.description: Offline signing separates key material from network access: the transaction is constructed and signed on an air-gapped device, then the raw signed hex is broadcast from a connected device via eth_sendRawTransaction. Skipping this separation exposes private keys to network-connected memory, negating the security benefit of cold storage.

wallet-developer.title: Key Management and Secure Storage
wallet-developer.description: Learn how to securely generate, encrypt, and store private keys in browser and mobile environments. Understand keystore file formats and hardware wallet integration.
topics: Web Crypto API for browser-native crypto
Secure key storage patterns in browsers
Hardware wallet communication via U2F or HID
wallet-developer.challenge: Build a mini key manager that generates a wallet, encrypts the private key with a password using ethers.js keystore format, and decrypts it on demand.

children.title: Session Proposal and Pairing URI
children.description: WalletConnect v2 uses a relay server to forward encrypted session proposals identified by a pairing URI; the dApp generates the URI and the wallet scans it to establish an end-to-end encrypted channel. Mishandling the URI expiry or relay topic means the pairing silently fails and the user sees a stuck QR code with no error feedback.

children.title: Session Lifecycle and Persistence
children.description: Sessions have an expiry timestamp and must be re-proposed when expired; persisting the session topic and keys in secure storage allows restoring the connection across page reloads without a new QR scan. Failing to handle sessionDelete or sessionExpire events leaves the UI in a connected-looking state while the wallet has already disconnected.

children.title: Injected Provider Discovery (EIP-6963)
children.description: EIP-6963 defines a window event-based discovery mechanism that lets dApps enumerate all installed wallet extensions without colliding on window.ethereum; before EIP-6963, two installed wallets would overwrite each other's provider reference. Wallets that do not emit the eip6963:announceProvider event are invisible to dApps using the new discovery API.

children.title: Connector Abstraction Patterns
children.description: Libraries like wagmi and Web3Modal abstract over MetaMask, WalletConnect, Coinbase Wallet, and hardware wallets behind a unified connector interface, so your dApp code does not need to branch per wallet type. Without this abstraction layer, adding a new wallet type requires touching every send/sign call site in the application.

children.title: Domain Separator and Type Hash
children.description: EIP-712 binds a signature to a specific contract and chain via the domain separator hash, preventing replay of the same signature on a different contract or network. Omitting the chainId from the domain struct makes signed messages portable across chains, enabling replay attacks that drain funds or grant unintended permissions.

children.title: EIP-2612 Permit: Gasless Approvals
children.description: EIP-2612 extends ERC-20 with a permit function that accepts an EIP-712 signature in lieu of a separate approve transaction, allowing users to grant token allowances without paying gas. Forgetting the nonce increment in the permit validation allows the same signed approval to be replayed repeatedly, granting unlimited allowances.

children.title: ecrecover and Signature Malleability
children.description: Solidity's ecrecover returns the signer's address from a (hash, v, r, s) tuple but does not check s-value malleability — an attacker can flip s to produce a second valid signature for the same message. OpenZeppelin's ECDSA library normalizes s and validates v, making signature checking safe without manual bit arithmetic.

children.title: ERC-1271 Smart Contract Signatures
children.description: ERC-1271 defines an isValidSignature interface for smart contract wallets (like ERC-4337 accounts) that cannot sign with a private key but can validate signatures through on-chain logic. Checking only ecrecover without falling back to ERC-1271 breaks signature verification for any user whose account is a smart contract wallet.

wallet-developer.title: Transaction Construction and Signing
wallet-developer.description: Build the logic for constructing, signing, and broadcasting Ethereum transactions with proper nonce management, gas estimation, and EIP-1559 fee fields.
wallet-developer.challenge: Write a function that constructs and signs an EIP-1559 transaction offline, logs the raw hex, and then broadcasts it separately using a provider.

children.title: UserOperation and EntryPoint
children.description: ERC-4337 introduces a pseudo-transaction object called UserOperation that is submitted to an alt mempool and executed through the singleton EntryPoint contract rather than directly by the EOA. Incorrectly constructing the callData or paymasterAndData fields causes the EntryPoint to revert during the validation phase, dropping the operation silently.

children.title: Bundler and Alt Mempool
children.description: Bundlers simulate and batch UserOperations into a single on-chain transaction, paying gas themselves and recouping it from the account's prefunded deposit in the EntryPoint. A UserOperation that passes local simulation can still be rejected by a bundler's stricter storage-access rules, so wallet developers must test against a real bundler, not just local simulation.

children.title: Paymaster for Gas Sponsorship
children.description: A paymaster is a contract that agrees to pay gas for a UserOperation on behalf of the user, enabling sponsored transactions and ERC-20 gas payments without the user holding ETH. If the paymaster's verifyPaymasterUserOp reverts or the deposit is insufficient, the operation fails at validation and the user receives no feedback unless the wallet explicitly checks paymaster balance.

children.title: Social Recovery and Session Keys
children.description: Smart accounts can implement social recovery modules that allow a set of guardian addresses to collectively rotate the signing key after a loss event, replacing the write-it-down seed phrase model. Session key modules allow delegating limited permissions (e.g., spend up to 0.1 ETH on a specific contract) without exposing the master key for each dApp interaction.

children.title: Pre-Sign Transaction Simulation
children.description: Simulating a transaction before presenting the signing prompt lets the wallet display token balance changes, contract interactions, and risk warnings before the user commits gas. Skipping simulation means users sign opaque hex calldata without understanding whether they are transferring tokens, granting unlimited allowances, or interacting with a known scam contract.

children.title: Allowance Risk and Address Poisoning
children.description: Unlimited token approvals granted to compromised or malicious contracts drain wallets long after the original transaction; wallets should display current allowance exposure and warn when a new approval would grant unlimited access. Address poisoning attacks send dust transactions from a look-alike address to pollute the wallet's history, tricking users into copy-pasting the attacker's address.

children.title: Building a Security Snap
children.description: MetaMask Snaps run in a sandboxed JavaScript environment and can intercept transactions via onTransaction, injecting custom risk analysis, blocklist checks, or simulation results into the approval UI. A snap that makes synchronous network calls or takes too long risks being killed by the timeout, so all external requests must be async and handle failures gracefully.

children.title: Domain Verification in Signing Prompts
children.description: Displaying the requesting domain name in every signing prompt is the primary defense against phishing sites that clone a legitimate dApp's UI and request identical signatures; users must be trained to check the domain before confirming. EIP-712 domain binding adds a second layer by making signatures only valid for the declared verifyingContract, even if the user is tricked into signing.

wallet-developer.title: WalletConnect Protocol
wallet-developer.description: Integrate WalletConnect v2 to allow users to connect mobile wallets to desktop dApps via QR code or deep link. Understand the relay server and session lifecycle.
topics: Pairing URI and QR code generation
wallet-developer.challenge: Build a React app that uses WalletConnect AppKit to connect a mobile wallet via QR code, send a test transaction, and display the confirmation.

children.title: EVM Chain Registry and Chain IDs
children.description: Each EVM-compatible network has a unique chainId that is included in transaction signatures to prevent replay across chains; presenting the wrong RPC endpoint under the correct chainId silently corrupts transaction submissions. Wallets should fetch chain metadata from chainlist.org or a similar registry rather than hardcoding RPC URLs that go stale.

children.title: viem Custom Chain Configuration
children.description: viem allows defining arbitrary EVM chains as typed configuration objects with RPC URLs, explorer links, and native currency metadata, enabling wallet code to support any EVM chain without library updates. Using hardcoded chain objects without a dynamic lookup mechanism means the wallet cannot add new chains at runtime when users request them via wallet_addEthereumChain.

children.title: Chainlink CCIP Token Transfers
children.description: Chainlink CCIP provides a standardized cross-chain messaging protocol where a Router contract on the source chain locks or burns tokens and a DON relays the message to the destination chain for minting or unlocking. Wallets integrating bridge flows must monitor the CCIP message status via the off-chain explorer because on-chain finality on the destination can take minutes to hours.

children.title: Multi-Chain Address Book UX
children.description: Addresses are valid on all EVM chains but the assets and contracts at those addresses differ per chain; an address book that does not tag entries with a chain can lead users to send tokens to the correct address on the wrong chain, permanently losing funds. Wallets should store (address, chainId) pairs and enforce chain context when populating recipient fields.

children.title: Ledger HID and Transport Layer
children.description: Ledger devices communicate over USB HID or WebHID using a binary APDU (Application Protocol Data Unit) protocol; the Ledger JavaScript SDK wraps this transport and exposes a high-level signTransaction API that sends the unsigned transaction to the device for user confirmation. Sending the full raw transaction bytes rather than the parsed fields causes the Ledger to display unreadable hex in its review screen, leading users to blindly approve.

children.title: Trezor Connect API
children.description: Trezor Connect provides a browser-based API that pops an iframe-based bridge to the Trezor device, isolating the private key confirmation UI from the dApp page to prevent clickjacking. Wallets that open the Trezor bridge in a hidden iframe or suppress the popup fail device verification because Trezor's CSP blocks communication from non-allowlisted origins.

children.title: BIP-44 Path Compatibility Across Devices
children.description: Ledger defaults to the m/44'/60'/0'/0/x derivation path for Ethereum while some older firmware and MetaMask's legacy mode use m/44'/60'/0'/x; using the wrong path generates different addresses that cannot access funds stored under the other path, locking users out. Wallet UX must offer both paths and let users select or auto-detect which one holds their balance.

children.title: Blind Signing vs Clear Signing
children.description: Most hardware wallets display raw hex calldata as blind signing, which offers no human-readable confirmation; clear signing plugins parse ABI-encoded calldata into readable field names and values on the device screen. Integrating without a clear signing plugin means every contract interaction displays an unreadable warning that normalizes dangerous approval habits.

resources.label: EIP-712 Clear Signing on Hardware Wallets

wallet-developer.title: Message Signing - EIP-712 Typed Data
wallet-developer.description: Implement EIP-712 typed structured data signing for human-readable signing prompts. Build permit-style approvals and off-chain authorization flows.
topics: Signature recovery and verification in Solidity
wallet-developer.challenge: Implement an EIP-712 signed permit flow where a user signs off-chain approval and a relayer submits the transaction on their behalf.

children.title: Browser Extension Security Model
children.description: Browser extensions run content scripts in the page context and a background service worker in an isolated origin; private keys must never cross from the background script into a content script or the page's JavaScript context where XSS can extract them. MetaMask's vault is encrypted in the background worker and the content script only proxies signed messages, enforcing this boundary at the architecture level.

children.title: Key Extraction Threat Modeling with STRIDE
children.description: STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) applied to a wallet surfaces threats like memory scraping of decrypted keys, DNS hijacking of RPC endpoints, and supply-chain attacks on npm dependencies. Without a formal threat model, critical attack surfaces are missed during code review and security audits.

children.title: CSP Headers in Wallet UIs
children.description: A strict Content Security Policy blocks inline scripts, eval, and unauthorized external script sources that could inject code into the wallet popup and exfiltrate signing prompts or keys. Wallets served over HTTP instead of a local extension origin cannot enforce CSP at all, making them trivially phishable through DNS hijacking.

children.title: Secure Context and HTTPS Requirements
children.description: The Web Crypto API, WebHID for hardware wallets, and WebAuthn are all restricted to secure contexts (HTTPS or localhost), so any wallet feature relying on these APIs silently fails on HTTP origins. Wallet developers must enforce HTTPS redirects and use HSTS headers to prevent protocol downgrade attacks that strip the secure context requirement.

children.title: Immunefi Bug Bounty Programs
children.description: Immunefi hosts the largest blockchain security bug bounty platform; publishing a program with clearly scoped assets and payout tiers incentivizes external researchers to responsibly disclose vulnerabilities before they are exploited. A wallet without a public disclosure policy discourages researchers from reporting findings and may face uncoordinated public disclosure that harms users before a patch is ready.

children.title: OWASP Top 10 for Wallet Applications
children.description: The OWASP Top 10 maps directly to wallet attack surfaces: injection attacks target RPC parameter construction, broken access control maps to insufficient permission checks before signing, and cryptographic failures cover weak key derivation or insecure random number generation. Running the OWASP Testing Guide checklist against a wallet before launch catches entire vulnerability classes that code review alone misses.

children.title: npm Dependency Auditing
children.description: Wallet codebases that import cryptographic primitives from npm are vulnerable to supply-chain attacks where a compromised package version injects key-logging code; pinning exact dependency versions in a lock file and running npm audit in CI catches known CVEs before deployment. The event-stream attack of 2018 demonstrated that even deeply transitive dependencies targeting crypto wallets are a real threat vector.

children.title: Subresource Integrity and Code Signing
children.description: Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes on loaded scripts ensure that a CDN-served library has not been tampered with between your build and the user's browser; wallets that load any external script without SRI allow a CDN compromise to inject arbitrary key-stealing code. Browser extension manifests should enumerate all permitted external connections so any unexpected outbound request from the extension is blocked.

wallet-developer.title: Account Abstraction - ERC-4337
wallet-developer.description: Learn ERC-4337 account abstraction to build smart contract wallets with social recovery, gas sponsorship, and batch transactions. Understand UserOperation and bundler flow.
wallet-developer.challenge: Deploy a simple ERC-4337 smart account on a testnet and send a UserOperation that is sponsored by a paymaster covering gas fees.

children.title: UserOperation Flow
children.description: ERC-4337 replaces EOA transactions with UserOperation objects that are collected by bundlers and submitted on-chain via the EntryPoint contract. Misunderstanding the flow leads to incorrect gas estimation, failed operations, and broken wallet UX. Every wallet developer must understand how UserOps travel from the user to the mempool to the chain.

children.title: Bundlers and the Alt Mempool
children.description: Bundlers collect UserOperations from an alternative mempool, simulate them, and batch them into a single on-chain transaction. Choosing or misconfiguring a bundler directly impacts transaction success rates, latency, and MEV exposure. Teams that ignore bundler selection often face silent operation failures under load.

children.title: Paymaster Architecture
children.description: Paymasters are smart contracts that can sponsor gas fees for users, enabling gasless transactions and ERC-20 gas payments. Incorrect paymaster validation logic can be exploited to drain the sponsor's deposit or cause mass operation failures. Understanding the two-phase validation model is essential before deploying any paymaster.

children.title: Smart Account Standards
children.description: Smart accounts are contract-based wallets that implement the IAccount interface and hold user funds. A poorly implemented smart account can lock funds permanently, allow unauthorized signers, or fail signature validation across chains. Auditing smart account logic before launch is non-negotiable for any production wallet.

children.title: Session Keys and Delegated Signing
children.description: Session keys allow dApps to sign operations on behalf of the user within scoped permissions, dramatically improving UX by removing repeated approval prompts. Misconfigured session key scopes can grant unrestricted access to user funds. Time-bounded, contract-scoped session keys are the safe pattern.

children.title: Social Recovery and Multi-Sig
children.description: Social recovery lets users designate guardians who can restore wallet access without a seed phrase, solving the self-custody UX problem. Poorly designed recovery schemes introduce centralization risk or allow guardian collusion to hijack accounts. Delay mechanisms and guardian thresholds must be tuned carefully for each deployment.

resources.label: Vitalik - Why We Need Account Abstraction

children.title: UserOp Gas Fields
children.description: ERC-4337 splits gas into callGasLimit, verificationGasLimit, and preVerificationGas, each covering a different phase of execution. Underestimating any field causes the UserOperation to revert at a specific phase, producing opaque error messages that confuse developers. Accurate simulation via eth_estimateUserOperationGas before submission is mandatory.

children.title: EntryPoint Contract Interaction
children.description: The EntryPoint is the singleton on-chain contract that validates and executes all UserOperations, acting as the trust anchor of the ERC-4337 system. Interacting with it incorrectly — for example by calling handleOps directly without proper simulation — can result in reverted bundles and slashed bundler deposits. Developers must treat the EntryPoint as an immutable protocol boundary, not a custom contract.

wallet-developer.title: Phishing Prevention and User Security
wallet-developer.description: Build wallet features that protect users from phishing, transaction simulation previews, and malicious contract interactions. Study common wallet attack vectors.
wallet-developer.challenge: Build a React component that simulates a transaction using Tenderly before presenting it to the user, displaying token changes and warnings for high-risk functions.

children.title: Transaction Simulation Before Signing
children.description: Simulating a transaction before the user signs it surfaces token approvals, asset transfers, and contract interactions in human-readable form, allowing users to catch malicious intent. Wallets that skip simulation leave users blind to drainer contracts that look like legitimate mints. Integrating simulation APIs is now a baseline security requirement for any production wallet.

children.title: Address Poisoning Attacks
children.description: Address poisoning sends a zero-value transaction from a vanity address that visually resembles the victim's frequent contacts, exploiting copy-paste habits. Wallets that only show the first and last characters of addresses create the exact conditions this attack requires. Showing full addresses with visual checksums and flagging unfamiliar senders are effective mitigations.

children.title: Domain and URL Verification
children.description: Phishing sites impersonate legitimate dApps using lookalike domains, typosquatting, and IDN homograph attacks to steal signatures and approvals. Wallets must verify the connecting origin against a blocklist and display the full domain prominently during connection and signing requests. Trusting user-provided display names without domain verification is a critical wallet flaw.

children.title: Unlimited Approval Warnings
children.description: ERC-20 unlimited approvals grant a contract perpetual access to a user's entire token balance, becoming a permanent liability if the contract is later exploited or upgraded maliciously. Wallets that display raw hex approval values instead of human-readable amounts systematically fail to communicate this risk. Clear UI differentiation between bounded and unbounded approvals is a security-critical design requirement.

children.title: Human-Readable Signing Requests (EIP-712)
children.description: EIP-712 enables wallets to display structured, typed data in signing prompts instead of opaque hex blobs, giving users a meaningful chance to understand what they are authorizing. Wallets that fall back to raw bytes for typed data silently become vectors for blind-signing phishing. Implementing EIP-712 display is mandatory for any wallet that aims to protect non-technical users.

children.title: Risk Scoring and Real-Time Alerts
children.description: Integrating real-time risk scoring APIs allows wallets to flag known scam contracts, rug pulls, and suspicious token movements before the user commits a transaction. Without scoring, users rely solely on their own judgment against adversaries who are highly optimized for deception. Even a simple heuristic block on known-bad contract addresses prevents a large class of retail losses.

children.title: Secure Enclave and Key Storage
children.description: Private keys must never be stored in plaintext in localStorage, application state, or logs — even transiently. Mobile wallets should leverage platform secure enclaves (iOS Secure Enclave, Android Keystore) to ensure keys are hardware-bound and never exposed to the application layer. A single key exposure incident can result in complete, irreversible loss of user funds.

children.title: Clipboard and Screenshot Protection
children.description: Seed phrases and private keys displayed on screen are vulnerable to screenshot malware, shoulder surfing, and automatic cloud photo backup. Wallets must actively suppress screenshots during key display on Android and iOS, and warn users never to paste keys into external apps. Failing to implement these protections is a common vector for mobile wallet drains.

wallet-developer.title: Multi-Chain and Cross-Chain Wallet Support
wallet-developer.description: Extend your wallet to support multiple EVM-compatible chains and implement basic cross-chain asset bridging flows. Manage chain-specific RPCs and network switching.
wallet-developer.challenge: Extend your wallet UI to support Ethereum, Arbitrum, and Polygon simultaneously, displaying balances on all three chains with a single wallet connection.

children.title: Chain Configuration and EIP-3085
children.description: EIP-3085 defines the wallet_addEthereumChain RPC method, giving dApps a standardized way to request adding new networks to the wallet. Without proper validation of incoming chain configs, a malicious dApp can inject a fake network that proxies transactions to a different chain than the user expects. Wallets must validate chainId, symbol, and RPC endpoints before persisting any chain addition.

children.title: Chain Switching and EIP-3326
children.description: EIP-3326 introduces wallet_switchEthereumChain, allowing dApps to request the wallet switch to a specific network without requiring manual user navigation. Improper handling of mid-session chain switches can cause signed transactions to be broadcast on the wrong chain, resulting in loss or failed operations. Wallets must surface chain switch requests as prominent, blocking UI prompts — never silent switches.

children.title: Bridge Risk and Trust Models
children.description: Cross-chain bridges vary widely in their trust assumptions — from native light-client bridges to multisig custodians — and wallets that surface all bridges equally obscure significant risk differences from users. Bridge exploits are historically the largest single category of DeFi losses, making it critical that wallets display bridge security metadata alongside routing options. Developers must understand lock-and-mint versus burn-and-mint mechanics to correctly display and validate bridge transactions.

children.title: Cross-Chain Message Verification
children.description: Cross-chain messaging protocols like LayerZero and Hyperlane deliver arbitrary messages between chains, but their security relies on the integrity of oracles or validators that can be compromised. Wallets integrating cross-chain operations must validate that the destination chain received and executed the expected message before marking a transaction complete. Showing optimistic success states before cross-chain finality is confirmed misleads users and causes support incidents.

children.title: Address Formats Across Chains
children.description: EVM chains share the same 0x address format but non-EVM chains like Solana, Bitcoin, and Cosmos use entirely different address schemes, making a single address field in a multi-chain wallet a serious UX and security liability. Sending assets to an EVM address on Solana or vice versa results in permanent loss that cannot be reversed. Wallets must validate the address format against the selected chain before allowing any send operation.

children.title: HD Wallet Derivation Paths Per Chain
children.description: BIP-44 defines per-coin derivation paths (e.g. m/44'/60'/0' for Ethereum, m/44'/0'/0' for Bitcoin), and using the wrong path generates a valid but incorrect address that the user cannot recover with standard wallets. Multi-chain wallets that derive all keys from a single Ethereum path expose users to fund loss if they restore their wallet in a chain-specific app expecting the canonical path. Correctly implementing and surfacing per-chain derivation paths is foundational to interoperability.

children.title: Per-Chain Balance and Nonce Tracking
children.description: Each chain maintains independent nonce sequences, token balances, and pending transaction queues, and wallet state that conflates chains produces stale balances, stuck transactions, and incorrect gas estimates. Wallets that poll a single RPC endpoint for all chains introduce subtle race conditions when the user switches chains mid-operation. State management must be strictly namespaced by chainId from the database layer up to the UI.

children.title: WalletConnect v2 Multi-Chain Sessions
children.description: WalletConnect v2 uses CAIP-2 chain identifiers and supports multi-chain sessions in a single pairing, replacing the v1 single-chain model. Wallets that implement v2 incorrectly may approve session proposals without validating that all requested chains and methods are supported, creating phantom approvals. Thorough namespace validation on both proposal and settlement is required to avoid silent capability mismatches.

wallet-developer.title: Production Wallet Security Audit and Launch
wallet-developer.description: Perform a security audit of your wallet covering key storage, signing flows, network communication, and phishing surface area. Apply mitigations and launch with a bug bounty.
topics: Content script and background page isolation
wallet-developer.challenge: Complete a full threat model for your wallet using STRIDE, fix all critical and high findings, document mitigations, and publish a responsible disclosure policy.

children.title: Smart Contract Audit Process
children.description: A professional smart contract audit systematically reviews code for reentrancy, integer overflow, access control flaws, and logic errors that automated tools miss. Skipping or rushing an audit before deploying wallet contracts — especially those holding user funds — is the single highest-risk decision a wallet team can make. Audit reports must be resolved, re-reviewed, and published to establish user trust.

resources.label: Trail of Bits - Building Secure Smart Contracts

children.title: Frontend and Extension Penetration Testing
children.description: Wallet frontends and browser extensions are exposed to XSS, prototype pollution, and malicious web page content injection attacks that can extract in-memory keys or forge signing requests. Penetration testing must cover the extension message-passing interface, content script isolation, and popup communication channels — not just the backend smart contracts. A compromised frontend makes all on-chain security irrelevant.

children.title: Structuring a Bug Bounty Program
children.description: A well-scoped bug bounty program with clear severity definitions, response SLAs, and tiered rewards creates a sustainable channel for responsible disclosure after launch. Programs with vague scope or low maximum payouts fail to attract top researchers, leaving critical vulnerabilities unreported or sold to adversaries. Publishing the bounty policy before mainnet launch signals security maturity to users and institutional partners.

children.title: Severity Classification (CVSS and Immunefi Scale)
children.description: Correctly classifying vulnerability severity ensures that critical findings like key extraction or unauthorized fund movement receive immediate escalation, while informational findings are addressed at a lower priority. Teams that apply generic CVSS scores without adjusting for the crypto context — where critical bugs may be immediately and irreversibly exploitable — systematically under-triage real risk. Using the Immunefi severity framework alongside CVSS gives the most accurate picture for wallet-specific threats.

children.title: Content Security Policy (CSP) for Wallets
children.description: A strict Content Security Policy prevents injected scripts from executing in the wallet UI, blocking the most common class of XSS attacks that attempt to exfiltrate keys or forge transaction data. Wallet extensions running without a CSP are trivially exploitable by malicious web pages that inject content scripts or abuse postMessage. CSP headers must be configured for both the extension popup and any hosted dApp interfaces before launch.

children.title: Supply Chain Security and Dependency Auditing
children.description: Wallet applications have broad npm dependency trees, and a single malicious or compromised package can introduce a backdoor that silently exfiltrates private keys at runtime. The event-stream and ua-parser-js incidents demonstrated that popular, trusted packages can be hijacked to target crypto wallets specifically. Wallet teams must enforce lockfiles, run automated SCA scans, and apply Subresource Integrity checks on any externally loaded assets.

children.title: Incident Response and Key Revocation
children.description: Every wallet team must have a documented incident response plan that covers smart contract pausing, key compromise communication, and user fund recovery procedures before going to production. The hours immediately following a security incident are chaotic — teams without a runbook consistently make decisions that worsen user losses. Clear chain-of-command, pre-tested communication channels, and circuit-breaker contract mechanisms are the minimum viable incident toolkit.

resources.label: NIST Computer Security Incident Handling Guide

children.title: Monitoring and Anomaly Detection Post-Launch
children.description: Post-launch monitoring via on-chain alerting detects unusual drains, abnormal approval volumes, or mass transaction failures in near real-time, enabling faster response before losses scale. Wallets that rely solely on user-reported issues discover exploits hours or days late, after the maximum damage is done. Setting up automated alerts on key contract events — including Approval, Transfer above threshold, and EntryPoint deposits — is the last line of defense after deployment.

gamefi-developer.title: Web, Blockchain, and Game Design Fundamentals
gamefi-developer.description: Understand the GameFi model combining blockchain ownership with game mechanics. Learn how on-chain assets, play-to-earn economics, and NFT ownership work in games.

resources.label: Play-to-Earn Game Design - Blockchain Game Alliance

gamefi-developer.challenge: Design a simple GameFi concept with a token economy: define what players earn, how they spend, and what assets are NFTs vs regular game data.

children.title: On-Chain vs Off-Chain Game State
children.description: Deciding which game data lives on-chain versus off-chain is the single most important architectural choice in GameFi. Putting too much on-chain makes every action expensive and slow; putting too little means players cannot truly own or trade their assets.

children.title: Play-to-Earn Economic Model
children.description: Play-to-earn mechanics tie real economic value to in-game activity, which attracts players but also speculators. Without a sustainable token sink and emission schedule, inflationary collapse follows — as demonstrated by the Axie Infinity SLP token in 2021.

children.title: Dual-Token Economy Design
children.description: Most sustainable GameFi projects separate a governance or value-store token from a high-velocity utility token used for daily gameplay. Conflating the two roles forces every player transaction to interact with a speculative asset, amplifying volatility and harming retention.

children.title: Unity Engine Overview for Blockchain Games
children.description: Unity is the dominant engine for GameFi titles because its WebGL export target lets games run in a browser alongside MetaMask without a native install. Understanding Unity's component model and C# scripting is prerequisite before adding any blockchain layer.

children.title: Provenance and Digital Ownership
children.description: The fundamental value proposition of NFTs in games is cryptographic proof of ownership that exists independently of the game operator. If the studio shuts down, the player still holds the token; without this guarantee the NFT is just a receipt.

children.title: Token Sink Mechanics
children.description: Any token minted as a reward must have a corresponding demand mechanism — crafting fees, upgrade costs, or governance locks — otherwise supply always outruns demand. Games that skip this step eventually see their in-game currency approach zero, destroying the earn incentive.

resources.label: Ethereum Developer Docs — Gas and Fees

children.title: Speculative vs Genuine Player Base
children.description: GameFi projects that attract speculators more than gamers collapse quickly when token prices fall, because purely financial users exit immediately. Designing engaging core gameplay loops independent of monetary reward keeps a loyal user base through market cycles.

children.title: Gas UX and Transaction Friction
children.description: Every on-chain action that forces a player to confirm a wallet popup interrupts game immersion. Excessive gas friction is the leading cause of user drop-off in GameFi; batching, meta-transactions, and session keys are the primary mitigations.

gamefi-developer.title: Solidity Basics for Game Contracts
gamefi-developer.description: Learn Solidity fundamentals with a game focus, writing contracts for in-game tokens and character NFTs. Understand how game logic can live on-chain.
topics: ERC-721 for unique characters or items
Access control for game admin functions
gamefi-developer.challenge: Deploy an ERC-721 character contract with on-chain stats (level, attack, defense) and an ERC-20 reward token that the game admin can mint.

children.title: Structs and Mappings for Player State
children.description: Game contracts frequently model characters, items, and match results as Solidity structs stored in mappings keyed by player address or token ID. Poor struct packing wastes storage slots and can double or triple gas costs for every mint or state update.

children.title: Access Control for Game Admin Functions
children.description: Game contracts need privileged roles: the game server signs reward payouts, the team controls upgrades, and a timelock guards parameter changes. Using OpenZeppelin's AccessControl rather than a single Ownable address makes role delegation auditable and revocable.

children.title: ERC-20 as In-Game Currency
children.description: Using a purpose-built ERC-20 for in-game currency rather than a native chain coin keeps reward accounting clean and allows the game contract to mint or burn tokens under controlled conditions. Misunderstanding the approval flow causes players to lose funds when the game contract tries to spend tokens on their behalf.

children.title: ERC-721 for Unique Game Characters
children.description: Each ERC-721 token is cryptographically unique and cannot be merged or subdivided, making it the correct primitive for characters, land parcels, or legendary items. Confusing ERC-721 with ERC-1155 leads to unnecessary gas costs when multiple copies of the same item type are minted.

children.title: Emitting Events for Game Actions
children.description: Solidity events are the cheapest way to record game activity on-chain and the only mechanism that off-chain indexers like The Graph can reliably capture. Missing or poorly indexed events forces game backends to replay the entire chain state just to rebuild a leaderboard.

children.title: CryptoZombies as a Learning Path
children.description: CryptoZombies builds a complete game-themed Solidity project step by step, covering ownership, attack mechanics, and token integration. Completing all lessons before writing production game contracts prevents common beginner errors such as unbounded loops and unchecked arithmetic.

children.title: Hardhat for Local Game Contract Testing
children.description: Running game contracts against a local Hardhat node lets you simulate hundreds of player interactions in seconds without gas costs. Without automated tests, even simple balance-accounting bugs go undetected until mainnet, where they become exploits.

children.title: Remix IDE for Rapid Prototyping
children.description: Remix IDE allows deploying and calling game contracts directly in the browser without any local toolchain setup, making it ideal for validating struct layouts and access control before committing to a full Hardhat project. Skipping this prototyping phase wastes hours on toolchain configuration for contracts that turn out to be architecturally flawed.

gamefi-developer.title: Unity and web3.js Integration
gamefi-developer.description: Connect a Unity WebGL game to Ethereum using web3.js and the MetaMask browser extension. Read and write contract data from within a running game session.
topics: Calling JavaScript from Unity with jslib
gamefi-developer.challenge: Build a Unity WebGL game that reads the player NFT stats from the contract and displays them on a HUD overlay inside the game.

children.title: Unity jslib Plugin System
children.description: Unity WebGL communicates with the browser through a JavaScript plugin layer called jslib. Without understanding this boundary, developers cannot call MetaMask from a running game session; any attempt to use native C# networking for blockchain calls will fail completely in WebGL builds.

resources.label: Unity Manual — Interaction with Browser Scripting
Unity Manual — WebGL Build and Player Settings

children.title: web3.js Provider Setup in WebGL Context
children.description: web3.js wraps the injected window.ethereum provider and translates JavaScript calls into JSON-RPC requests. Misunderstanding async callback chains in this context leads to race conditions where the game renders before wallet state is confirmed, showing incorrect balances.

children.title: Reading NFT Ownership and Stats at Runtime
children.description: Game logic that depends on NFT attributes must query the contract at session start and re-query after any mutation. Caching stale on-chain state causes players to see incorrect character stats or item counts, eroding trust in the game's fairness.

children.title: Sending Transactions from In-Game Events
children.description: Triggering a blockchain transaction from a game event — such as claiming a reward after a level clear — requires a wallet confirmation popup that pauses gameplay. Designing around this friction with batching or deferred settlement keeps the player experience smooth.

children.title: Unity WebGL Build Configuration
children.description: WebGL builds of Unity games are significantly larger than native builds and require careful compression and code stripping settings to achieve acceptable load times. An unoptimized build can exceed 100 MB, causing most casual players to abandon loading before the wallet connect screen appears.

children.title: Cross-Origin and HTTPS Constraints
children.description: MetaMask and other injected wallets only operate on HTTPS origins. Serving a WebGL game from an HTTP host silently blocks all wallet injection, making the integration appear broken when the real issue is protocol security policy.

children.title: Account Detection and Chain ID Validation
children.description: A game that does not verify the connected chain ID will happily read NFT ownership from the wrong network, showing items the player does not actually own on the intended chain. Chain ID validation must happen before any game state is loaded.

resources.label: MetaMask Docs — Detecting the Ethereum Provider

children.title: Graceful Disconnection and Session Handling
children.description: Players frequently switch accounts or networks mid-session; the game must listen to accountsChanged and chainChanged events and either re-authenticate or display a clear error. Ignoring these events causes the game to act on behalf of the wrong player address.

gamefi-developer.title: NFT Game Assets - ERC-1155 and Metadata
gamefi-developer.description: Use ERC-1155 for efficient batch minting of game items, weapons, and consumables. Store item metadata on IPFS and render attributes inside the game.
gamefi-developer.challenge: Create an ERC-1155 game item contract with 5 item types, mint a starter pack for a test player, and render the item attributes in a Unity inventory screen.

children.title: ERC-1155 Batch Operations
children.description: ERC-1155 allows minting and transferring multiple item types in a single transaction, which is essential for loot drops that award swords, shields, and potions simultaneously. Using ERC-721 for the same use case would cost 3-10x more gas and make loot box reveals economically unviable.

children.title: Burning Items for Crafting Mechanics
children.description: Crafting systems that consume lower-tier items to produce higher-tier ones provide the token sink that prevents item inflation. ERC-1155's burn function combined with game logic must be atomic — burn the inputs and mint the output in a single transaction — otherwise a revert after the burn permanently destroys items.

children.title: Item Metadata Schema and Attributes
children.description: Game items need structured metadata with attributes like damage, rarity, and durability that both the game client and marketplace UIs can parse. Deviating from the OpenSea metadata standard means items render incorrectly in every third-party marketplace without custom integration.

children.title: IPFS Pinning with Pinata
children.description: IPFS content is only available as long as at least one node pins it; without a pinning service like Pinata, item metadata disappears from the network over time, breaking tokenURI resolution and rendering NFTs valueless in any marketplace.

children.title: balanceOfBatch for Inventory Lookups
children.description: ERC-1155's balanceOfBatch allows querying a player's inventory across dozens of item types in a single RPC call. Calling balanceOf individually for each item type in a large inventory causes hundreds of network round trips, making inventory loading prohibitively slow.

children.title: setApprovalForAll and Operator Risk
children.description: ERC-1155's setApprovalForAll grants a contract permission to move every item a player owns, not just specific tokens. Players who do not understand the scope of this approval can lose their entire inventory if a marketplace or game contract is compromised.

children.title: Fetching and Parsing IPFS Metadata at Runtime
children.description: Unity's UnityWebRequest can retrieve JSON metadata from an IPFS gateway URL, which the game client parses to load the correct 3D model and attribute values. A hardcoded IPFS gateway that goes offline will break all item rendering; using a configurable gateway list prevents this single point of failure.

children.title: Addressable Assets for Dynamic Item Loading
children.description: Unity Addressables allow loading 3D item meshes on demand based on an item ID retrieved from the blockchain, avoiding the need to bundle every possible item into the initial WebGL build. Without this, adding a new item requires a full game client rebuild and redeployment.

gamefi-developer.title: Randomness and Loot Generation
gamefi-developer.description: Implement verifiable random number generation using Chainlink VRF for fair loot drops and procedural game content. Understand why on-chain randomness from blockhash is unsafe.
topics: Mapping random words to loot tables
VRF callback and game state update
gamefi-developer.challenge: Build a loot box contract that uses Chainlink VRF to determine which ERC-1155 item a player receives when they open a box.

children.title: Why blockhash Randomness is Exploitable
children.description: Miners and validators can see the blockhash before it is committed, meaning anyone who controls block production can reroll loot drops by selectively including or excluding transactions. A single exploited loot box contract can drain a game's entire item treasury in minutes.

resources.label: SWC Registry — Weak Sources of Randomness
Solidity Docs — Block and Transaction Properties

children.title: Chainlink VRF v2 Architecture
children.description: Chainlink VRF generates randomness off-chain with a cryptographic proof that the on-chain contract verifies before accepting the result, making it impossible for anyone — including Chainlink — to predict or manipulate the output. Omitting the proof verification step defeats the entire purpose of using VRF.

children.title: Funding and Managing VRF Subscriptions
children.description: Chainlink VRF v2 uses a subscription model where LINK tokens are pre-funded to pay for randomness requests. A depleted subscription silently fails all VRF callbacks, leaving loot boxes stuck in a pending state that players interpret as a hack or rug pull.

children.title: Mapping Random Words to Loot Tables
children.description: A VRF response contains one or more random uint256 values that must be mapped to item rarity tiers using modular arithmetic. Off-by-one errors in rarity threshold calculations cause certain items to be impossible to obtain or over-represented, breaking the designed drop economy.

children.title: Asynchronous VRF Fulfillment Flow
children.description: VRF operates in two transactions: a request that emits an event and a fulfillment callback that delivers the random number. Game UX must handle this async gap — typically showing a pending animation — because any UI that assumes synchronous randomness will display incorrect results.

resources.label: Chainlink VRF — Get a Random Number

children.title: Re-entrancy Risk in VRF Callbacks
children.description: The VRF fulfillment callback (fulfillRandomWords) is called by an external Chainlink contract, making it a re-entrancy vector if the callback itself triggers token mints or transfers without using ReentrancyGuard. A crafted callback exploit can mint unlimited items if this guard is absent.

children.title: VRFCoordinatorV2Mock for Local Testing
children.description: Chainlink provides a mock VRF coordinator for local Hardhat and Foundry tests that lets developers simulate fulfillment without actual LINK spend. Skipping mock testing means every iteration requires a testnet deployment and real LINK, slowing development by an order of magnitude.

children.title: Chainlink VRF on Testnets
children.description: Before mainnet launch, VRF contracts must be tested on a supported testnet with real VRF infrastructure to catch gas estimation errors and subscription balance management issues that the mock cannot reproduce. The Sepolia testnet is the current recommended Chainlink VRF testing environment.

gamefi-developer.title: Play-to-Earn Reward Mechanics
gamefi-developer.description: Design and implement smart contract reward systems where players earn tokens through gameplay. Build staking mechanics for NFT characters and anti-bot protections.

resources.label: Staking Contract Tutorial - Smart Contract Programmer

gamefi-developer.challenge: Build a staking contract where players stake NFT characters to earn ERC-20 tokens per block, with a 24-hour unstaking cooldown.

children.title: Per-Block Reward Emission
children.description: Rewards accrued per block must be calculated lazily to avoid O(n) loops over all stakers on every transaction. Using a cumulative reward-per-share accumulator pattern — as pioneered by Synthetix staking — reduces reward distribution to O(1) regardless of the number of players.

children.title: NFT Staking for Passive Rewards
children.description: Staking game NFTs in a reward contract transfers custody to the contract address, which must be accounted for when the game reads NFT ownership — the staking contract becomes the owner, not the player. Forgetting to implement a view that resolves staked-to-original-owner causes staked characters to disappear from the game HUD.

children.title: Cooldowns and Commit-Reveal Against Bots
children.description: Without per-wallet cooldowns between reward claims, bots can submit hundreds of micro-transactions per block and drain the reward pool before any human player can react. Commit-reveal schemes add a mandatory delay between committing a game result and revealing it to claim rewards, making MEV-based reward sniping uneconomical.

children.title: Reward Halving and Emission Schedules
children.description: Pre-programming a halving schedule into the reward contract ensures that token supply growth slows over time in a predictable, auditable way that cannot be altered by the team unilaterally. Projects that rely on manual admin calls to reduce emission have repeatedly rug-pulled players by delaying or reversing the reduction.

children.title: Treasury Management for Reward Pools
children.description: A reward pool that is not backed by real protocol revenue will eventually exhaust its reserves and crash the token price. Sustainable GameFi projects tie emission rates to active player counts or revenue, ensuring the game can pay out rewards indefinitely rather than relying on new player capital.

children.title: ERC-20 Minting Access Control
children.description: The game contract that mints reward tokens must hold a MINTER_ROLE that is revocable by governance, not an uncapped Ownable mint function. A compromised private key with unrestricted mint access can print infinite tokens and collapse the economy in a single transaction.

children.title: Daily Quest and Login Reward Contracts
children.description: Implementing daily quest streaks on-chain using block timestamps creates a transparent, manipulation-proof attendance system. However, block timestamps can be manipulated by validators by a few seconds — quest windows should use at least 12-hour grace periods to prevent legitimate players from being denied rewards due to minor timestamp variance.

children.title: Season and Battle Pass Mechanics On-Chain
children.description: Season passes implemented as time-bounded NFTs give players a verifiable record of past participation that holds collectible value after the season ends. Implementing this off-chain means the studio can retroactively revoke pass benefits, destroying player trust.

gamefi-developer.title: On-Chain Marketplace for Game Items
gamefi-developer.description: Build a peer-to-peer marketplace for game items where players list, buy, and trade ERC-1155 assets using the in-game ERC-20 token. Implement royalties for creators.
topics: ERC-2981 royalty on game item sales
gamefi-developer.challenge: Build a game item marketplace where players can list items for a fixed price in game token, and the contract takes a 2.5% fee on each sale.

children.title: Listing and Order Struct Design
