children.description: A marketplace listing struct must capture the seller address, item contract, token ID, quantity, price, and an expiry timestamp in a single storage slot cluster to minimize gas. A poorly packed struct that stores these across multiple slots doubles the gas cost of every listing creation and cancellation.

resources.label: Solidity Docs — Layout of State Variables in Storage

children.title: Escrow and Order Matching
children.description: When a buyer purchases a listed item, the marketplace must atomically transfer the payment token in and the item out; partial success would mean either the buyer loses funds without receiving the item or the seller loses the item without payment. Using Solidity's check-effects-interactions pattern prevents this class of reentrancy-based theft.

children.title: ERC-2981 Royalty Standard for Game Items
children.description: Implementing ERC-2981 allows item creators to receive a percentage of every secondary sale automatically in any marketplace that respects the standard. Without it, game studios and players who craft rare items receive nothing on resale, removing the economic incentive to create rare items in the first place.

children.title: Marketplace Fee Distribution to Treasury
children.description: A well-designed marketplace takes a percentage fee on every trade and routes it to a DAO treasury or liquidity pool, creating a revenue loop that sustains development. Sending fees directly to an EOA rather than a multisig means a single key compromise drains all protocol revenue.

children.title: Reading and Displaying Active Listings
children.description: Marketplace listings stored purely in contract state require iterating over a mapping which is impossible from outside the EVM without an index. Emitting ListingCreated and ListingCancelled events and indexing them in a subgraph is the standard pattern for building a performant marketplace browse page.

children.title: Approval and Purchase Transaction Flow
children.description: ERC-1155 purchases require the buyer to first call setApprovalForAll on the item contract, then call buy on the marketplace — a two-transaction UX that confuses new players. Displaying a clear two-step progress indicator and explaining why approval is required before purchase reduces abandonment significantly.

children.title: Time-Based Listing Expiry
children.description: Listings without expiry timestamps remain valid indefinitely, meaning a player who listed an item cheaply months ago can be front-run when its price spikes. Adding a block.timestamp expiry to listings protects sellers from stale-price exploitation without requiring an explicit cancellation transaction.

children.title: Cancel Listing Gas Efficiency
children.description: Deleting a listing mapping entry refunds a storage slot gas rebate, making cancellations cheaper than they appear. Guiding players to cancel old listings before they expire actively frees storage and reduces the blockchain's state bloat, which is good protocol hygiene.

gamefi-developer.title: Layer 2 for GameFi - Low Gas and Fast Finality
gamefi-developer.description: Deploy game contracts on a Layer 2 network to reduce gas costs for high-frequency game transactions. Implement asset bridging between mainnet and L2.
topics: Gas cost comparison L1 vs L2
L2 transaction finality and sequencer trust
gamefi-developer.challenge: Redeploy your game contracts to Polygon Mumbai, test all game flows, and implement a UI showing gas cost savings compared to Ethereum mainnet.

children.title: Why L2 Is Mandatory for High-Frequency Game Transactions
children.description: A single Ethereum mainnet transaction costs several dollars of gas; a game that processes fifty transactions per play session would require hundreds of dollars per player per day in fees. Layer 2 networks reduce this to fractions of a cent, making the economics of on-chain game state viable.

children.title: Deploying to Polygon PoS
children.description: Polygon PoS is EVM-compatible, meaning existing Hardhat and Foundry deployments work without contract code changes. The primary deployment change is pointing the RPC URL to the Polygon node and funding the deployer wallet with MATIC instead of ETH.

resources.label: Hardhat Docs — Deploying to a Live Network

children.title: Official Polygon Bridge for NFTs
children.description: The Polygon native bridge uses a lock-and-mint model: NFTs are locked on Ethereum mainnet and equivalent tokens minted on Polygon. Without understanding this model, developers incorrectly assume the same token address exists on both chains, causing asset display bugs and failed marketplace listings.

children.title: Arbitrum for Low-Latency Game Actions
children.description: Arbitrum's optimistic rollup provides near-instant transaction confirmation from the sequencer while inheriting Ethereum mainnet security for settlements. For games where fast feedback is critical — fighting games, real-time strategy — Arbitrum's sub-second soft finality is preferable to Polygon's separate consensus.

children.title: Chain ID Awareness in Game Contracts
children.description: A contract deployed on Polygon and one on Ethereum mainnet at the same address are completely independent; ownership records do not sync. A game client that queries Ethereum mainnet for an NFT owned on Polygon will always show zero balance, appearing as if all assets were lost.

children.title: L2 Transaction Finality and Sequencer Trust
children.description: Optimistic rollups like Arbitrum have a 7-day challenge window before L1 finality; during this period the sequencer can theoretically reorder transactions. Games that treat soft finality as irreversible without understanding this trust assumption may allow exploits during the challenge period.

children.title: Displaying Gas Savings to Players
children.description: Showing players the ETH equivalent cost of their L2 transactions versus mainnet demonstrates the value of the L2 architecture and educates the community. Projects that do not communicate this leave players questioning whether the game is using a real blockchain or a centralized server dressed up as Web3.

children.title: MATIC and ARB Token Onboarding
children.description: Players need the native L2 gas token to pay for transactions; a game that assumes players already hold MATIC creates a significant onboarding barrier. Integrating a fiat-to-crypto on-ramp or gasless relayer for new players removes this friction at the cost of a small per-transaction subsidy.

gamefi-developer.title: GameFi Security and Anti-Cheat
gamefi-developer.description: Audit GameFi contracts for exploits including reward manipulation, NFT duplication bugs, and oracle gaming. Implement server-signed score submission for anti-cheat.
topics: Sybil attack resistance with per-wallet limits
Flash loan attacks on staking rewards
gamefi-developer.challenge: Implement server-signed score submission so only scores approved by the game server can trigger token rewards, and write a Foundry test demonstrating replay protection.

children.title: Server-Signed Score Submission
children.description: Off-chain game servers should sign score results with a private key, and the reward contract should verify the signature before minting tokens. Without this, any player can call the reward function directly with an arbitrarily high score, draining the entire reward pool in a single transaction.

children.title: Replay Protection with Nonces
children.description: A signed score message without a nonce can be replayed indefinitely to claim the same reward multiple times. Each signed message must include a per-player nonce that the contract increments on use, and the contract must reject any message whose nonce has already been consumed.

children.title: Flash Loan Attacks on Staking Rewards
children.description: An attacker can borrow a large amount of game tokens via flash loan, stake them to accrue rewards in the same block, claim those rewards, and repay the loan — all atomically. Adding a minimum staking duration of at least one block eliminates this attack vector entirely.

children.title: Sybil Attack Resistance
children.description: A single actor operating hundreds of wallets can claim per-wallet bonuses repeatedly, effectively multiplying their reward rate. Linking in-game progress to NFT ownership — rather than raw wallet addresses — makes Sybil attacks expensive because each wallet would need its own NFT.

children.title: Slither Static Analysis for Game Contracts
children.description: Slither detects common vulnerabilities like unchecked return values, arbitrary mints, and dangerous delegatecall usage in seconds without deploying a contract. Running Slither before any testnet deployment catches the majority of critical vulnerabilities that human review misses under time pressure.

resources.label: Trail of Bits — Smart Contract Security Blog

children.title: Immunefi Bug Bounty for GameFi Projects
children.description: Launching a bug bounty program on Immunefi before mainnet invites white-hat researchers to find vulnerabilities for a reward rather than exploiting them for profit. A $50,000 bounty ceiling is inexpensive compared to the reputational and financial damage of a live exploit against a game with real player assets.

resources.label: SWC Registry — Smart Contract Weakness Classification

children.title: Transparent vs UUPS Proxy Patterns
children.description: Upgradeable proxies let the team patch security bugs in live game contracts, but they also let a compromised admin drain all player funds by pointing the proxy at a malicious implementation. A timelock on all upgrades with a 48-hour delay gives players time to withdraw before a malicious upgrade executes.

children.title: Storage Collision in Upgrades
children.description: Changing the storage layout between proxy implementations by inserting a new variable before an existing one silently corrupts all existing player data by shifting every subsequent storage slot. Using OpenZeppelin's storage gap pattern in base contracts prevents this class of catastrophic upgrade bug.

gamefi-developer.title: Mainnet Launch and Live Game Operations
gamefi-developer.description: Launch your GameFi project to mainnet with verified contracts, a production Unity WebGL build, and operational tooling for monitoring player activity and protocol health.
topics: Tenderly alerts for reward contract events
gamefi-developer.challenge: Ship a playable GameFi demo on Polygon mainnet with at least one NFT asset, one earn mechanic, and a Tenderly alert monitoring the reward pool balance.

children.title: Mainnet Deployment Checklist for Games
children.description: A GameFi mainnet launch requires verifying contracts on Polygonscan, configuring Chainlink VRF subscriptions with sufficient LINK float, and confirming that all admin roles point to the multisig rather than the deployer EOA. Skipping any item on this checklist has historically led to exploits within hours of launch.

children.title: Gnosis Safe Treasury for Game Revenue
children.description: Game revenue from marketplace fees, minting, and staking must flow to a multisig treasury rather than a single EOA to prevent a single point of failure. Gnosis Safe allows threshold-based approval so no single team member can unilaterally move funds.

children.title: Unity WebGL Compression and Load Time
children.description: Production WebGL builds should enable Brotli compression and code stripping to achieve load times under 10 seconds on a typical broadband connection. A 150 MB uncompressed build will lose 80% of players during the loading screen before any blockchain interaction begins.

resources.label: Unity Manual — WebGL Deploying and Running

children.title: CDN Hosting for WebGL Game Assets
children.description: Serving Unity WebGL assets from a CDN with correct MIME types and CORS headers ensures fast global load times and prevents browser security blocks. Hosting directly from an S3 bucket without CORS headers breaks wallet injection because the browser treats cross-origin scripts as untrusted.

children.title: Tenderly Alerts for Reward Contract Events
children.description: Configuring Tenderly alerts on RewardClaimed and PoolDrained events lets the team detect abnormal claim rates that indicate a reward-manipulation exploit within seconds rather than hours. Without automated alerting, exploits that drain the entire reward pool often go unnoticed until a player reports a missing balance.

children.title: Emergency Pause and Circuit Breakers
children.description: Every game contract that holds or mints value should implement OpenZeppelin's Pausable pattern, allowing the team to halt all state changes within seconds of detecting an exploit. A game that cannot pause is forced to choose between an emergency contract upgrade — which takes hours — and watching an attacker drain the treasury in real time.

children.title: On-Chain Analytics for Player Activity
children.description: Tracking daily active wallets, item mint rates, and reward claim volumes from contract events in a subgraph gives the team real data to tune tokenomics and detect anomalies. Projects that rely on Discord reports to detect abuse are always hours behind attackers who automate their exploits.

children.title: Timelock-Gated Parameter Changes
children.description: Changing emission rates, item drop probabilities, or marketplace fees through a timelocked governance proposal gives players at least 48 hours to react before the change takes effect. Unannounced instant parameter changes — even beneficial ones — consistently trigger community panic and mass token sell-offs.

dao-developer.title: Web, Blockchain, and DAO Fundamentals
dao-developer.description: Understand what a DAO is, how on-chain governance works, and why token-weighted voting enables decentralized decision-making. Survey major DAO platforms and patterns.
topics: What is a DAO and why it exists
Major DAO platforms - Compound, Uniswap, MakerDAO
dao-developer.challenge: Compare the governance parameters of Compound, Uniswap, and one other DAO: quorum, proposal threshold, voting period, and timelock delay.

children.title: Token-Weighted Voting
children.description: Token-weighted voting is the backbone of most on-chain DAOs, where one token equals one vote. Misunderstanding its plutocratic tendencies leads to governance capture by large holders and proposals that serve a minority of the community.

children.title: Quorum and Proposal Thresholds
children.description: Quorum ensures decisions have enough participation to be legitimate, while proposal thresholds prevent spam by requiring a minimum token balance to submit. Setting either too high or too low leads to governance paralysis or easy attacks.

children.title: On-Chain Execution via Timelock
children.description: On-chain governance enforces that only passed proposals can trigger contract state changes, eliminating trusted intermediaries. Without understanding timelocks and execution delays, a DAO can be drained or exploited the moment a malicious proposal passes.

children.title: Major DAO Platforms
children.description: Studying live DAOs like Compound, Uniswap, and MakerDAO reveals how governance parameters are tuned in production. Ignoring their design decisions means repeating mistakes that have already caused governance failures and treasury losses.

children.title: Why DAOs Exist
children.description: DAOs replace centralized coordinators with smart contract rules, enabling trustless collaboration at scale. Without internalizing this purpose, developers build governance systems that recreate the same single points of failure they were meant to eliminate.

children.title: Governance Trade-offs
children.description: Every governance parameter — voting period, quorum, timelock — represents a trade-off between security, participation, and agility. Failing to understand these trade-offs results in DAOs that are either ungovernable or trivially attackable.

children.title: Proposal Lifecycle States
children.description: A Governor proposal moves through Pending, Active, Succeeded, Queued, and Executed states — each with distinct time windows. Not knowing these states causes developers to mis-time calls and ship UIs that show incorrect proposal status to users.

children.title: Governance Analytics
children.description: Platforms like Tally and DeepDAO expose real participation rates, delegate rankings, and treasury flows across major DAOs. Tracking this data is essential for benchmarking your own DAO parameters against battle-tested deployments.

dao-developer.title: Governance Tokens - ERC-20 with Voting
dao-developer.description: Implement an ERC-20 governance token with vote delegation using OpenZeppelin ERC20Votes. Understand checkpointing and why vote snapshots prevent double voting.
topics: Governance token vs utility token tradeoffs
dao-developer.challenge: Deploy an ERC-20Votes token, test delegation between 3 accounts, and assert that getPastVotes returns correct historical balances at specific block numbers.

children.title: Vote Delegation
children.description: ERC-20Votes requires token holders to self-delegate before their balance counts as voting power — an invisible footgun that leaves governance inactive if documentation omits it. Understanding delegation is also critical for liquid governance delegation markets.

children.title: Vote Checkpointing
children.description: Vote balance checkpoints record historical balances at specific blocks so that proposals snapshot voting power at the moment they are created, not when votes are cast. Missing this mechanism allows attackers to buy tokens after a proposal is created and vote with them.

children.title: Governance vs Utility Tokens
children.description: A governance token must balance voting power distribution with economic incentives — conflating it with a pure utility token creates misaligned incentives where large holders vote to maximize their yield rather than protocol health. Choosing the right model at launch is hard to reverse.

children.title: getPastVotes and Snapshot Safety
children.description: getPastVotes retrieves a past checkpoint balance and is the function Governors call to validate voting power at proposal creation time. Bugs in how you call or cache this value can silently allow ineligible participants to vote.

children.title: Preventing Vote Manipulation
children.description: Without checkpointing, an attacker can buy tokens, vote on an existing proposal, and sell tokens in the same block — a flash-loan governance attack. The ERC-20Votes checkpoint model is the primary defense against this class of exploit.

resources.label: Governance Attack Case Studies - Rekt News

children.title: Permit-Based Delegation
children.description: ERC-20Permit lets users sign off-chain delegations without paying gas, dramatically lowering the barrier to participation for passive token holders. Omitting permit support means users must send two transactions to participate in governance, reducing turnout.

children.title: Initial Supply and Vesting
children.description: The initial token supply distribution and vesting schedules determine voting power concentration from day one. Poorly designed distributions result in core team wallets controlling quorum indefinitely, which undermines the legitimacy of every subsequent vote.

children.title: Testing Delegation Flows
children.description: Delegation state is stateful and order-dependent — testing it requires carefully sequencing delegate, transfer, and getPastVotes calls across multiple blocks. Skipping these tests means subtle governance bugs only surface on mainnet when real value is at stake.

dao-developer.title: OpenZeppelin Governor Framework
dao-developer.description: Build a complete on-chain governance system using OpenZeppelin Governor with quorum, voting period, and a timelock controller for delayed execution.
topics: Proposal lifecycle - Pending, Active, Succeeded, Queued, Executed
Proposing and voting in Hardhat tests

resources.label: Build an On-Chain DAO - Patrick Collins

dao-developer.challenge: Deploy a Governor and TimelockController, write a full test that creates a proposal, passes it through voting, queues it, and executes a parameter change.

children.title: Governor Module Composition
children.description: OpenZeppelin Governor is built from composable mixins — GovernorVotes, GovernorQuorumFraction, GovernorTimelockControl — and choosing the wrong combination produces a Governor that silently skips quorum checks or executes without a timelock. Understanding each module's responsibility is non-negotiable before deploying.

children.title: Proposal Description Hash
children.description: The proposal ID is derived from the targets, values, calldatas, and the keccak256 of the description — any mismatch between creation and execution parameters causes the transaction to revert. Getting this hash wrong is one of the most common Governor integration bugs.

resources.label: Build an On-Chain DAO - Patrick Collins

children.title: Timelock Configuration
children.description: The TimelockController enforces a minimum delay between a proposal passing and its execution, giving the community time to exit if they disagree with the outcome. Misconfiguring PROPOSER and EXECUTOR roles allows any address to queue or execute arbitrary calls.

children.title: Role Management in Timelock
children.description: TimelockController uses AccessControl with PROPOSER, EXECUTOR, and CANCELLER roles — the Governor contract must hold PROPOSER and EXECUTOR, not an EOA. Assigning these roles to individual wallets reintroduces centralization that the DAO was designed to eliminate.

children.title: Pending to Active Transition
children.description: A proposal starts in Pending state for a configurable voting delay — the block at which it becomes Active is the snapshot block for voting power. Developers who bypass this delay in tests end up with incorrect voting power snapshots that mask real bugs.

resources.label: Build an On-Chain DAO - Patrick Collins

children.title: Queue and Execute Flow
children.description: After a proposal succeeds it must be queued in the TimelockController before execution — skipping the queue step causes execution to revert, and missing the timelock window requires re-queuing. Testing the full Pending-Active-Succeeded-Queued-Executed path is the only way to verify the entire system works.

children.title: Time Manipulation in Tests
children.description: Governor tests require advancing block numbers and timestamps to move proposals through voting delay and voting period — Hardhat provides mine and time.increase helpers for this. Tests that don't advance time never reach the Active or Succeeded states and give false confidence.

children.title: Voting Against and Abstain
children.description: OpenZeppelin Governor supports For, Against, and Abstain votes — all three affect quorum calculations differently depending on the GovernorQuorumFraction configuration. Only testing For votes leaves Against and Abstain accounting bugs undetected.

resources.label: Build an On-Chain DAO - Patrick Collins

dao-developer.title: Snapshot - Off-Chain Voting
dao-developer.description: Integrate Snapshot for gasless off-chain voting using signed messages stored on IPFS. Understand when off-chain voting is appropriate and how to build a custom Snapshot strategy.
topics: How Snapshot stores votes on IPFS
Snapshot and on-chain execution via SafeSnap
dao-developer.challenge: Create a Snapshot space for a test DAO, configure a custom token strategy, and simulate a proposal vote using the Snapshot SDK.

children.title: Snapshot Space Configuration
children.description: A Snapshot space defines the voting strategies, proposal validation rules, and IPFS storage settings for a DAO's off-chain votes. Misconfiguring strategies — such as pointing to the wrong token address — silently gives all token holders zero voting power and makes every vote invalid.

children.title: EIP-712 Signatures in Snapshot
children.description: Snapshot votes are EIP-712 signed messages stored on IPFS — the signed data includes the proposal ID, choice, and timestamp, making them verifiable off-chain without gas. Understanding this structure is essential for building custom voting integrations or verifying vote authenticity programmatically.

children.title: Token and Delegation Strategies
children.description: Snapshot strategies compute voting power from on-chain state at the proposal's snapshot block — built-in strategies cover ERC-20 balances, delegation, NFT holdings, and LP positions. Using the wrong strategy type means the community votes with incorrect power, potentially invalidating the result.

children.title: SafeSnap On-Chain Execution
children.description: SafeSnap bridges Snapshot off-chain votes to on-chain execution via an Optimistic Reality oracle, enabling gasless voting with real contract execution. Without this bridge, off-chain votes are purely advisory and require a trusted party to manually execute outcomes.

children.title: Gas Cost vs Finality Trade-off
children.description: Off-chain voting with Snapshot eliminates gas barriers for participation but trades cryptographic finality for social consensus — a sufficiently motivated team can ignore vote results. Choosing between on-chain and off-chain voting requires understanding what level of trust your DAO places in its core contributors.

children.title: Hybrid Governance Patterns
children.description: Many mature DAOs use Snapshot for temperature checks and signal gathering while reserving on-chain Governor for binding execution — this hybrid model maximizes participation while preserving trustless enforcement. Implementing it well requires both systems to use the same voting power source at matching snapshot blocks.

children.title: Programmatic Proposal Creation
children.description: The Snapshot.js SDK allows developers to create proposals and cast votes programmatically, enabling DAO tooling, bots, and integrations without requiring browser-based interaction. Not knowing the SDK means relying entirely on the Snapshot UI, which blocks automation use cases.

children.title: Querying Snapshot with GraphQL
children.description: Snapshot exposes a GraphQL API for querying spaces, proposals, and votes — this is the correct way to build governance dashboards that show off-chain vote history alongside on-chain data. Polling the IPFS gateway directly instead leads to inconsistent results and rate limiting.

dao-developer.title: DAO Treasury Management
dao-developer.description: Build and manage a DAO treasury contract that holds ETH and tokens, executes expenditures only through governance votes, and reports on allocations.
dao-developer.challenge: Set up a Gnosis Safe as a DAO treasury, configure a 3-of-5 multisig, and execute a test fund transfer via a passed Governor proposal.

children.title: Governance-Gated Fund Releases
children.description: Treasury funds should only be moveable through a passed governance proposal routed through the TimelockController — any direct admin key bypasses this guarantee and exposes the treasury to a single point of failure. Designing the permission graph correctly from deployment is critical because it is hard to change after funds arrive.

children.title: Treasury Contract Patterns
children.description: A dedicated treasury contract holds ETH and ERC-20 tokens separately from the Governor logic, making upgrades and audits cleaner. Mixing treasury management with governance logic creates contracts that are difficult to audit and risky to upgrade.

children.title: Gnosis Safe as DAO Treasury
children.description: Gnosis Safe is the industry standard for DAO treasury management because it provides multi-sig security, module support, and direct integration with governance systems via the SafeSnap module. Operating a treasury without multi-sig means a single compromised key can drain all funds.

children.title: Approval Thresholds and Signer Selection
children.description: Choosing the right M-of-N threshold for a multi-sig treasury balances execution speed against attack resistance — a 2-of-3 is fast but fragile, while a 7-of-9 is secure but slows emergency responses. Documenting the rationale for your threshold is as important as the setting itself.

children.title: Budget Proposals and Categories
children.description: Well-run DAOs define spending categories — grants, operations, development, marketing — and require proposals to reference a category and remaining budget. Without structured budget proposals, treasury spending becomes opaque and community confidence erodes.

children.title: Treasury Diversification
children.description: Holding 100% of treasury assets in the native governance token creates catastrophic risk — a token price drop wipes out the runway needed to pay contributors and operate the protocol. Diversification into stablecoins and blue-chip assets is a governance decision that requires careful on-chain execution.

children.title: On-Chain Balance Reporting
children.description: Providing real-time on-chain treasury balance reports — queryable via subgraph or direct contract calls — builds community trust and enables data-driven spending decisions. DAOs that rely on manual spreadsheets for reporting introduce human error and undermine the transparency DAOs promise.

children.title: Gnosis Safe Modules
children.description: Safe modules extend the Safe with custom logic — for example a spending allowance module for recurring grants or a Reality module for Snapshot-triggered execution. Not knowing Safe's module system means rebuilding functionality that already exists and is audited.

dao-developer.title: Aragon Framework
dao-developer.description: Build a DAO using the Aragon OSx framework with modular plugins for voting, permissions, and treasury. Understand how Aragon separates DAO core from governance logic.
topics: Aragon App for no-code DAO management

resources.label: Build a DAO with Aragon - YouTube

dao-developer.challenge: Deploy an Aragon OSx DAO on a testnet with the TokenVoting plugin, create a proposal via SDK, and execute it through the governance flow.

children.title: DAO and Plugin Separation
children.description: Aragon OSx separates the immutable DAO core — which holds assets and manages permissions — from upgradeable governance plugins like TokenVoting. This architecture means governance logic can be swapped without migrating funds, a critical capability that monolithic DAO contracts lack entirely.

children.title: Permission Management with ACL
children.description: Aragon's access control list grants and revokes permissions that govern who can call which functions on which contracts — every governance action is ultimately a permission check. Misconfiguring the ACL at deploy time can lock a DAO out of its own treasury or allow anyone to install malicious plugins.

children.title: Plugin Installation and Configuration
children.description: Plugins are installed through the DAO's plugin manager and require a matching PluginSetup contract that defines which permissions to grant — installing a plugin incorrectly leaves it unable to execute proposals. Understanding the setup contract pattern is necessary for building and auditing Aragon-based DAOs.

children.title: Upgrading Plugins
children.description: Aragon OSx uses UUPS upgradeable proxies for plugins, meaning governance logic can be upgraded via a proposal without redeploying the DAO or migrating funds. Not understanding UUPS upgrade safety leads to storage collisions that corrupt all DAO state during an upgrade.

children.title: Creating Proposals via SDK
children.description: The Aragon SDK provides a TypeScript client for creating proposals, casting votes, and executing actions without manually encoding calldata — reducing the chance of encoding bugs that cause proposals to silently fail on execution. Skipping the SDK and encoding manually introduces an entire class of hard-to-test bugs.

resources.label: Build a DAO with Aragon - YouTube

children.title: Aragon App No-Code Interface
children.description: The Aragon App provides a no-code DAO management interface used by non-technical community members to participate in governance — understanding its UX is essential for configuring your DAO in a way that matches what community members will actually see. DAOs that ignore this often ship governance flows that confuse participants.

children.title: Choosing the Right Framework
children.description: OpenZeppelin Governor is simpler but monolithic — once deployed its logic is fixed unless you add a proxy. Aragon OSx adds upgrade flexibility and plugin composability at the cost of more complex deployment and auditing surface area. Choosing the wrong framework at launch means either living with constraints or a costly migration.

children.title: Testnet Deployment Workflow
children.description: Deploying and testing a full Aragon OSx DAO on a testnet requires understanding the DAO factory, plugin registry, and plugin setup contracts — deploying them in the wrong order results in a DAO that can't install any plugins. The Aragon SDK handles this sequence for you if you follow its deployment guide.

dao-developer.title: DAO Frontend and Governance UI
dao-developer.description: Build a governance dashboard that shows proposals, voting power, delegation status, and treasury balances. Query on-chain governance data from a subgraph.
topics: Transaction feedback for vote and delegate
dao-developer.challenge: Build a governance dashboard showing all active proposals with vote counts, a delegate button, and a treasury balance panel, sourcing data from a subgraph.

children.title: Displaying Proposals and Vote Counts
children.description: A governance dashboard must show proposal state, vote totals, quorum progress, and time remaining in a way that non-technical community members can act on — unclear vote status is the number one cause of low participation. Building this correctly requires querying both the Governor contract directly and a subgraph for historical proposals.

children.title: Voting Power and Delegation UI
children.description: Users need to see their current voting power, who they have delegated to, and how to redelegate — without this information they cannot effectively participate in governance. Missing delegation UI is the primary reason users hold tokens but never vote.

children.title: Indexing Governor Events
children.description: Governor contracts emit ProposalCreated, VoteCast, ProposalQueued, and ProposalExecuted events — a subgraph maps these into queryable entities that power paginated proposal lists and vote histories. Reading raw events from JSON-RPC instead becomes extremely slow as proposal count grows into the hundreds.

children.title: Treasury Balance Display
children.description: Treasury balance panels must aggregate ETH and multiple ERC-20 holdings with current USD prices — doing this entirely via JSON-RPC requires one call per token and is prone to rate limiting. A subgraph combined with a price feed aggregator provides a scalable and consistent approach.

children.title: Vote and Delegate Transaction Feedback
children.description: Governance transactions — castVote and delegate — must provide clear pending, confirmed, and error states because users often vote close to the voting period deadline and retrying a failed vote may be impossible. Poor transaction feedback causes users to double-submit or walk away believing their vote counted when it did not.

children.title: Proposal Creation Form
children.description: Proposal creation requires encoding target addresses, calldata, and values correctly before submitting to the Governor — a single byte error makes the proposal unexecutable even if it passes a vote. The UI must validate all inputs and preview the decoded action before the user signs.

children.title: Next.js and wagmi Setup
children.description: Governance dashboards benefit from server-side rendering for proposal lists — users without a connected wallet should still see proposals — which requires a careful split between SSR data fetching and client-side wallet interactions. Mixing both in client components breaks SSR and causes layout shifts.

children.title: Tally Integration
children.description: Tally provides a ready-made governance UI and API that many DAOs embed rather than building from scratch — integrating via Tally API saves months of frontend development while providing battle-tested UX. Understanding Tally's data model is also useful for building custom dashboards that complement the Tally interface.

dao-developer.title: Advanced Voting Mechanisms
dao-developer.description: Explore advanced voting mechanisms beyond token-weighted majority voting. Implement quadratic voting to reduce plutocracy and conviction voting for continuous preference expression.
dao-developer.challenge: Implement a quadratic voting contract where votes cost the square of the voting power spent, and write a test showing it reduces large-token-holder dominance.

children.title: Quadratic Voting
children.description: Quadratic voting charges a cost in voting credits equal to the square of the votes cast, reducing the marginal influence of large token holders and giving smaller holders proportionally more voice. Implementing it without a robust Sybil resistance mechanism defeats its purpose entirely — one large holder can split into many wallets and regain linear power.

children.title: Conviction Voting
children.description: Conviction voting accumulates voting power over time as tokens are locked behind a proposal — the longer tokens stay committed, the more conviction builds, filtering out low-quality proposals. Not understanding the conviction curve math leads to implementations where proposals can never reach the execution threshold or always pass immediately.

children.title: Rage Quit for Minority Protection
children.description: Rage quit lets dissenting members exit with their proportional share of treasury assets before a passed proposal executes — it is the primary protection for minority stakeholders against governance capture. Without rage quit, minority holders are locked into decisions they opposed and cannot recover their capital.

children.title: Optimistic Approval Patterns
children.description: Optimistic approval assumes a proposal is valid unless challenged within a dispute window, dramatically speeding up low-contention decisions. Misunderstanding the challenge window leads to systems where disputes arrive after execution has already occurred, making them unenforceable.

children.title: Identity Solutions for Quadratic Voting
children.description: Quadratic voting only reduces plutocracy if each participant is a unique human — without identity verification, Sybil attackers create many wallets to regain linear power at a lower cost. Integrating Gitcoin Passport, Proof of Humanity, or Worldcoin ID is the minimum viable defense.

children.title: Delegation Trees
children.description: Vote delegation trees allow indirect delegation — A delegates to B who delegates to C — creating liquid democracy where non-active participants channel power to trusted experts. Cycles in the delegation graph cause infinite loops that can brick voting power accounting if not detected and prevented at contract level.

children.title: Square Root Vote Calculation
children.description: Implementing quadratic voting in Solidity requires integer square root approximation because the EVM has no native sqrt — using the wrong algorithm produces systematic rounding errors that accumulate across many votes and can shift an outcome. Always benchmark your integer sqrt implementation against known test vectors.

children.title: Vote Credit Accounting
children.description: Quadratic voting requires tracking a separate credit balance per voter per proposal — reusing the token balance directly is incorrect and allows top-up attacks mid-vote. The credit balance must be snapshotted at proposal creation and decremented as votes are cast.

dao-developer.title: DAO Security and Attack Vectors
dao-developer.description: Study governance-specific attack vectors including flash loan governance takeovers, proposal front-running, and timelock bypasses. Harden your DAO contracts against each.
topics: Slither and Foundry fuzzing for governance

resources.label: Governance Attack Case Studies - Rekt News
Secure DAO Governance - Trail of Bits

dao-developer.challenge: Write a Foundry test that demonstrates a flash loan governance attack on a vulnerable Governor, then patch the contract and prove the attack fails.

children.title: Flash Loan Governance Attacks
children.description: An attacker can borrow a massive token supply via flash loan, create a proposal, manipulate governance, and repay the loan in a single transaction — all within one block. The fix is using historical vote snapshots via ERC-20Votes, but any Governor that reads live balances instead of past checkpoints is immediately vulnerable.

resources.label: Governance Attack Case Studies - Rekt News
Secure DAO Governance - Trail of Bits

children.title: Proposal Front-Running and Griefing
children.description: An attacker can front-run a legitimate proposal by submitting an identical one first, causing the legitimate proposer's transaction to revert on duplicate ID — or cancel it if the attacker has canceller role. Protecting against this requires unique descriptions and carefully scoping the CANCELLER role.

resources.label: Secure DAO Governance - Trail of Bits

children.title: Timelock Bypass Scenarios
children.description: If a Governor's executor role is misconfigured to allow arbitrary addresses, an attacker can queue and execute transactions without a passed proposal — bypassing all governance entirely. The TimelockController's EXECUTOR role should be set to the zero address to allow anyone to execute, or to a specific address that enforces proposal passage.

resources.label: Governance Attack Case Studies - Rekt News

children.title: Proxy Upgrade via Governance Attack
children.description: If a DAO governs an upgradeable proxy, a successful governance attack immediately translates to complete protocol takeover — the attacker can replace implementation logic to drain all funds. DAOs that govern upgradeable contracts must have the highest quorum and longest timelock parameters of any governance action.

resources.label: Secure DAO Governance - Trail of Bits

children.title: Slither for Governance Contracts
children.description: Slither has governance-specific detectors that flag missing access controls, incorrect timelock configurations, and delegate call dangers — running it before testnet deployment catches a class of bugs that manual review routinely misses. Fixing all high-severity Slither findings is the minimum bar before any governance contract goes live.

resources.label: Secure DAO Governance - Trail of Bits

children.title: Foundry Fuzzing for Governance
children.description: Fuzzing Governor contracts with random proposal targets and calldatas surfaces edge cases in proposal ID collision, voting accounting overflows, and execution reentrancy that deterministic tests never reach. A governance contract with less than 10,000 fuzz runs on its critical paths should not be deployed to mainnet.

children.title: Quorum and Threshold Manipulation Defense
children.description: An attacker can manipulate quorum by burning tokens from circulation or acquiring tokens to push past the proposal threshold at a favorable block — governance parameters must account for realistic token distribution scenarios including whale consolidation. Dynamic quorum that adjusts to circulating supply is more robust than fixed token counts.

resources.label: Governance Attack Case Studies - Rekt News

children.title: Emergency Pause Mechanisms
children.description: A guardian multisig with the ability to cancel dangerous proposals before execution is a necessary safety valve in the early days of a DAO when governance parameters are being calibrated. The guardian's power must be scoped narrowly to cancel-only — not execute — to avoid reintroducing centralization.

dao-developer.title: Mainnet DAO Launch and Operations
dao-developer.description: Launch your DAO on Ethereum mainnet with token distribution, governance parameters, treasury setup, and community onboarding. Establish operational workflows for proposals and execution.
dao-developer.challenge: Deploy your complete DAO to mainnet with token distribution to 5 test delegates, pass a real proposal through full governance flow, and write an operational runbook.

children.title: Token Distribution and Genesis Event
children.description: The initial token distribution — vesting schedules, airdrop criteria, team allocations — determines voting power concentration for years and is nearly impossible to reverse without another governance vote. A poorly designed genesis event results in a DAO where founders or VCs permanently control quorum, which undermines the legitimacy of every subsequent proposal.

children.title: Mainnet Governor and Timelock Deployment
children.description: Governor and TimelockController must be deployed with correct role assignments from the very first transaction — there is no safe window to fix role misconfiguration after other users have started delegating and creating proposals. Using a deployment checklist and a fork-based rehearsal is the minimum bar for mainnet.

children.title: Tally Integration for Live DAOs
children.description: Tally automatically picks up any OpenZeppelin Governor deployment and provides a full proposal management UI, delegate registry, and governance analytics — integrating Tally from day one saves building these features yourself and immediately makes the DAO accessible to non-technical participants.

children.title: Community Proposal Documentation
children.description: A well-documented proposal process — including a forum post template, off-chain temperature check requirement, and minimum discussion period before on-chain submission — dramatically improves governance quality and reduces spam proposals. DAOs without documented processes experience chaotic governance that discourages serious contributors.

children.title: DAO Legal Wrappers
children.description: Without a legal wrapper — such as a Wyoming DAO LLC, Marshall Islands DAO, or Cayman foundation — DAO members can be personally liable for the DAO's actions in many jurisdictions. Choosing the right legal structure at launch protects contributors and enables the DAO to sign contracts and open bank accounts.

children.title: Treasury Diversification Strategy
children.description: Launching with 100% of treasury in the native token creates runway risk — a price drop can wipe out all operating capital before the DAO passes a diversification proposal. Encoding a treasury diversification plan into the launch proposal prevents the need for a contentious governance vote during a market downturn.

children.title: Operational Runbook
children.description: A DAO operations runbook documents who monitors the Governor for proposals, what the escalation path is for emergency situations, and how contributors get paid — without it, coordination happens informally and critical proposals get missed. The runbook should be a living governance document itself, updatable only through a passed proposal.

children.title: Monitoring and Incident Response
children.description: Tenderly alerts on Governor and TimelockController events — ProposalCreated, ProposalQueued, and CallExecuted — give the team early warning when unexpected governance activity occurs. Without monitoring, a malicious proposal can be queued and executed before anyone in the community notices.

zk-developer.title: Web, Math, and ZK Fundamentals
zk-developer.description: Build the mathematical and conceptual foundation for zero-knowledge proofs. Understand finite fields, polynomial commitments, and the intuition behind what ZK proofs prove.

resources.label: Proofs, Arguments, and Zero Knowledge - Justin Thaler

zk-developer.challenge: Write a plain-language explanation of how a ZK-SNARK allows proving knowledge of a secret without revealing it, using an analogy of your choice.

children.title: Completeness, Soundness, and Zero-Knowledge
children.description: These three properties define whether a proof system is actually secure. A system missing soundness lets cheaters forge proofs; one missing zero-knowledge leaks the secret. Every design decision in a ZK protocol traces back to preserving all three simultaneously.

children.title: Trusted Setup and Toxic Waste
children.description: Groth16 requires a per-circuit trusted setup where the randomness must be destroyed afterward — if it survives, anyone can forge proofs. Understanding why ceremonies exist and what MPC-based ceremonies mitigate is essential before deploying any production ZK system.

resources.label: Proofs, Arguments, and ZK - Justin Thaler

children.title: Finite Fields and Modular Arithmetic
children.description: ZK circuits operate over finite fields — all values wrap at a prime modulus. If you forget this and assume normal integer behavior, circuits silently accept invalid witnesses. Knowing the field size of bn128 or bls12-381 prevents overflow bugs that an auditor would flag immediately.

children.title: Polynomial Representations of Computation
children.description: Computation is encoded as polynomial constraints via QAP or PLONK's gate system. Misunderstanding this makes it impossible to reason about why a circuit is sound or where constraint gaps exist — both of which are at the core of every ZK circuit audit.

children.title: Interactive vs Non-Interactive Proofs
children.description: Interactive proofs require a live verifier challenge, making them unsuitable for blockchains. The Fiat-Shamir heuristic converts them to non-interactive form, which is how every on-chain ZK-SNARK works. Skipping this connection makes it hard to understand why a random oracle is modeled in the proof.

children.title: ZK-SNARKs vs ZK-STARKs
children.description: SNARKs are small and cheap to verify on-chain but rely on elliptic-curve pairings that quantum computers could break; STARKs are larger but post-quantum secure and require no trusted setup. Choosing the wrong one for your threat model can cost thousands in gas or expose future cryptographic risk.

children.title: ZK Learning Resources and Community
children.description: ZK is a fast-moving field where papers lag months behind tooling. Knowing where the active research community publishes — 0xPARC, ZKProof, IACR ePrint — lets you evaluate new proving systems before they are widely documented and avoid relying on outdated tutorials.

children.title: Elliptic Curves in ZK Systems
children.description: Groth16 uses the BN254 pairing-friendly curve; Barretenberg uses BN254 and BLS12-381; STARKs avoid curves altogether. Confusing which curve a proving system uses leads to mismatched field sizes between your circuit and your on-chain verifier, which produces incorrect proofs that are still accepted.

zk-developer.title: Circom - Arithmetic Circuit Language
zk-developer.description: Learn Circom to express computation as arithmetic circuits with rank-1 constraint systems. Write your first circuits and compile them to witness computation artifacts.
zk-developer.challenge: Write a Circom circuit that proves knowledge of two numbers a and b such that a * b = c without revealing a or b, and generate a valid proof.

children.title: Signals, Constraints, and the R1CS Model
children.description: Circom distinguishes between signals (values) and constraints (relationships). Writing an assignment without a constraint compiles fine but creates an under-constrained circuit that accepts forged proofs. Every Circom developer must internalize that <== constrains while --> only assigns.

children.title: Templates and Component Composition
children.description: Circom circuits are built by composing reusable templates — analogous to functions in normal code. Without understanding how component instantiation passes signals, it is easy to wire inputs incorrectly and produce circuits that constrain the wrong relationships, bypassing the intended proof logic.

children.title: Compiling Circuits and Generating Witnesses
children.description: A circuit only produces a proof when a valid witness — assignments to all signals satisfying all constraints — is provided. Misunderstanding the compile-to-witness pipeline means debugging failed proofs without knowing whether the constraint system or the witness generator is at fault.

children.title: circomlib Reusable Circuit Components
children.description: circomlib provides audited components for common operations: Poseidon hashing, Merkle proofs, bit decomposition, and comparators. Re-implementing these from scratch introduces soundness bugs that the community has already discovered and fixed in the library.

children.title: Rank-1 Constraint Systems (R1CS)
children.description: Circom compiles to R1CS, a matrix format where each constraint is expressed as (A · w) * (B · w) = (C · w). Developers who skip this cannot manually verify their circuits produce correct R1CS output, which matters when a proving key is being generated for production use.

children.title: Field Arithmetic in Circuit Constraints
children.description: All arithmetic in Circom happens modulo the BN254 field prime. An addition that wraps around the prime produces a different result than expected in regular integer arithmetic, causing constraint satisfaction to fail silently or, worse, accept a wrong witness. This is one of the top sources of Circom bugs.

children.title: snarkjs CLI Workflow
children.description: snarkjs is the primary JavaScript toolchain for the full Circom-to-proof pipeline. Developers who don't understand each CLI step — ptau, zkey, prove, verify — cannot diagnose why a proof fails or automate the pipeline in a CI environment.

children.title: Hardhat-Circom Plugin Integration
children.description: The hardhat-circom plugin compiles circuits and generates verifier contracts inside a Hardhat project, enabling end-to-end testing in the same environment as the application contracts. Without this integration, developers test circuits and contracts in separate pipelines and miss cross-layer integration bugs.

zk-developer.title: snarkjs - Groth16 Proof Generation
zk-developer.description: Use snarkjs to run a trusted setup, generate Groth16 proofs, and verify them both in JavaScript and on Ethereum. Understand the proving and verifying key structure.
topics: Creating proofs with snarkjs groth16 prove
zk-developer.challenge: Complete a full Groth16 workflow: compile a Circom circuit, run trusted setup, generate a proof, and export the Solidity verifier contract.

children.title: Powers of Tau and Phase 2 Setup
children.description: Groth16 requires two setup phases: a universal Powers of Tau ceremony and a circuit-specific phase 2. Skipping phase 2 or reusing another circuit's zkey is a critical security failure — the verifier will accept invalid proofs for any circuit sharing the toxic waste.

children.title: Proving and Verification Key Structure
children.description: The proving key is large and secret-derived; the verification key is small and public. Distributing the proving key publicly is harmless but distributing a corrupted verification key lets an attacker make any false statement look valid. Understanding which key is which prevents deployment mistakes.

children.title: Automating the Prove-Verify Pipeline
children.description: Production ZK applications call proof generation from a browser or server, not manually from CLI. Setting up a programmatic pipeline with snarkjs's JavaScript API — or WASM witness generator for the browser — is required before any real user can interact with your application.

children.title: Exporting the Solidity Verifier
children.description: snarkjs generates a Groth16Verifier.sol contract that implements pairing-based on-chain verification. Developers who do not understand the public signal encoding will pass signals in the wrong order and get every valid proof rejected on-chain without a clear error message.

children.title: Proof Size and Verification Gas
children.description: A Groth16 proof is 192 bytes and costs roughly 200,000 gas to verify on Ethereum — one of the cheapest among zk-SNARK systems. Knowing this budget determines whether your application can afford multiple proofs per transaction, and whether batching is necessary.

children.title: Public vs Private Inputs in Groth16
children.description: Public inputs are visible to the verifier and committed into the proof; private inputs (witnesses) are hidden. Accidentally marking a secret as public exposes it to every on-chain observer. Incorrectly marking a public value as private makes it impossible to verify correctness without the prover's cooperation.

children.title: MPC Ceremonies and Contribution Verification
children.description: A multi-party computation ceremony for phase 2 ensures the toxic waste is destroyed as long as at least one participant is honest. Developers who skip this for production systems allow any single party — including themselves — to silently break the soundness of every proof ever generated.

children.title: Verifying Ceremony Contributions
children.description: After a ceremony, anyone can run the contribution verifier to confirm no single participant poisoned the output. Skipping this verification step means deploying on trust alone, which defeats the purpose of having a ceremony and exposes the protocol to a single point of compromise.

zk-developer.title: On-Chain Proof Verification with Solidity
zk-developer.description: Deploy a Groth16 verifier contract to Ethereum and build a Solidity contract that gates functionality behind successful proof verification.
zk-developer.challenge: Deploy a verifier contract and build an application contract that mints an ERC-20 token only when a valid ZK proof is submitted, preventing double minting via nullifiers.

children.title: Deploying the Auto-Generated Verifier Contract
children.description: snarkjs exports a Solidity verifier that uses Ethereum's BN254 precompile contracts for pairing checks. Deploying with mismatched compiler settings — particularly optimizer runs — can cause the pairing to fail silently, making every valid proof appear invalid on-chain.

children.title: Integrating the Verifier into Application Contracts
children.description: The verifier contract exposes a verifyProof function, but the application contract must correctly decode the proof calldata and pass public signals in the right order. A single off-by-one in the signal array silently rejects all proofs, breaking the entire ZK-gated flow.

children.title: Nullifier Hashes for Uniqueness Enforcement
children.description: Without nullifiers, the same valid proof can be submitted multiple times — a critical flaw for any ZK application that gates access or rewards. The nullifier hash commits the prover to a one-time-use token without revealing which identity or secret was used.

children.title: Proof Replay and Front-Running
children.description: A valid proof broadcast to the mempool can be front-run by an attacker who submits the same proof first in their own transaction. Binding the proof to a recipient address or nonce as a public input closes this attack vector — neglecting it makes ZK-gated minting and voting exploitable.

children.title: Gas Cost of On-Chain Pairing Checks
children.description: The BN254 pairing precompile costs roughly 45,000 gas per pairing plus 34,000 base cost. Groth16 requires 3 pairings, totaling around 180,000–220,000 gas. If your application needs multiple proofs per transaction, this cost compounds quickly and may require proof batching or a ZK rollup layer.

children.title: Calldata Encoding for Proof Submission
children.description: Groth16 proofs are submitted as three elliptic curve point structs plus a public signals array. Using incorrect ABI encoding or wrong tuple ordering causes the verifier to receive malformed data and revert with a generic error. Testing calldata encoding end-to-end before mainnet is mandatory.

children.title: End-to-End ZK Contract Testing with Hardhat
children.description: Testing a ZK application requires generating a real proof inside the test script, encoding it as calldata, and submitting it to the deployed verifier. Mocking the verifier defeats the purpose — subtle encoding mismatches only surface with a real Groth16 proof.

children.title: Foundry for ZK Verifier Testing
children.description: Foundry's fuzz testing can stress-test the verifier contract with random invalid proofs to confirm they always revert. Without this, a subtle encoding edge case that accepts malformed proofs may only be discovered after deployment when an attacker exploits it.

zk-developer.title: Noir - Rust-Like ZK Language
zk-developer.description: Learn Noir as a developer-friendly language for writing ZK circuits with Rust-like syntax. Use Nargo to compile and test circuits and Barretenberg as the proving backend.
topics: Private and public inputs in Noir
zk-developer.challenge: Rewrite your multiplier circuit in Noir, write 3 Nargo unit tests, and generate a proof using the Barretenberg prover.

children.title: Noir Syntax and Type System
children.description: Noir uses a Rust-inspired syntax with a static type system that catches many constraint errors at compile time rather than at proof generation. Developers who skip Noir's type discipline and treat it like Circom will miss compile-time guarantees that prevent entire classes of soundness bugs.

children.title: Private and Public Inputs in Noir
children.description: Noir enforces the public/private distinction at the function signature level. Accidentally making a private input public or vice versa changes the security model of the entire proof and is easy to miss during code review without understanding how Noir's visibility modifiers propagate through the constraint system.

children.title: Nargo Test Framework
children.description: Nargo provides a native unit testing framework for Noir circuits, letting you assert expected outputs without running a full proof. Skipping circuit-level tests means bugs only surface during expensive proof generation runs or, worse, on-chain during user transactions.

children.title: Barretenberg Proving Backend
children.description: Barretenberg is Aztec's ultra-PLONK proving backend that Nargo uses by default. Developers who do not understand the backend's curve and recursion capabilities cannot reason about proof generation time, proof size trade-offs, or whether their circuit is compatible with Aztec Network's private execution.

children.title: When to Choose Noir over Circom
children.description: Noir offers developer ergonomics similar to Rust with recursion built in; Circom gives lower-level control and a mature ecosystem of audited templates. Choosing Circom for a project that needs recursion or Noir for a project that needs circomlib components wastes significant porting time mid-development.

children.title: Noir Recursion and Proof Composition
children.description: Noir supports recursive proofs natively — verifying a Noir proof inside another Noir circuit. This is the foundation of proof aggregation for rollups. Developers who do not explore this capability early miss the most powerful feature separating Noir from Circom for scalability-focused applications.

children.title: UltraPlonk vs Standard PLONK
children.description: Barretenberg implements UltraPlonk, which extends PLONK with lookup tables and custom gates that reduce circuit size for operations like range checks and hashing. Using standard PLONK for bit-intensive operations can inflate proving time 10x compared to using UltraPlonk's native lookup support.

children.title: Proof Size and Verification Tradeoffs by Backend
children.description: Groth16 proofs are 192 bytes; PLONK proofs are 768 bytes; STARKs can reach several kilobytes. On Ethereum, calldata costs 16 gas per non-zero byte, so a STARK proof can cost 5x more than a SNARK in calldata alone. Ignoring this during architecture design leads to unaffordable gas costs in production.

zk-developer.title: ZK Identity and Private Credentials
zk-developer.description: Build ZK identity systems where users prove membership in a set or possession of credentials without revealing which member they are. Study the Semaphore protocol.
zk-developer.challenge: Build an anonymous voting app using Semaphore where 5 registered members can each vote once without revealing their identity.

children.title: Merkle Tree Membership Proofs
children.description: ZK identity systems use Merkle trees to commit a set of members on-chain and let any member prove inclusion without revealing which leaf they are. A bug in the Merkle proof circuit — such as missing a left/right direction constraint — allows a non-member to claim membership, breaking the entire access control system.

children.title: Poseidon Hash for ZK-Friendly Hashing
children.description: SHA-256 inside a ZK circuit requires thousands of constraints; Poseidon, designed for finite fields, needs fewer than 300 per call. Using a non-ZK-native hash for commitments or nullifiers makes proving 10–50x slower and may make browser-side proving impractical.

children.title: Semaphore Identity and Commitment Scheme
children.description: A Semaphore identity is a secret key from which a trapdoor and nullifier are derived; the commitment is the Poseidon hash of these values and is stored in the on-chain Merkle tree. Misunderstanding the commitment structure means building an identity system where two identities can share a commitment, allowing impersonation.

children.title: Nullifier Hash for Anonymous Single-Use Actions
children.description: The Semaphore nullifier hash binds an action to a specific context — such as a vote topic — preventing the same identity from acting twice in the same context while remaining anonymous across contexts. Reusing nullifiers across contexts or storing them incorrectly allows double-voting without detection.

children.title: Anonymous Credential Systems
children.description: ZK credentials let users prove properties — over-18, KYC-verified, token holder — without revealing which specific credential they hold. A design that does not include a binding to the presenting session allows the credential to be transferred and used by another party, undermining the entire access model.

children.title: ZK Mixer and Private Transfer Patterns
children.description: Mixers like Tornado Cash use ZK proofs to break the link between a deposit and withdrawal transaction. The core security relies on nullifier non-reuse enforced on-chain and Merkle inclusion proofs. Any off-by-one in nullifier storage or Merkle tree updates creates a double-spend vulnerability.

children.title: Group Management and Merkle Tree Updates
children.description: When members join or leave a Semaphore group, the on-chain Merkle root updates. Proofs generated against an old root are invalid. Applications that cache the old root or accept proofs without verifying against the latest root allow excluded members to continue acting as if they were still members.

children.title: Off-Chain Proof Generation for Identity
children.description: Browser-based proof generation for identity proofs must complete in under a few seconds for acceptable UX — Poseidon-based circuits can achieve this, but SHA-256-based ones cannot. Developers who prototype with server-side proving and then ship to the browser discover an unusable 30-second wait that requires a full circuit rewrite.

zk-developer.title: PLONK and Universal Trusted Setup
zk-developer.description: Understand PLONK as an improvement over Groth16 with a universal trusted setup. Generate PLONK proofs using snarkjs and compare proving time and verifier gas with Groth16.
topics: When to choose PLONK vs Groth16
zk-developer.challenge: Generate both a Groth16 and a PLONK proof for the same Circom circuit and compare proof size, generation time, and on-chain verification gas cost.

children.title: Universal Trusted Setup vs Circuit-Specific
children.description: PLONK's SRS (Structured Reference String) is universal — one ceremony supports all circuits up to a maximum size. Groth16 requires a new ceremony per circuit. Understanding this difference determines whether deploying a second circuit requires coordinating another public ceremony with dozens of participants.

children.title: KZG Polynomial Commitments
children.description: PLONK uses KZG commitments to achieve constant-size proofs regardless of circuit complexity. The KZG scheme requires a trusted setup but it is circuit-universal. Without understanding KZG, developers cannot evaluate whether a PLONK-based system's security assumptions are acceptable for their threat model.

children.title: FFLONK for Smaller On-Chain Verifiers
children.description: FFLONK is a PLONK variant that reduces on-chain verification gas by roughly 30% compared to standard PLONK by using a more efficient commitment evaluation. Developers unaware of FFLONK choose standard PLONK and pay unnecessary gas for every proof submitted in a high-frequency application.

children.title: Lookup Tables in UltraPlonk
children.description: UltraPlonk extends PLONK with plookup-style lookup tables, allowing entire operations like range checks and SHA-256 rounds to be proven with a single lookup gate instead of thousands of arithmetic constraints. Missing this optimization makes hash-heavy circuits impractically slow on standard PLONK.

children.title: Proof Size and Verification Cost Comparison
children.description: Groth16 produces a 192-byte proof verified in ~200k gas; PLONK produces a 768-byte proof verified in ~300k gas. The 50% higher gas cost of PLONK is offset by not needing a per-circuit ceremony. The right choice depends entirely on how many distinct circuits your application will deploy over its lifetime.

children.title: When to Choose PLONK over Groth16
children.description: If you expect to update circuits frequently — for bug fixes or feature additions — PLONK's universal setup eliminates the ceremony bottleneck. If you have one stable circuit and need the cheapest possible on-chain verification, Groth16 wins. Making this choice wrong locks the team into expensive ceremony coordination or unnecessary gas costs.

children.title: Circuit Size and Proving Time Relationship
children.description: PLONK proving time scales roughly linearly with constraint count but with a higher constant than Groth16 due to FFT-based polynomial operations. A 100k constraint circuit that takes 3 seconds to prove with Groth16 may take 8 seconds with PLONK. This gap matters significantly for browser-based provers where the user is waiting.

children.title: Hardware Acceleration for ZK Proving
children.description: GPU acceleration for MSM (multi-scalar multiplication) and NTT (number theoretic transform) can reduce Groth16 and PLONK proving times by 10–100x. Developers building server-side provers who don't exploit GPU acceleration pay dramatically higher infrastructure costs and cannot compete with ZK protocols that do.

zk-developer.title: ZK Rollup Concepts and zkEVM
zk-developer.description: Study how ZK rollups use validity proofs to scale Ethereum. Deploy contracts to a zkEVM rollup and understand how EVM execution is proven with ZK-SNARKs.
topics: Validity proof vs fraud proof rollups
zkEVM types - bytecode compatible, language compatible

resources.label: An Incomplete Guide to Rollups - Vitalik Buterin

zk-developer.challenge: Deploy your ZK voting contract to Polygon zkEVM testnet, verify it works identically to the L1 version, and measure the cost difference.

children.title: Validity Proofs vs Fraud Proofs
children.description: ZK rollups post a validity proof that cryptographically guarantees correct execution; optimistic rollups post no proof and rely on a 7-day challenge window. Confusing the two means misunderstanding withdrawal finality — ZK rollup withdrawals finalize in minutes, not days, which is critical for liquidity-sensitive applications.

resources.label: An Incomplete Guide to Rollups - Vitalik

children.title: Sequencer and Prover Roles in ZK Rollups
children.description: The sequencer orders transactions and the prover generates the validity proof; in decentralized systems these are separate roles. Developers who think of them as one entity cannot reason about censorship risk, liveness failures, or what happens when the prover falls behind the sequencer during high-volume periods.

children.title: zkEVM Type 1 through Type 4 Trade-offs
children.description: Vitalik's zkEVM classification ranges from Type 1 (fully Ethereum-equivalent, slowest to prove) to Type 4 (compiles high-level language, fastest). Choosing a Type 4 zkEVM for a project that depends on EVM precompiles will cause silent deployment failures that only surface when a user calls a contract that uses an unsupported opcode.

children.title: State Diff vs Full Calldata Publishing
children.description: Some ZK rollups publish only the state diff on L1 rather than full calldata, reducing costs but preventing independent reconstruction of L2 state from L1 data alone. Applications that assume full data availability may fail to reconstruct their state after an L2 outage if the rollup uses state diff publishing.

children.title: Deploying Contracts to zkSync Era
children.description: zkSync Era uses a custom compiler (zksolc) that differs from standard solc — some Solidity patterns like assembly with CREATE2 behave differently. Deploying unmodified Hardhat projects to zkSync without the zksolc compiler plugin produces contracts that compile but fail at runtime in hard-to-diagnose ways.

children.title: Deploying on StarkNet with Cairo
children.description: StarkNet uses Cairo contracts rather than Solidity — a completely different language and execution model. Developers who approach StarkNet expecting EVM compatibility will find that Solidity contracts cannot be deployed without a full rewrite in Cairo, which requires learning an entirely different constraint-based programming model.

children.title: Gas Cost Comparison L1 vs L2
children.description: ZK rollup transactions cost 10–100x less than L1 Ethereum depending on calldata compression and proof amortization. Developers who do not benchmark their specific contract on the target L2 make inaccurate cost projections and may design fee structures that are either too high or economically unsustainable.

children.title: Bridging Assets Between L1 and ZK Rollups
children.description: ZK rollup bridges use the on-chain validity proof to allow instant L2-to-L1 withdrawals without a challenge period. Developers who use an optimistic bridge for a ZK rollup asset introduce an unnecessary 7-day delay that alienates users expecting fast finality.

zk-developer.title: Circuit Security and Soundness Testing
zk-developer.description: Audit ZK circuits for under-constrained signals, unused inputs, and soundness bugs that allow invalid proofs to pass. Use static analysis tools specific to ZK circuits.

resources.label: ZK Circuit Auditing - Trail of Bits

zk-developer.challenge: Run circomspect on your Semaphore circuit, fix all warnings, then manually attempt to forge a proof by submitting under-constrained witness values and confirm it fails.

children.title: Under-Constrained Signals
children.description: An under-constrained signal in Circom is one that appears in the circuit but has no constraint fixing its value — the prover can set it to anything and the proof still verifies. This is the most common ZK circuit bug and has been exploited in production systems; circomspect detects it automatically but only if you run it.

children.title: Unchecked Arithmetic Overflow
children.description: Circom does not automatically range-check signals — a signal declared as a bit may hold values larger than 1 unless you add an explicit IsZero or LessThan constraint. Missing these checks allows witnesses that satisfy the algebraic constraints but represent semantically invalid states, breaking application logic silently.

resources.label: Common ZK Vulnerabilities - Trail of Bits

children.title: circomspect Audit Workflow
children.description: circomspect is Trail of Bits' static analyzer for Circom circuits that detects under-constrained signals, unused outputs, and signal assignment without constraints. Running it as part of CI prevents the class of bugs that have drained millions from production ZK applications and would otherwise only be found by a paid circuit auditor.

resources.label: Trail of Bits ZK Audit Blog

children.title: Reading ZK Circuit Audit Reports
children.description: Public ZK circuit audit reports from Trail of Bits, Zellic, and Veridise document recurring bug patterns with severity ratings and PoC exploits. Developers who study these before writing their own circuits avoid re-inventing the same vulnerabilities that auditors have found across every major ZK project.

children.title: Groth16 Trusted Setup Assumptions
children.description: If any single participant in a Groth16 phase 2 ceremony retains their random toxic waste, they can generate valid proofs for any statement in that circuit. Developers who use a solo or unverified setup in production have a single point of failure that is impossible to audit from outside the ceremony.

children.title: Simulating Invalid Proof Attacks
children.description: Testing that invalid proofs are rejected requires crafting malformed proofs — wrong signals, fabricated curve points, or copied proofs from a different circuit. Developers who only test valid proofs have no assurance that their verifier contract correctly rejects every possible forgery attempt.

resources.label: Foundry Fuzz Testing for ZK Verifiers

children.title: Formal Verification of ZK Circuits
children.description: Formal verification tools like Picus and halo2-verifier can mathematically prove that a circuit is uniquely determined — meaning no under-constrained signals exist. For high-value circuits protecting large treasuries, formal verification provides stronger security guarantees than any number of manual audits or fuzz runs.

children.title: Differential Testing Across Proving Backends
children.description: Running the same circuit through multiple proving backends — snarkjs Groth16, snarkjs PLONK, and Barretenberg — and checking that all three accept the same valid witnesses and reject the same invalid ones catches backend-specific implementation bugs that a single-backend test suite completely misses.

zk-developer.title: Mainnet Deployment and ZK Application Launch
zk-developer.description: Deploy a production ZK application to Ethereum mainnet with a public trusted setup ceremony, verified on-chain verifier, and documentation for external proof generation.
topics: Organizing a public Powers of Tau ceremony
Mainnet verifier and application contract deployment
Proof generation SDK for end users

resources.label: Powers of Tau Ceremony Guide - Hermez

zk-developer.challenge: Deploy your ZK voting system to Ethereum mainnet with a verifiable trusted setup, publish a proof generation guide, and document the ceremony participant list.

children.title: Organizing a Public Powers of Tau Ceremony
