children.description: A credible production ZK application requires a public multi-party computation ceremony with independent participants contributing randomness. Ceremonies with fewer than 10 independent participants from different organizations are difficult to defend as trustworthy — a fact that will be scrutinized by security reviewers and users before depositing funds.

resources.label: Hermez Powers of Tau Ceremony Docs

children.title: Verifying Ceremony Contributions
children.description: After collecting all contributions, the organizer must run the contribution verifier and publish the transcript so anyone can independently confirm the ceremony output is untainted. Skipping publication means users must trust the organizer's word — negating the security benefit of the ceremony entirely.

children.title: Verifier Contract Deployment and Verification
children.description: The on-chain verifier contract must be deployed with compiler settings that match exactly what was used during the trusted setup verification — any mismatch causes the pairing to fail. Verifying the source on Etherscan with the identical compiler version gives users and auditors confidence the bytecode matches the published circuit.

children.title: Circuit Versioning and Upgrade Strategy
children.description: Unlike smart contracts, ZK circuits cannot be upgraded without a new trusted setup ceremony. Developers who ship a v1 circuit with known limitations and plan to upgrade it later discover they must run another public ceremony — meaning a version upgrade takes weeks of ceremony coordination rather than a contract upgrade transaction.

children.title: Building a Client-Side Proof Generation SDK
children.description: End users need a JavaScript or mobile SDK that wraps the WASM witness generator and proof generation into a single function call. Shipping raw snarkjs CLI instructions to users creates insurmountable UX friction and exposes private inputs through command-line arguments that appear in shell history.

children.title: Proof Generation Performance in Production
children.description: WASM-based proof generation in the browser for large circuits (1M+ constraints) can take 30–120 seconds. Developers who don't profile proof generation time before launch discover at the last moment that the UX is unusable and must either reduce circuit complexity, add a server-side prover, or split the proving across worker threads.

resources.label: ZK Proving in the Browser - PSE

children.title: Verifier Gas Optimization Techniques
children.description: Packing public signals efficiently, using FFLONK instead of PLONK, and batching multiple proofs into a single transaction are the three primary levers for reducing on-chain verification cost. A production application that ignores gas optimization may spend 3–5x more per user interaction than necessary.

children.title: Post-Deployment ZK Application Monitoring
children.description: Production ZK applications can fail silently — a prover bug might cause valid proofs to be rejected, blocking all user transactions without any obvious on-chain error. Setting up Tenderly alerts on verifyProof reverts and tracking proof generation success rates in the SDK is the minimum monitoring needed to detect these failures before they compound.

web3-indexer.title: Web, Blockchain, and Data Fundamentals
web3-indexer.description: Understand why blockchain data requires specialized indexing and how smart contract events serve as structured data emission. Learn the basics of GraphQL as a query language.
topics: GraphQL vs REST for blockchain data
web3-indexer.challenge: Use the Ethereum JSON-RPC eth_getLogs method to manually fetch all Transfer events from an ERC-20 contract and display sender, receiver, and amount.

children.title: Blockchain Data Limitations
children.description: Raw RPC nodes expose only the latest state and a flat log stream — they cannot filter, sort, or paginate historical data efficiently. Without an indexing layer, every UI query would replay thousands of blocks on the client, making complex dashboards impossible to build.

children.title: Events as Structured Data Emission
children.description: Solidity events are the primary mechanism contracts use to signal state changes off-chain. Indexed topics enable O(1) log filtering by address or value, and misunderstanding the topic encoding means your indexer silently misses or mis-decodes critical data.

children.title: GraphQL vs REST for Blockchain Data
children.description: GraphQL allows clients to request exactly the fields they need across nested entity relationships in a single round-trip — critical when combining tokens, pools, and swaps in one query. REST endpoints over-fetch flat data and multiply requests, driving up latency on subgraph-backed dashboards.

children.title: Centralized vs Decentralized Indexing
children.description: Centralized indexers like Alchemy or your own Postgres pipeline are fast and flexible but introduce a single point of failure and trust. The Graph decentralized network adds crypto-economic guarantees and censorship resistance at the cost of higher query fees and slower iteration cycles.

children.title: Entity Relationships and Derived Fields
children.description: Using @derivedFrom in The Graph schema avoids storing redundant reverse-lookup arrays in every entity save, keeping handler costs low. Getting the relationship direction wrong forces expensive full scans on every query and can make the subgraph unsync-able on large datasets.

children.title: BigInt, Bytes, and Type Safety
children.description: Ethereum amounts and addresses require BigInt and Bytes scalar types that do not map to native JavaScript numbers — overflow or truncation bugs here silently corrupt balance and ID data. Choosing the wrong type at schema design time forces a breaking schema migration that wipes and re-syncs the entire subgraph.

children.title: Indexed Fields for Efficient Filtering
children.description: Only fields marked as indexed in The Graph schema are stored in a way that supports where-clause filtering without full table scans. Forgetting to index a high-cardinality field used in a filter causes timeout errors in production that cannot be fixed without redeploying the subgraph.

children.title: Interface and Union Types
children.description: Interface types let a single query field return multiple entity kinds — useful when a protocol has different event sources that share common fields. Misusing union types instead of interfaces forces clients to write fragmented queries that double the request payload and complicate frontend rendering logic.

web3-indexer.title: GraphQL Schema Design for Blockchain Data
web3-indexer.description: Design well-structured GraphQL schemas for on-chain data with proper entity relationships, indexed fields, and derived fields. Learn schema best practices for The Graph.
topics: BigInt and Bytes types in The Graph
web3-indexer.challenge: Design a GraphQL schema for a DEX that models Token, Pool, Swap, Liquidity, and User entities with all necessary relationships.

children.title: subgraph.yaml Configuration
children.description: The manifest file ties an ABI, a start block, and a set of event handlers together — getting the startBlock wrong makes the subgraph re-index millions of unnecessary blocks, wasting sync time. A mismatched ABI version causes silent handler skips where events are emitted on-chain but never stored.

children.title: graph init and Project Structure
children.description: The Graph CLI scaffolds the correct file layout, generates TypeScript bindings from an ABI, and configures the YAML manifest automatically — skipping this and writing files manually introduces subtle path or encoding mismatches that only surface at deploy time. Understanding generated code helps debug mapping compilation errors quickly.

children.title: Entity Store Operations
children.description: Every handler must explicitly call store.save() — forgetting this means entity updates appear in local memory but are never persisted, producing queries that return stale or missing data. Using store.load() before mutating an existing entity is mandatory to avoid overwriting sibling fields set by a different handler.

children.title: AssemblyScript Type Constraints
children.description: AssemblyScript lacks a full JavaScript runtime — there are no closures over nullable types, no dynamic property access, and all nullable values require explicit null checks. Ignoring these constraints compiles successfully but produces runtime panics that crash the entire indexing node mid-sync.

children.title: Data Source Templates for Factories
children.description: Factory contracts like Uniswap V2 deploy new pair contracts at runtime, so the subgraph manifest cannot list their addresses statically. Data source templates allow the indexer to start tracking a newly created contract address the moment the factory emits its PairCreated event, enabling complete protocol coverage.

children.title: Block Handlers and Call Handlers
children.description: Block handlers run on every block and are extremely expensive — misusing them for aggregations that could be done in event handlers causes indexing to fall behind the chain head. Call handlers capture non-event contract interactions but require a non-pruned archive node and dramatically increase data costs.

children.title: Deploying to Subgraph Studio
children.description: Subgraph Studio provides a hosted environment for testing before publishing to the decentralized network, with a built-in GraphQL playground and sync progress dashboard. Skipping Studio and going straight to the network means every debug iteration burns GRT signal and requires curator coordination.

children.title: Graph Explorer Query Interface
children.description: The Graph Explorer provides a GraphQL playground that displays the full auto-generated schema and lets you build queries interactively before writing any frontend code. Not using it during development means field name typos and relation mismatches only surface as runtime errors in the application.

web3-indexer.title: Building Your First Subgraph
web3-indexer.description: Create a subgraph from scratch using the Graph CLI, write AssemblyScript event handlers, and deploy to Subgraph Studio for testing. Index a simple ERC-20 or NFT contract.
topics: Entity store operations - save, load, remove

resources.label: Build a Subgraph - The Graph Academy

web3-indexer.challenge: Build and deploy a subgraph for an ERC-20 token that indexes all Transfer events and exposes a query for top 10 holders by balance.

children.title: TypeScript-First Indexing with Ponder
children.description: Ponder runs event handlers in plain TypeScript with full Node.js access, hot module reload, and type-safe ABIs generated via viem — eliminating the AssemblyScript compilation step that makes Graph subgraphs slow to iterate. Misunderstanding that Ponder is self-hosted rather than decentralized means operators must manage their own uptime and database.

children.title: ponder.schema.ts Entity Definition
children.description: Ponder schemas are TypeScript files that define tables and column types which are automatically migrated to SQLite or Postgres — schema changes during development do not require manual migrations. Getting column types wrong at the schema level produces silent cast errors when writing large BigInt values from high-decimal ERC-20 tokens.

children.title: Type-Safe Handlers with viem
children.description: Ponder uses viem's ABI type inference to provide fully typed event arguments in every handler, eliminating the manual decoding that causes off-by-one errors in tuple-typed events. Ignoring TypeScript errors in handlers means the build still succeeds locally but crashes at runtime when an unexpected null address hits a database constraint.

children.title: Built-In GraphQL API and Hot Reload
children.description: Ponder automatically exposes a GraphQL API based on the schema that hot-reloads when handlers change, enabling instant feedback during development without re-deploying or re-syncing. Not using the local GraphQL playground to verify queries before integrating the frontend leads to pagination and filter mismatches that are painful to debug in production.

children.title: Pagination Patterns for Large Datasets
children.description: The Graph enforces a default page size of 100 and a maximum of 1000 items per query — applications that do not paginate will silently truncate results, showing incomplete data to users. Cursor-based pagination using the id field is more reliable than offset-skip pagination which becomes inconsistent as new entities are added during query execution.

children.title: Query Complexity and Depth Limits
children.description: The Graph enforces query complexity scoring — deeply nested or heavily filtered queries that exceed the limit are rejected entirely, not partially executed. Understanding the scoring formula lets you split expensive queries into batched smaller requests rather than discovering the limit only when a production feature goes down.

children.title: Apollo Client Caching for Subgraph Data
children.description: Apollo normalizes subgraph responses into an in-memory cache keyed by entity ID, allowing multiple components to share live data without redundant network requests. Incorrectly configuring cache policies for time-series data causes stale swap counts or TVL figures to persist long after the user expects fresh values.

children.title: Combining Subgraph Queries with Contract Reads
children.description: Subgraphs index finalized state up to the last synced block, while direct RPC calls return the latest uncommitted state — combining both requires reconciling the block lag to avoid showing inconsistent figures. Not handling this lag means a user's just-submitted transaction appears missing from the UI for seconds or minutes, eroding trust.

web3-indexer.title: Advanced Subgraph Patterns
web3-indexer.description: Go beyond event handlers with call handlers, block handlers, and data source templates for factory contracts. Handle complex protocols with dynamic contract tracking.
topics: Call handlers for non-event contract calls
Data source templates for factory contracts
web3-indexer.challenge: Build a subgraph for a Uniswap V2 style factory that dynamically tracks each newly created pair using data source templates.

children.title: GraphQL Subscriptions over WebSockets
children.description: GraphQL subscriptions push new event data to clients the moment the subgraph processes a matching block, eliminating polling intervals and the associated stale-data windows. Forgetting to unsubscribe on component unmount creates WebSocket memory leaks that degrade performance as users navigate a multi-page dApp.

children.title: WebSocket Providers for Raw Event Streaming
children.description: Providers like Alchemy or Infura expose WebSocket JSON-RPC endpoints that stream new block headers and log events without polling, enabling sub-second UI updates. Using HTTP polling as a fallback is acceptable but requires careful backoff logic to avoid rate-limiting when a provider temporarily rejects requests under load.

children.title: Event-Driven UI Updates
children.description: Combining an initial GraphQL query with a subscription that appends new events avoids re-fetching the full dataset on every block while still showing live activity. Building real-time UIs without this pattern results in either expensive full re-fetches every few seconds or a static feed that misses events between polling intervals.

children.title: Polling as a Fallback Strategy
children.description: Not all subgraph deployments support subscriptions, so building a configurable polling interval as a fallback ensures the UI degrades gracefully rather than going completely stale. Setting poll intervals without awareness of The Graph's block indexing latency means polling fires faster than new data arrives, wasting RPC quota.

children.title: Deploying Subgraphs Across Networks
children.description: Each EVM chain requires a separate subgraph deployment because the manifest binds to a specific network's RPC endpoint and contract addresses — a single subgraph cannot span chains. Assuming contract addresses are identical across networks causes the subgraph to index the wrong contract or fail to sync entirely on chains where the protocol deployed at a different address.

children.title: Cross-Chain Data Aggregation
children.description: Combining data from chain-specific subgraphs requires a gateway or client-side merge that handles different block timestamps and currency denominations per chain. Summing TVL or volume across chains without normalizing to a common price oracle and decimal base produces wildly incorrect aggregate figures.

children.title: Reorg Safety in Subgraph Handlers
children.description: The Graph automatically rolls back entity state when a chain reorganization removes a previously indexed block, but custom off-chain pipelines must implement their own rollback logic or risk serving confirmed-but-later-orphaned data. Not accounting for reorgs in a high-frequency trading feed can show phantom swaps to users that never actually settled.

children.title: Network-Specific RPC Configuration
children.description: Different chains have vastly different block times, log limits per eth_getLogs call, and archive node availability — using Ethereum mainnet RPC assumptions on a high-throughput L2 like Arbitrum causes log request timeouts and gaps in indexed data. Always configure per-network block range limits and RPC endpoint rate limits separately.

web3-indexer.title: Ponder - TypeScript-Native Indexing
web3-indexer.description: Use Ponder as a modern TypeScript-first alternative to The Graph for local and self-hosted indexing. Write type-safe event handlers with hot reload and a built-in GraphQL API.
web3-indexer.challenge: Index the same ERC-20 contract with Ponder, compare the developer experience and query latency vs your The Graph subgraph.

children.title: Mocking Events in Matchstick
children.description: Matchstick lets you construct synthetic Ethereum event objects and feed them directly to handlers without running a full node, making handler logic testable in milliseconds. Skipping mocks and only testing against live testnet data means handler bugs only surface after slow re-syncs, dramatically increasing debug cycle time.

children.title: Asserting Entity Values in Tests
children.description: Matchstick provides assert.fieldEquals to verify specific entity fields after a handler runs, catching regressions in balance math and address normalization before they reach production. Not asserting field values — only checking that no exception was thrown — gives false confidence because a handler can silently save a zero or null value.

children.title: Reducing Entity Saves in Hot Handlers
children.description: Every store.save() call in a subgraph handler is an expensive database write — batching state updates and saving once at the end of a handler can reduce sync time by 50% or more on high-volume protocols. Calling save() inside loops that iterate over array fields is the single most common subgraph performance mistake.

children.title: Derived Fields vs Stored Fields
children.description: Storing a pre-computed aggregate like totalVolumeUSD directly on an entity trades storage space for faster queries, while derived fields keep handlers lean at the cost of query-time computation. Choosing stored aggregates for fields that update on every event creates excessive write amplification that causes the subgraph to lag behind the chain head.

children.title: GRT Curation Signal Mechanics
children.description: Curators stake GRT on a subgraph to signal its value to the network — indexers prioritize syncing subgraphs with more signal, so a freshly published subgraph with no signal may not be served for hours or days. Not adding enough signal on launch means the first users of your production dApp receive timeout errors while waiting for an indexer to pick up the subgraph.

children.title: Query Fees and Billing
children.description: Queries to the decentralized network are paid in GRT through a micropayment channel — unexpected query volume spikes can exhaust the billing balance and start returning 402 errors that break the entire frontend. Setting up billing alerts and understanding the fee-per-query calculation before going to production prevents embarrassing production outages.

children.title: Subgraph Versioning and Migration
children.description: Publishing a new subgraph version to the network triggers a full re-sync from startBlock — any schema change that breaks the existing API silently returns empty results for frontend queries still pointing at the old deployment ID. Versioning subgraphs with immutable deployment IDs and migrating query clients independently is the safe upgrade path.

children.title: Monitoring Sync Status and Indexing Errors
children.description: The Graph exposes an indexing status API and a Subgraph Studio health dashboard that show the latest synced block and any handler-level error counts — ignoring these during production operation means silent data gaps accumulate unnoticed. Setting up automated alerts on indexing_error > 0 prevents customer-reported data discrepancies that are hard to trace retroactively.

web3-indexer.title: Querying and Optimizing GraphQL APIs
web3-indexer.description: Write efficient GraphQL queries for large on-chain datasets with pagination, filtering, and ordering. Understand query complexity limits and caching strategies.
topics: Pagination with first, skip, and cursor
web3-indexer.challenge: Write 5 optimized GraphQL queries for your DEX subgraph: top pools by volume, recent swaps, user history, 24h price change, and TVL over time.

children.title: Query Structure and Fields
children.description: Understanding how to compose GraphQL queries is the foundation of working with subgraph APIs. Well-structured queries reduce over-fetching and improve response times significantly. Mastering field selection and aliases helps you build efficient data pipelines from indexed blockchain data.

children.title: Arguments and Variables
children.description: GraphQL variables allow you to write reusable, parameterized queries that can be dynamically filled at runtime. This is critical for filtering on-chain events like token transfers or contract interactions. Using variables also protects against injection issues in production applications.

children.title: Filtering and Ordering
children.description: The Graph's subgraph APIs expose powerful where clauses and orderBy fields that let you slice blockchain data precisely. Filtering by block number, timestamp, or entity fields is essential for paginating historical chain data. Incorrect filters can return massive datasets and degrade app performance.

children.title: Pagination with Skip and First
children.description: Blockchain datasets can contain millions of events, so pagination is non-negotiable for production applications. The Graph supports cursor-based pagination using skip and first parameters to page through large result sets. Understanding the limits of deep pagination helps you design efficient data loaders.

children.title: Query Complexity and Cost
children.description: GraphQL queries that span deeply nested entities or request thousands of records can exhaust rate limits and slow your application. Understanding query complexity scoring helps you write leaner queries that stay within service quotas. Optimized queries also reduce latency for end users interacting with dApps.

children.title: Batching and Caching
children.description: Batching multiple entity lookups into a single query reduces network round-trips and improves throughput for dashboards and analytics tools. Client-side caching with tools like Apollo Client prevents redundant requests for frequently accessed on-chain state. Combining batching with caching is standard practice in production Web3 frontends.

children.title: GraphiQL and Playground
children.description: GraphiQL is an in-browser IDE that lets you explore subgraph schemas and test queries interactively before writing application code. It provides schema introspection, autocomplete, and live query execution against any GraphQL endpoint. Using the playground early speeds up development and helps you discover available entity fields.

children.title: Alchemy Subgraph Queries
children.description: Alchemy provides hosted GraphQL endpoints for popular subgraphs with enterprise-grade reliability and rate limits. Using Alchemy as a query gateway gives you monitoring dashboards and higher throughput than free public endpoints. This is especially valuable during traffic spikes in popular dApp launches.

web3-indexer.title: Real-Time Data - Subscriptions and WebSockets
web3-indexer.description: Add real-time on-chain data to your frontend using GraphQL subscriptions and WebSocket providers. Subscribe to new events and update the UI instantly.
topics: Polling as a fallback for subscription-limited APIs
web3-indexer.challenge: Add a GraphQL subscription to your DEX dashboard so that new swaps appear live in a feed without page refresh.

children.title: Subscription Protocol
children.description: GraphQL subscriptions enable servers to push data to clients when events occur, making them ideal for live blockchain feeds. The subscription-transport-ws and graphql-ws protocols define how clients and servers negotiate persistent connections. Choosing the right protocol impacts compatibility with The Graph's hosted and decentralized endpoints.

children.title: Streaming New Blocks
children.description: Subscribing to new block events allows your application to react to on-chain state changes in near real-time without polling. This reduces unnecessary API calls and gives users a live experience when monitoring token prices or contract events. Block-level subscriptions are the backbone of live trading dashboards and alerting systems.

children.title: WebSocket Lifecycle
children.description: WebSocket connections to blockchain nodes stay open across multiple messages, unlike HTTP which closes after each response. Managing connection lifecycle such as open, ping, reconnect, and close is critical to building resilient real-time applications. Unhandled disconnects can cause silent data gaps in event listeners.

children.title: Reconnection Strategies
children.description: Network interruptions are common in production environments, so implementing exponential backoff reconnection is a must. Without automatic reconnection, your application loses real-time event coverage silently until a user reloads. Libraries like ethers.js provide WebSocketProvider helpers that handle reconnection transparently.

children.title: Listening to Contract Events
children.description: Smart contracts emit events that are the primary source of real-time state changes on EVM chains. Subscribing to logs with eth_subscribe lets you receive new Transfer, Swap, or custom events the moment they are included in a block. Event listening is the standard pattern for updating UI state without re-querying the entire subgraph.

children.title: Handling Reorganizations
children.description: Blockchain reorganizations can invalidate blocks and events your application already processed, causing stale or incorrect state. Implementing reorg detection by comparing parent hashes prevents displaying rolled-back transactions to users. Robust real-time apps always assume events are provisional until several confirmations are received.

children.title: Message Queues for Event Buffering
children.description: High-throughput DeFi protocols can emit thousands of events per minute, overwhelming a single consumer process. Using a message queue like Redis Streams decouples event ingestion from processing, preventing data loss during spikes. This architecture is standard in production indexers that power analytics platforms.

children.title: The Graph Firehose
children.description: The Graph's Firehose is a low-latency blockchain data stream that delivers block and transaction data in a structured binary format. It enables subgraphs and custom indexers to process chain data faster than traditional polling methods. Firehose is the recommended data source for high-performance subgraphs on supported chains.

web3-indexer.title: Multi-Chain Indexing
web3-indexer.description: Extend your indexing infrastructure to cover multiple EVM-compatible chains with chain-specific subgraphs and a unified query layer. Handle cross-chain data aggregation.
topics: Chain ID filtering in schema and queries
Separate vs unified subgraph for multi-chain
web3-indexer.challenge: Deploy your ERC-20 subgraph to both Ethereum mainnet and Arbitrum One, then write a combined query that aggregates total transfers across both chains.

children.title: Chain Differences and EVM Compatibility
children.description: While Ethereum and EVM-compatible chains share the same smart contract interface, differences in block times, gas models, and precompiles affect how you index them. Understanding chain-specific quirks prevents bugs when porting subgraphs between networks like Polygon, Arbitrum, or Avalanche. Accounting for these differences early saves significant rework in production.

children.title: Chain IDs and Network Configuration
children.description: Each blockchain network has a unique chain ID that must be configured correctly in your indexer to avoid routing transactions to the wrong network. Mismatched chain IDs are a common source of production bugs when deploying multi-chain subgraphs. The Graph's network configuration files map chain names to their correct endpoints and IDs.

children.title: Separate vs Unified Subgraphs
children.description: Deciding whether to deploy one subgraph per chain or a unified cross-chain subgraph impacts maintainability and query complexity. Separate subgraphs are simpler but require clients to query multiple endpoints and merge data. Unified approaches reduce client complexity but increase the complexity of your mapping logic.

children.title: Cross-Chain Entity Normalization
children.description: When indexing the same protocol on multiple chains, entities like tokens or users need a consistent schema that accounts for chain-specific differences. Normalizing entity IDs with chain prefixes prevents collisions between identically addressed contracts on different networks. A well-normalized schema enables clean multi-chain analytics without post-processing.

children.title: Solana Indexing Basics
children.description: Solana's account-based model and program architecture differ fundamentally from Ethereum's contract model, requiring different indexing strategies. Programs emit logs rather than typed events, so parsers must decode instruction data using IDLs from tools like Anchor. Supporting Solana in a multi-chain indexer expands your addressable market significantly.

children.title: NEAR Protocol Indexing
children.description: NEAR uses a sharded architecture where transactions can span multiple shards, complicating event ordering for indexers. The Graph supports NEAR subgraphs through its Firehose integration, abstracting much of the shard complexity. Indexing NEAR opens access to a growing ecosystem of DeFi and NFT protocols.

children.title: Cross-Chain Data Aggregation
children.description: Combining data from multiple chains into a unified view requires either a dedicated aggregation service or a federated GraphQL gateway. Without aggregation, clients must join data manually, increasing frontend complexity and latency. Tools like The Graph's Subgraph Composition allow combining multiple subgraphs into a single queryable API.

children.title: Canonical Token Mapping
children.description: The same asset often has different contract addresses on each chain, requiring a canonical mapping layer to unify balances and liquidity. Maintaining an up-to-date token registry across chains is operationally challenging but essential for correct multi-chain analytics. Token lists from projects like Uniswap provide a starting point for building this mapping.

web3-indexer.title: Subgraph Testing and Performance
web3-indexer.description: Write unit tests for subgraph handlers using Matchstick, profile slow queries, and optimize entity updates that touch hot code paths in high-volume protocols.
topics: Mocking events and contract calls in tests
Asserting entity field values in Matchstick
Reducing entity saves in hot handlers
Derived field vs stored field tradeoffs
web3-indexer.challenge: Write Matchstick tests covering all event handlers in your DEX subgraph with at least 90% handler coverage, and optimize the slowest query to under 200ms.

children.title: Matchstick Testing Framework
children.description: Matchstick is The Graph's official unit testing framework for subgraph mappings, allowing you to simulate blockchain events and assert entity state without deploying to a network. Writing tests with Matchstick catches logic errors in handlers before they index incorrect data into production. Fast unit tests give developers confidence to refactor mapping code safely.

children.title: Mocking Contract Calls
children.description: Subgraph mappings often call smart contract view functions to enrich indexed data, and these calls must be mocked in tests to avoid hitting a live node. Matchstick's createMockedFunction helper lets you specify return values for any contract call in your test scenarios. Proper mocking ensures your tests are deterministic and fast regardless of network state.

children.title: Local Graph Node Testing
children.description: Running a local Graph Node with Docker allows you to deploy and query your subgraph against real or forked chain data before production deployment. Integration tests against a local node catch issues like missing event handlers or incorrect entity relationships that unit tests cannot surface. A local testing environment mirrors production conditions closely and reduces deployment surprises.

children.title: Forked Chain Testing
children.description: Testing against a forked mainnet snapshot lets you validate your subgraph against real historical data without spending on live transactions. Tools like Hardhat Network's forking mode combined with a local Graph Node create a realistic test environment for complex DeFi subgraphs. Fork testing is essential before indexing protocols with intricate token mechanics.

children.title: Indexing Speed Benchmarks
children.description: Slow mappings increase sync time dramatically, especially when catching up from genesis on a high-throughput chain. Profiling handler execution time helps identify expensive operations like unnecessary contract calls or inefficient entity lookups. Optimizing hot paths in your mappings can reduce sync time from days to hours.

children.title: Reducing Store Operations
children.description: Each entity save and load in a subgraph mapping incurs overhead in the Graph Node's store, and excessive operations are the most common source of slow syncing. Batching entity updates and avoiding redundant loads with in-memory caching significantly improves throughput. Monitoring store operation counts in Graph Node logs is the first step in performance tuning.

children.title: Subgraph Health Endpoints
children.description: The Graph Node exposes a health endpoint that reports indexing status, current block, and any fatal errors for each deployed subgraph. Integrating health checks into your monitoring stack lets you detect sync failures before users report broken data. Automated alerts on sync lag prevent prolonged outages in data-sensitive applications.

children.title: Error Handling in Handlers
children.description: Unhandled errors in event handlers cause subgraphs to enter a failed state and stop indexing, resulting in stale data for all downstream consumers. Using try-catch patterns and logging meaningful error messages helps diagnose and recover from unexpected on-chain data formats. Graceful error handling is the difference between a resilient and a brittle indexer.

web3-indexer.title: Production Deployment on The Graph Network
web3-indexer.description: Publish your subgraph to The Graph decentralized network, add GRT signal to attract indexers, and set up monitoring and alerts for sync status and query errors.
topics: Publishing to The Graph Network vs hosted service
Monitoring sync status and indexing errors

resources.label: Deploying to The Graph Network - YouTube

web3-indexer.challenge: Publish your DEX subgraph to The Graph Network, add curation signal, confirm at least one indexer is serving it, and set up a dashboard monitoring query success rate.

children.title: Decentralized vs Hosted Service
children.description: The Graph's decentralized network distributes indexing across independent node operators, eliminating the single point of failure present in hosted services. Migrating to the network gives your subgraph censorship resistance and geographic redundancy that centralized hosting cannot provide. Understanding the economic model of indexers and curators is essential before committing to decentralized deployment.

children.title: GRT Token and Curation
children.description: GRT is the native token of The Graph Network used to signal on subgraphs, incentivize indexers, and pay for query fees. Curating a subgraph by signaling GRT attracts indexers to pick it up and service queries on the decentralized network. Understanding curation dynamics helps you ensure your subgraph has sufficient indexer coverage from day one.

children.title: Publishing to The Graph Network
children.description: Publishing a subgraph to the decentralized network requires deploying it on-chain via the Subgraph Studio and paying a small GRT fee. The publish transaction registers your subgraph manifest on-chain, making it permanently addressable by its deployment ID. A well-tested subgraph should pass through Subgraph Studio's hosted environment before being published to the network.

children.title: Versioning and Upgrades
children.description: Subgraph upgrades deploy a new version with a fresh deployment ID while the previous version continues serving queries during re-indexing. Planning your upgrade strategy prevents query downtime when you need to add new entities or fix handler bugs. The Graph Studio's version management UI lets you monitor the new version's sync progress before routing traffic to it.

children.title: Query Fee Mechanism
children.description: On The Graph Network, consumers pay per-query fees in GRT to indexers who serve their requests, creating a sustainable economic model for data availability. Understanding fee negotiation via payment channels helps you estimate costs for high-query-volume applications. Optimizing your query patterns reduces fee spend without sacrificing data freshness.

children.title: Allocations and Indexer Selection
children.description: Indexers allocate stake to specific subgraphs to earn indexing rewards, and more stake allocated means better query availability and response times. As a subgraph developer, signaling more GRT on your subgraph attracts higher-quality indexers willing to maintain fresh sync. Monitoring which indexers are allocated to your subgraph helps you identify reliability issues early.

children.title: SLA Monitoring on the Network
children.description: The decentralized network does not guarantee SLAs the way a managed service would, so building your own monitoring layer is essential for production applications. Tracking query latency and error rates per indexer lets you implement client-side fallback logic when individual nodes degrade. Proactive monitoring ensures your dApp remains responsive even during indexer disruptions.

children.title: Cost Optimization Strategies
children.description: Reducing the number of entities your subgraph indexes and caching query results at the application layer are the two most impactful cost-reduction strategies on the network. Reviewing your subgraph's entity schema periodically to remove unused fields keeps indexing costs and query complexity low. A lean subgraph also re-syncs faster after upgrades, reducing downtime for your users.
